Mathematically, both curves are exponential transient responses governed by a **first-order linear differential equation** that arises from the **principle of conservation of energy (Heat Balance)**. ## 1.2 Real-World Analogy 🧠 > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The Bucket with a Hole** Think of an electric machine as a **water bucket** placed under a **running tap (the electrical losses → heat input)**. - The **water level** in the bucket represents the **temperature rise (θ)** above ambient. - The **flow rate of the tap** represents the **power loss ($P_{loss}$)** in the machine. - The **hole at the bottom** of the bucket represents **heat dissipation** to the surroundings (radiation + convection). - The **size (cross-section) of the bucket** represents the **thermal capacity** (mass × specific heat) of the machine. **Behavioural Mapping:** | Bucket Scenario | Electrical Equivalent | |----------------|----------------------| | Tap is on, bucket fills up gradually | **Heating Curve** (θ rises towards steady-state) | | Tap is on but bucket never overflows (steady level) | **Final steady-state temperature rise** ($\theta_f$) | | Tap is closed, water drains out slowly | **Cooling Curve** (θ falls towards ambient) | | Slow drain (small hole) | **Large cooling time constant** (self-ventilated, fan stops) | | Fast drain (big hole) | **Small cooling time constant** (forced ventilation continues) | In a machine, when the load is switched **ON**, heat is generated by $I^2R$ losses, iron losses, and friction. The temperature does not jump instantly to its final value — it rises **exponentially** because the machine's mass needs to be "thermally charged" (the bucket is filling). ## 1.3 Why Are These Curves So Important in KTU Examinations? > [!IMPORTANT] > These curves are the **DNA of motor rating selection**. Every industrial motor must be rated so that its final temperature does not exceed the **insulation class limit** (Class A: 105°C, Class B: 130°C, Class F: 155°C, Class H: 180°C). The heating curve directly answers: *"If I run this motor for 't' minutes at this load, how hot will it get?"* — the central question of **motor duty cycle design**. --- # 📐 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Heat Balance Equation (First Principles) When an electric machine operates, **energy conservation** dictates: > **Heat Generated in time $dt$ = Heat Stored in machine body + Heat Dissipated to surroundings in time $dt$** $$P_{loss} \cdot dt \;=\; m \cdot c \cdot d\theta \;+\; A \cdot \lambda \cdot \theta \cdot dt$$ Where the variables carry the standard KTU symbols: - $P_{loss}$ → Total power loss (W), assumed **constant** for the heating case - $m$ → Active mass of the machine (kg) - $c$ → Specific heat of the machine material (J/kg·°C) - $A$ → Effective cooling surface area (m²) - $\lambda$ → Heat dissipation coefficient (W/m²·°C) - $\theta$ → Temperature rise above ambient (°C) at time $t$ ## 2.2 Structural Reformulation → Standard First-Order ODE Dividing the heat balance equation by $dt$ and re-arranging: $$m \cdot c \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} \;+\; A \cdot \lambda \cdot \theta \;=\; P_{loss}$$ Dividing throughout by $A \cdot \lambda$: $$\frac{m \cdot c}{A \cdot \lambda} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} \;+\; \theta \;=\; \frac{P_{loss}}{A \cdot \lambda}$$ ## 2.3 Defining the Two Foundational Quantities This reformulation reveals the **two pillars of thermal modelling**: > [!NOTE] > **Pillar 1 — Thermal Time Constant ($\tau$)** > > $$\boxed{\tau \;=\; \frac{m \, c}{A \, \lambda} \quad \text{[seconds]}}$$ > > Represents the **thermal inertia** of the machine. A heavier machine or one with poor heat dissipation has a **larger $\tau$**. > [!NOTE] > **Pillar 2 — Final Steady-State Temperature Rise ($\theta_f$)** > > $$\boxed{\theta_f \;=\; \frac{P_{loss}}{A \, \lambda} \quad \text{[°C]}}$$ > > The temperature the machine **eventually settles at** if it runs forever. It is the steady-state where all generated heat is being dissipated (no more storage). The governing differential equation now takes its **canonical, KTU-recurring form**: $$\boxed{\tau \,\frac{d\theta}{dt} \;+\; \theta \;=\; \theta_f}$$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet 📋 > [!IMPORTANT] > **EXAM GOLD TABLE** — Memorize these expressions verbatim; they appear in nearly every Part B question on thermal rating. | # | Quantity | Mathematical Form | Units | Physical Meaning | |---|----------|-------------------|-------|------------------| | 1 | Heating Time Constant | $\tau_h = \dfrac{m c}{A \lambda}$ | seconds (s) | Time to reach 63.2% of $\theta_f$ | | 2 | Final Temperature Rise | $\theta_f = \dfrac{P_{loss}}{A \lambda}$ | °C or K | Asymptotic temperature rise | | 3 | Heating Equation | $\theta(t) = \theta_f \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_h}\right)$ | °C | Rise from ambient | | 4 | Cooling Equation | $\theta(t) = \theta_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau_c}$ | °C | Decay from initial $\theta_0$ | | 5 | Newton-Rate Coefficient | $\lambda = \dfrac{P_{loss}}{A \cdot \theta_f}$ | W/m²·°C | Heat transfer rate per unit area | | 6 | Total Dissipation at Steady State | $P_{loss} = A \lambda \theta_f$ | W | All loss becomes dissipation | | 7 | Thermal Resistance | $R_{th} = \dfrac{1}{A \lambda}$ = $\dfrac{\theta_f}{P_{loss}}$ | °C/W | Resistance to heat flow (analogy: $R$ in Ohm's law) | | 8 | Heating-Cooling Time Constant Ratio (Self-ventilated) | $\tau_c = (2 \text{ to } 3)\,\tau_h$ | — | $\tau_c \vert_{\text{stopped}} > \tau_h$ because fan is off | | 9 | Heating-Cooling Time Constant Ratio (Force-ventilated) | $\tau_c \approx \tau_h$ | — | Cooling air is forced by external fan | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility 🌍 | Application Domain | How Heating/Cooling Curves Are Used | |--------------------|--------------------------------------| | **Electric Vehicle (EV) Motor Design** | Predicting battery/motor temperature during repeated acceleration (intermittent duty). | | **Crane & Hoist Drives** | Short-time rating selection — motor runs for 5 min, then rests. | | **Rolling Mills & Presses** | Intermittent periodic duty (S4, S5 duty cycles per IS/IEC). | | **Transformer Sizing** | Same $\tau$-based model with oil temperature dynamics. | | **Servo Motor Selection** | Determining continuous vs. peak torque based on thermal envelope. | --- # 🧮 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse the **heating** equation with the **cooling** equation. They look superficially similar but differ in the initial conditions and the sign inside the exponent. Read the case carefully — **"is the machine being loaded or unloaded?"** ## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Heating Equation (Cold Machine → Constant Load) **Problem Statement:** A cold machine (initial temperature rise = 0) is switched ON at $t = 0$ and subjected to a constant loss $P_{loss}$. Derive the temperature rise $\theta(t)$. **Starting Point — The Canonical ODE:** $$\tau \, \frac{d\theta}{dt} + \theta = \theta_f$$ **Step 1:** Rearrange into standard linear form: $$\frac{d\theta}{dt} + \frac{1}{\tau}\,\theta = \frac{\theta_f}{\tau}$$ **Step 2:** Identify the integrating factor (IF): $$\text{IF} = e^{\int \frac{1}{\tau} dt} = e^{t/\tau}$$ **Step 3:** Multiply the ODE by the IF: $$e^{t/\tau} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} + \frac{1}{\tau}\,e^{t/\tau}\,\theta = \frac{\theta_f}{\tau}\,e^{t/\tau}$$ **Step 4:** Recognize the LHS as a perfect derivative: $$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left[\theta \, e^{t/\tau}\right] = \frac{\theta_f}{\tau}\,e^{t/\tau}$$ **Step 5:** Integrate both sides with respect to $t$: $$\theta \, e^{t/\tau} = \int \frac{\theta_f}{\tau}\,e^{t/\tau}\, dt = \theta_f \cdot e^{t/\tau} + C$$ **Step 6:** Isolate $\theta$: $$\theta = \theta_f + C \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$ **Step 7:** Apply initial condition — at $t = 0$, the machine is cold, so $\theta(0) = 0$: $$0 = \theta_f + C \cdot e^{0} \;\Longrightarrow\; C = -\theta_f$$ **Step 8:** Substitute back $C = -\theta_f$: $$\boxed{\theta(t) = \theta_f \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_h}\right) \quad \text{[Heating Equation]}}$$ **Verification of Limits (the engineer's sanity check):** - At $t = 0$: $\theta(0) = \theta_f(1 - 1) = 0$ ✓ (cold machine) - At $t = \tau_h$: $\theta(\tau_h) = \theta_f(1 - e^{-1}) = 0.632\,\theta_f$ ✓ (63.2% rule) - At $t = 4\tau_h$: $\theta(4\tau_h) \approx 0.982\,\theta_f \approx \theta_f$ ✓ (machine "thermally saturated") - At $t \to \infty$: $\theta(\infty) = \theta_f(1 - 0) = \theta_f$ ✓ (steady state) ## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Cooling Equation (Hot Machine → Switched Off) **Problem Statement:** A hot machine at initial temperature rise $\theta_0$ is switched OFF at $t = 0$. The loss $P_{loss} = 0$. Derive $\theta(t)$. **Starting Point:** When the machine is OFF, the heating source vanishes: $P_{loss} = 0 \Rightarrow \theta_f = 0$. The ODE collapses to: $$\tau_c \, \frac{d\theta}{dt} + \theta = 0$$ **Step 1:** Separate variables: $$\frac{d\theta}{\theta} = -\frac{1}{\tau_c}\,dt$$ **Step 2:** Integrate: $$\ln \theta = -\frac{t}{\tau_c} + \ln K$$ **Step 3:** Exponentiate: $$\theta(t) = K \cdot e^{-t/\tau_c}$$ **Step 4:** Apply IC: at $t = 0$, $\theta(0) = \theta_0$: $$\theta_0 = K \cdot e^{0} \;\Longrightarrow\; K = \theta_0$$ **Step 5:** Final cooling equation: $$\boxed{\theta(t) = \theta_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau_c} \quad \text{[Cooling Equation]}}$$ **Verification of Limits:** - At $t = 0$: $\theta(0) = \theta_0$ ✓ - At $t = \tau_c$: $\theta(\tau_c) = \theta_0 \cdot e^{-1} = 0.368\,\theta_0$ ✓ (machine has shed 63.2% of its rise) - At $t \to \infty$: $\theta(\infty) = 0$ ✓ (cools to ambient) ## 3.3 Derivation 3: General Loading Case (Hot Machine → Different Load) **Problem Statement:** A machine is already hot (at $\theta_0$) and its load changes such that the new $P_{loss}$ leads to a new $\theta_f$. Derive $\theta(t)$. **Starting Point — Same Canonical ODE:** $$\tau_h \, \frac{d\theta}{dt} + \theta = \theta_f^{\text{new}}$$ **Step 1:** The general solution is $\theta = \theta_f^{\text{new}} + C e^{-t/\tau_h}$. **Step 2:** Apply IC: at $t = 0$, $\theta(0) = \theta_0$: $$\theta_0 = \theta_f^{\text{new}} + C \;\Longrightarrow\; C = \theta_0 - \theta_f^{\text{new}}$$ **Step 3:** Final general equation: $$\boxed{\theta(t) = \theta_f^{\text{new}} + \left(\theta_0 - \theta_f^{\text{new}}\right)e^{-t/\tau_h}}$$ **Special Case Validation:** - If $\theta_0 = 0$ (cold start) → $\theta(t) = \theta_f^{\text{new}}(1 - e^{-t/\tau_h})$ ✓ matches Heating Eq. - If $\theta_f^{\text{new}} = 0$ (switched off) → $\theta(t) = \theta_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau_c}$ ✓ matches Cooling Eq. (using $\tau_c$). ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard Style) 🎯 > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A 15 kW, 3-phase induction motor has a heating time constant of 60 minutes. When delivering rated output continuously, the steady-state temperature rise is 75°C above ambient. If the motor is started from cold, calculate: > (a) The temperature rise after **30 minutes** of operation. > (b) The time required to reach a temperature rise of **50°C**. > (c) If the motor is now switched OFF from the steady-state of 75°C, with cooling time constant = 90 minutes, find the temperature rise **2 hours** later. **Given Data:** $\tau_h = 60$ min, $\theta_f = 75$°C, $\tau_c = 90$ min. ### Part (a): Temperature after 30 minutes $$\theta(30) = 75 \left(1 - e^{-30/60}\right)$$ $$= 75 \left(1 - e^{-0.5}\right)$$ $$= 75 \left(1 - 0.6065\right)$$ $$= 75 \times 0.3935$$ $$\boxed{\theta(30) \approx 29.51 \;°\text{C}}$$ ### Part (b): Time to reach 50°C Set $\theta(t) = 50$: $$50 = 75 \left(1 - e^{-t/60}\right)$$ $$\frac{50}{75} = 1 - e^{-t/60}$$ $$e^{-t/60} = 1 - 0.6667 = 0.3333$$ $$-\frac{t}{60} = \ln(0.3333) = -1.0986$$ $$\boxed{t = 60 \times 1.0986 \approx 65.92 \text{ minutes}}$$ ### Part (c): Cooling after 2 hours from 75°C $$\theta(120) = 75 \cdot e^{-120/90}$$ $$= 75 \cdot e^{-1.3333}$$ $$= 75 \times 0.2636$$ $$\boxed{\theta(120) \approx 19.77 \;°\text{C}}$$ --- # 📊 Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Heating and Cooling Curve — Visual Plot (Mermaid Graph) The following diagram provides a **block-level functional architecture flow** of the heating/cooling process, augmented with a coordinate-mapped sketch of the two curves. ```mermaid graph LR startA[Cold Machine
theta equals 0] --> switchOn{Load ON} switchOn --> heatProc[Heat Generation
P loss watts] heatProc --> storage[Thermal Storage
m times c times d theta] storage --> tempRise[Temperature Rise
theta increases] tempRise --> dissProc[Heat Dissipation
A times lambda times theta] dissProc --> equilib[Steady State
theta equals theta f] equilib --> switchOff{Load OFF} switchOff --> coolDecay[Cooling Begins
P loss equals 0] coolDecay --> ambRet[Returns to Ambient
theta approaches 0] style startA fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000 style switchOn fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000 style heatProc fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,color:#000 style storage fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 style tempRise fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 style dissProc fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000 style equilib fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#4527A0,color:#000 style switchOff fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000 style coolDecay fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#00695C,color:#000 style ambRet fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Annotated Temperature vs. Time Plot (Coordinate-Mapped Schematic) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph HEATING["HEATING PHASE — Cold Start"] H0[Point A: t=0, theta=0] -->|Exponential Rise| H1[Point B: t=tau h, theta equals 0.632 theta f] H1 --> H2[Point C: t=4 tau h, theta approximately theta f] end subgraph COOLING["COOLING PHASE — Switched OFF"] C0[Point C prime: t=0, theta equals theta f] -->|Exponential Decay| C1[Point D: t=tau c, theta equals 0.368 theta f] C1 --> C2[Point E: t=4 tau c, theta approximately 0] end style H0 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000 style H1 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 style H2 fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000 style C0 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 style C1 fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#004D40,color:#000 style C2 fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram — Self vs. Force Ventilated Cooling ```mermaid graph TB subgraph SELF_VENT["SELF-VENTILATED MOTOR — tau c is greater than tau h"] SV1[During Run] -->|Fan ON, Full Cooling| SV2[Tau h = Base Value] SV3[After Stop] -->|Fan OFF, Reduced Cooling| SV4[Tau c = 2 to 3 times tau h] end subgraph FORCE_VENT["FORCE-VENTILATED MOTOR — tau c equals tau h"] FV1[During Run] -->|External Fan ON| FV2[Tau h = Base Value] FV3[After Stop] -->|External Fan Continues| FV4[Tau c approximately equal to tau h] end style SV2 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 style SV4 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000 style FV2 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 style FV4 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Guide for KTU Students:** > - The **heating curve** always starts at the **origin (0, 0)** and rises asymptotically toward $\theta_f$. > - The **cooling curve** always starts at some $\theta_0$ and falls asymptotically toward **zero**. > - Both curves pass through the **63.2% / 36.8% mark** at exactly one time constant. > - In the plot, the heating curve is **concave-down** (rate of rise decreases over time), while the cooling curve is **concave-up** (rate of fall decreases over time). --- # 🎯 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define heating time constant of an electric machine. State its significance in motor rating.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **heating time constant ($\tau_h$)** of an electric machine is defined as the time required for the temperature rise of the machine, when starting from cold, to reach **63.2% of its final steady-state temperature rise ($\theta_f$)** if the heating continues at its initial rate. Mathematically: $$\tau_h = \frac{m \, c}{A \, \lambda} \quad \text{seconds}$$ **Significance:** - It indicates **how quickly** the machine reaches thermal equilibrium. - A **small $\tau_h$** means the machine heats up (and cools down) rapidly — suitable for **intermittent duty** applications. - A **large $\tau_h$** indicates high thermal inertia — useful for **short-time overload** absorption. - It is a critical parameter in selecting motors for various **duty cycles (S1 to S10)** as per IEC 60034-1. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Why is the cooling time constant of a self-ventilated motor greater than its heating time constant?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** In a **self-ventilated motor**, the cooling is provided by a **fan mounted on the motor shaft itself**. - **During running:** The shaft rotates → the fan circulates air → effective heat dissipation is high → small effective $\tau_h$. - **After switching off:** The shaft stops → the fan stops → natural convection is the only cooling mechanism → heat dissipation drops sharply → $\tau_c$ becomes **2 to 3 times larger** than $\tau_h$. This is the fundamental reason: $$\tau_c = \frac{m \, c}{A \, \lambda_{\text{natural}}} \quad \text{where} \quad \lambda_{\text{natural}} \ll \lambda_{\text{forced}}$$ In contrast, a **separately force-ventilated motor** has an external blower running independently, so $\tau_c \approx \tau_h$. --- ## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Heating Equations & Duty Cycle Analysis* > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 14 Marks]** **A 25 HP, 400 V, 3-phase induction motor has the following thermal data:** - Heating time constant: $\tau_h = 90$ minutes - Final steady-state temperature rise (at rated load): $\theta_f = 80$°C - Cooling time constant (when stopped): $\tau_c = 180$ minutes - Insulation class: **Class B** (permissible hot-spot temperature: 130°C) - Ambient temperature: $40$°C #### **(a) [7 marks]** If the motor is started from cold and run continuously at rated load, plot the heating curve equation and find: 1. The temperature rise after 1 hour. 2. The time to reach $90\%$ of final temperature rise. 3. The **maximum safe continuous load** if the permissible temperature rise is $90$°C (i.e., the motor can be re-rated). #### **(b) [7 marks]** The motor is operated on a **duty cycle**: runs at rated load for 60 minutes, then switched OFF for 30 minutes. Assuming the cycle starts from cold, find the **maximum temperature rise reached during the cycle** and verify whether it remains within the Class B insulation limit. **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) Solution: **Step 1 — Heating Equation:** $$\theta(t) = \theta_f \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_h}\right) = 80 \left(1 - e^{-t/90}\right)$$ **Sub-part 1:** At $t = 60$ min: $$\theta(60) = 80 \left(1 - e^{-60/90}\right) = 80 \left(1 - e^{-0.6667}\right)$$ $$= 80 \left(1 - 0.5134\right) = 80 \times 0.4866$$ $$\boxed{\theta(60) \approx 38.93 \;°\text{C}}$$ **Sub-part 2:** Time to reach 90% of $\theta_f$: Set $\theta(t) = 0.9 \times 80 = 72$°C: $$72 = 80 \left(1 - e^{-t/90}\right) \;\Longrightarrow\; e^{-t/90} = 0.1$$ $$t = 90 \times \ln(10) = 90 \times 2.3026$$ $$\boxed{t \approx 207.23 \text{ minutes} \approx 3 \text{ hours } 27 \text{ min}}$$ **Sub-part 3 — Re-rating for $\theta_{max} = 90$°C:** For the same machine, the new loss $P_{loss}'$ produces $\theta_f' = 90$°C. Since $\theta_f \propto P_{loss}$: $$\frac{P_{loss}'}{P_{loss, \text{rated}}} = \frac{\theta_f'}{\theta_f} = \frac{90}{80} = 1.125$$ So the new loss is **12.5% higher** than rated. For a motor with copper loss dominating: $$P_{loss} \propto I^2 \;\Longrightarrow\; \frac{I'}{I_{\text{rated}}} = \sqrt{1.125} \approx 1.0607$$ Thus the new safe current is **~6.07% above rated** — only a **marginal over-rating** is possible. The new safe output is approximately: $$P_{out}' \approx 25 \times 1.06 \approx 26.5 \text{ HP}$$ (assuming efficiency remains comparable). #### Part (b) Solution: **Step 1 — Heating for 60 min from cold:** $$\theta(60) = 80 \left(1 - e^{-60/90}\right) = 38.93 \;°\text{C} \quad \text{(from above)}$$ **Step 2 — Cooling for 30 min from $\theta_0 = 38.93$°C:** Using the cooling equation with $\tau_c = 180$ min: $$\theta(30) = 38.93 \cdot e^{-30/180} = 38.93 \cdot e^{-0.1667}$$ $$= 38.93 \times 0.8465 = 32.95 \;°\text{C}$$ **Step 3 — Second heating cycle (start from $\theta = 32.95$°C, target $\theta_f = 80$°C):** Use the **general loading equation**: $$\theta(t) = \theta_f + (\theta_0 - \theta_f)\,e^{-t/\tau_h}$$ At $t = 60$ min: $$\theta(60) = 80 + (32.95 - 80) e^{-60/90}$$ $$= 80 + (-47.05)(0.5134)$$ $$= 80 - 24.15$$ $$\boxed{\theta_{\max} \approx 55.85 \;°\text{C}}$$ **Step 4 — Verify against insulation limit:** Maximum permissible temperature = $40 + 90 = 130$°C → wait, the temperature *rise* limit is **90°C** (hot-spot allowance above ambient). $$\theta_{\max} = 55.85 \;°\text{C} < 90 \;°\text{C} \quad \checkmark$$ The motor operates safely within Class B insulation limits. Hence, the cycle is **thermally safe**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > 1. Many students forget to use the **general loading equation** for the second cycle and incorrectly apply the cold-start heating equation. **Marks lost: 2-3.** > 2. Some students do not check insulation limit; merely computing temperature is **incomplete** — always **comment on safety**. > 3. The units must be consistent — mixing minutes and seconds in $\tau$ is a common **unitary pitfall**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice — Cooling Curve Analysis* > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 14 Marks]** **A 10 kW DC shunt motor is operated until it reaches a steady-state temperature rise of $60$°C above ambient. The heating and cooling time constants are $\tau_h = 45$ minutes and $\tau_c = 120$ minutes respectively.** #### **(a) [7 marks]** 1. Derive the heating and cooling equations from first principles. 2. The motor runs for 30 minutes from cold. What is the temperature rise? 3. If the motor runs continuously for 4 hours, what is the percentage utilization of the steady-state value? #### **(b) [7 marks]** After reaching the steady state, the motor is switched OFF for 1 hour and then re-started on the **same load**. 1. Calculate the temperature rise when the motor is restarted. 2. How long will it take (from restart) to reach 95% of the steady-state value? 3. Comment on the thermal stress implications. **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) Solution: **Step 1 — Heating Derivation** (already shown in §3.1): $$\boxed{\theta(t) = \theta_f \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_h}\right) = 60 \left(1 - e^{-t/45}\right)}$$ **Step 2 — Cooling Derivation** (already shown in §3.2): $$\boxed{\theta(t) = \theta_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau_c} = \theta_0 \cdot e^{-t/120}}$$ **Sub-part 2:** At $t = 30$ min, $\tau_h = 45$ min: $$\theta(30) = 60 \left(1 - e^{-30/45}\right) = 60 \left(1 - e^{-0.6667}\right)$$ $$= 60 \times 0.4866 = 29.20 \;°\text{C}$$ **Sub-part 3:** Percentage utilization at $t = 240$ min: $$\theta(240) = 60 \left(1 - e^{-240/45}\right) = 60 \left(1 - e^{-5.333}\right)$$ $$= 60 \times (1 - 0.00482) = 60 \times 0.9952 = 59.71 \;°\text{C}$$ Utilization: $$\frac{59.71}{60} \times 100\% \approx 99.52\%$$ #### Part (b) Solution: **Step 1 — Cooling for 60 min from $\theta_0 = 60$°C:** $$\theta(60) = 60 \cdot e^{-60/120} = 60 \cdot e^{-0.5} = 60 \times 0.6065$$ $$\theta_{\text{at restart}} = 36.39 \;°\text{C}$$ **Step 2 — Re-heating (general loading equation):** At $t = 0$ (restart), $\theta_0 = 36.39$°C, $\theta_f = 60$°C, $\tau_h = 45$ min: $$\theta(t) = 60 + (36.39 - 60) e^{-t/45} = 60 - 23.61 \cdot e^{-t/45}$$ **At the instant of restart ($t = 0$):** $\theta = 36.39$°C. **Step 3 — Time to reach 95% of steady state:** Set $\theta(t) = 0.95 \times 60 = 57$°C: $$57 = 60 - 23.61 \cdot e^{-t/45}$$ $$23.61 \cdot e^{-t/45} = 3$$ $$e^{-t/45} = \frac{3}{23.61} = 0.1271$$ $$t = 45 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{0.1271}\right) = 45 \times \ln(7.868)$$ $$= 45 \times 2.063 \approx 92.83 \text{ minutes} \approx 1 \text{ hour } 33 \text{ min}$$ **Step 4 — Thermal Stress Comment:** Since $\tau_c = 120$ min $> \tau_h = 45$ min, the motor does **not** cool fully in 1 hour. At restart, the winding is already at $36.39$°C above ambient. The **effective thermal headroom** is reduced — the machine reaches 95% of its rated steady-state in only **~93 min instead of the cold-start time of ~135 min** (since $4\tau_h = 180$ min for cold start to ~98%). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Q B):** > 1. When the machine is **not fully cooled**, students often erroneously use the cold-start heating equation. Use the **general loading form** with $\theta_0 \neq 0$. > 2. Many forget to convert time units — $\tau$ in **minutes** but the question may ask in **hours**. Always normalize. > 3. The thermal stress comment is **not optional** — KTU awards at least 1 mark for the engineering interpretation. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin this on your wall!)** - ✅ **Heating Equation:** $\theta(t) = \theta_f \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_h}\right)$ — always starts from 0, rises toward $\theta_f$. - ✅ **Cooling Equation:** $\theta(t) = \theta_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau_c}$ — starts from $\theta_0$, decays to 0. - ✅ **General Loading Equation:** $\theta(t) = \theta_f^{\text{new}} + \left(\theta_0 - \theta_f^{\text{new}}\right) e^{-t/\tau_h}$ — the **master equation** that subsumes both heating and cooling. - ✅ **Heating Time Constant:** $\tau_h = \dfrac{m c}{A \lambda}$ — depends on mass, specific heat, cooling area, dissipation coefficient. - ✅ **Final Steady-State Temperature Rise:** $\theta_f = \dfrac{P_{loss}}{A \lambda}$ — at steady state, all loss = all dissipation. - ✅ **63.2% Rule (Heating):** At $t = \tau_h$, $\theta = 0.632\,\theta_f$. At $t = 4\tau_h$, $\theta \approx 0.98\,\theta_f$ (machine considered fully heated). - ✅ **36.8% Rule (Cooling):** At $t = \tau_c$, $\theta = 0.368\,\theta_0$. At $t = 4\tau_c$, $\theta \approx 0.02\,\theta_0$ (machine considered fully cooled). - ✅ **Self-Ventilated Motors:** $\tau_c = (2 \text{ to } 3)\,\tau_h$ because the shaft-mounted fan stops when the motor is switched off. - ✅ **Force-Ventilated Motors:** $\tau_c \approx \tau_h$ because the external blower continues regardless of motor state. - ✅ **Thermal Resistance Analogy:** $R_{th} = \dfrac{\theta}{P_{loss}} = \dfrac{1}{A \lambda}$ — direct analogy to Ohm's law with $\theta \leftrightarrow V$, $P \leftrightarrow I$. - ✅ **Re-rating Logic:** If permissible $\theta$ > actual $\theta_f$, the motor can be re-rated to a **higher output** by the ratio $\sqrt{\theta_{per}/\theta_f}$ (for copper-loss-dominant machines). - ✅ **Insulation Class Limits (most-tested):** Class A = 105°C, B = 130°C, F = 155°C, H = 180°C (max winding temperature). - ✅ **Duty Cycle Tip:** For intermittent duty, the **average loss** over one full cycle determines the equivalent $\theta_f$ — if it is below rated, the motor is safe. > **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Heating and Cooling Curves** 🎓

topics.title: Loading Conditions and Classes of Duty
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Loading Conditions and Classes of Duty

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Loading Condition** of an electric drive refers to the **functional relationship between the load torque (T_L) and the speed (ω) or position (θ) of the driven shaft** during the operation of a motor-load system. It is the mechanical characteristic curve T_L = f(ω) that the motor must overcome throughout its operating cycle.

**Class of Duty** (or **Duty Cycle**) is the **standardized pattern of load and rest intervals** applied to a machine (motor), classified as per **IS 325 / IEC 60034-1** standards. Each class (S1 to S8) describes a specific sequence of starting, running, braking, no-load, and rest periods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlight:** Proper matching of motor characteristics with the **load torque-speed profile** and **duty class** is the foundational step in drive selection. Mismatching leads to motor overheating, poor speed regulation, or oversized/costly installations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of an electric motor like a **delivery person carrying parcels on a bicycle**:

- If the parcels (load) are **always the same weight** → the rider needs **constant pedaling effort** regardless of speed. This is a **Constant Torque Load** (e.g., conveyor belt, compressor).

- If the parcels are **stacked high and the rider goes fast**, air resistance (drag) becomes huge → effort grows with the *square* of speed. This is a **Quadratic Torque Load** (e.g., fan, centrifugal pump).

- If the rider has a **fixed-time delivery** and goes faster, the parcel must be lighter. This is a **Constant Power Load** (e.g., lathe, traction at high speed).

- The **delivery schedule** — whether the rider works 8 hours straight, takes breaks, or does burst deliveries — is the **Class of Duty** of the drive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The motor is not just selected by *rated power*. It is selected by the **shape of the load curve** (loading condition) **and** the **time-pattern of loading** (duty class). Both are non-negotiable per IS 325 / IEC 60034-1.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Reference Ambient Temperature:** **40°C** (as per IS 325 for Indian motors).
- **Duty Cycle Designation Temperature Rise Limits:** Class A = **60°C**, Class E = **75°C**, Class B = **80°C**, Class F = **100°C**, Class H = **125°C**.
- **Overload Factor (λ):** typically **1.15 to 1.6** (1-minute rating for short-time duties).
- **Duty Cycle Time (T):** total period of one working + rest cycle, in **seconds**.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Loads — Structured Logic Breakdown

Electric drive loads are classified into **two fundamental categories**: **Active Loads** and **Passive Loads**.

#### A. Active Loads (Potential Energy Sources)
Active loads *deliver* energy back to the system during transient conditions (e.g., a load being lowered, or a spring unwinding).

- **Gravitational Loads:** Hoists, elevators, cranes — torque sign changes with direction of motion.
  $$T_L = m \cdot g \cdot r \cdot \sin\theta$$
- **Elastic / Spring Loads:** Presses, spring-loaded reels — torque depends on angular position.
  $$T_L = K \cdot \theta$$
- **Compressive/Tensile Loads:** Rolling mills, calenders — torque often considered as a function of position and material thickness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Active loads can cause **negative torque** (regenerative braking) which feeds energy back to the supply. The motor drive *must* be a **regenerative four-quadrant drive** (e.g., hoist lowering, electric vehicle downhill braking).

#### B. Passive Loads (Kinetic Energy Sinks)
Passive loads *absorb* energy — they can never return it. They are further sub-divided by their speed-dependence.

| # | Load Category | Functional Form | Physical Cause | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Constant Torque Load** | $T_L = \text{constant}$ | Friction, weight lifting | Conveyor, hoist at constant speed, compressor |
| 2 | **Linear Torque Load** | $T_L \propto \omega$ | Viscous friction | Dashpot, eddy-current brakes |
| 3 | **Quadratic (Parabolic) Torque Load** | $T_L \propto \omega^2$ | Fluid friction / windage | Fan, centrifugal pump, blower, propeller |
| 4 | **Inverse (Constant Power) Load** | $T_L \propto 1/\omega$ | Material removal / winding | Lathe, centerless grinder, winder/unwinder |
| 5 | **Pulse / Impact Load** | $T_L = f(t)$ discrete peaks | Reciprocating mass | Punch press, rolling mill stand, forging hammer |
| 6 | **Friction Loads (Coulomb)** | $T_L = \mu \cdot F_N \cdot r$ | Dry sliding friction | Sliding trolley, railway traction (Coulomb part) |

#### C. The Generalized Load Equation

Every practical load torque can be expressed as a **polynomial in speed**:

$$T_L = T_0 + T_v \cdot \omega + T_a \cdot \omega^2$$

Where:
- $T_0$ → **Frictional / Coulomb component** (speed-independent).
- $T_v \cdot \omega$ → **Viscous friction component** (linear with speed).
- $T_a \cdot \omega^2$ → **Aerodynamic / fluid friction component** (quadratic with speed).

In **electrical engineering convention**, for motor drive matching:
- $T_L = K_1 \cdot \omega^n$ where $n = 0$ (const), $n = 1$ (linear), $n = 2$ (quadratic).

### 2.2 Active vs Passive — Engineering Consequences

| Parameter | Active Load | Passive Load |
|---|---|---|
| Energy direction | Bi-directional (regen possible) | Uni-directional (only absorbed) |
| Effect during slow-down | Drives the motor (acts as prime-mover) | Resists the motion always |
| Drive quadrant requirement | Four-quadrant (Q1–Q4) | Often one-quadrant (Q1) sufficient |
| Example | Hoist lowering, EV downhill | Fan, pump, conveyor |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Selection of motor rating** in rolling mills, paper mills, and printing machines.
- **Sizing flywheels** for impact loads (punch press, reciprocating compressor).
- **Determining heating cycles** for S3 to S8 duty-class motors.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **directly examinable** in KTU Module 1 (Part A and Part B). Memorize the columns, not just the equations.

| Sl. | Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Boundary / Range |
|-----|---------|----------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Generalized load equation | $T_L = T_0 + T_v\omega + T_a\omega^2$ | $T_L$ in N·m, $\omega$ in rad/s | Valid for any passive load |
| 2 | Constant torque | $T_L = K_1$ | $K_1$ in N·m | $\dfrac{dT_L}{d\omega} = 0$ |
| 3 | Linear torque | $T_L = K_2 \cdot \omega$ | $K_2$ in N·m·s/rad | $\dfrac{dT_L}{d\omega} = K_2$ |
| 4 | Quadratic torque | $T_L = K_3 \cdot \omega^2$ | $K_3$ in N·m·s²/rad² | $\dfrac{dT_L}{d\omega} = 2K_3\omega$ |
| 5 | Inverse / constant power | $T_L = \dfrac{P_L}{\omega}$ | $P_L$ in W | $P_L = \text{const}$, $T_L\omega = \text{const}$ |
| 6 | Mechanical power developed | $P_L = T_L \cdot \omega$ | $P_L$ in W | Steady-state match: $P_m = P_L$ |
| 7 | Equivalent (RMS) torque | $T_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\sum T_{Li}^2 \cdot t_i}$ | $T$ in s | Variable load → constant load equiv. |
| 8 | Equivalent current method | $I_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\sum I_i^2 \cdot t_i}$ | $I$ in A | Used when $T_L \propto I$ |
| 9 | Equivalent power method | $P_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\sum P_i^2 \cdot t_i}$ | $P$ in W | Used when $T_L \propto P / \omega$ |
| 10 | Overload factor (short-time duty) | $\lambda = \dfrac{T_{max}}{T_{rated}}$ | dimensionless | $\lambda \approx 1.15$ to $1.6$ for S2 |
| 11 | Thermal time constant | $\tau_{th} = \dfrac{H}{S}$ | $H$ in J/°C, $S$ in W/°C | $\tau_{th} = 30$ to $120$ min |
| 12 | Heating during variable load | $Q = \sum I_i^2 R \cdot t_i$ | $Q$ in J | $Q_{variable} = Q_{rated}$ for equivalent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical substitution rule for KTU:** Use $T_{eq}$ method when **flux is held constant** (constant $\Phi$). Use $I_{eq}$ method when motor constants are unknown. Use $P_{eq}$ method for non-linear $T$–$I$ relations (e.g., series motor, cage rotor).

### 2.5 Classes of Duty — IS 325 / IEC 60034-1 Classification

| Code | Name | Sketch / Pattern | Typical Use |
|------|------|------------------|-------------|
| **S1** | Continuous duty | Run at constant load until thermal equilibrium | Pumps, fans, compressors |
| **S2** | Short-time duty | Run → Stop → Cool to ambient | Crane motors, valve actuators, door openers |
| **S3** | Intermittent periodic duty (no starting) | Run → Rest → Run (cyclic) | Hoist, conveyor with breaks |
| **S4** | Intermittent periodic duty **with starting** | Start → Run → Rest → Start | Press feeders, weaving machines |
| **S5** | Intermittent periodic duty with **starting and braking** | Start → Run → Brake → Rest | Machine tool spindles, escalators |
| **S6** | Continuous operation with **intermittent load** | Run with load → Run no-load → repeat | Mixers, agitators |
| **S7** | Continuous operation with **starting and braking** (no rest) | Start → Run → Brake → Start (cyclic) | Robotics actuators, reversing drives |
| **S8** | Continuous operation with **periodic speed changes** | Run at $\omega_1$ → Run at $\omega_2$ → Run at $\omega_3$ | Variable-speed machine tools |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **S2, S3, S4, S5, S6, S7, S8 are collectively called "DUTY-CYCLE RATINGS"** — the motor rating in each case is **higher than the continuous rating** because the machine is allowed to cool during the off-period. This is the most-tested concept in KTU Module 1 Part B.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equivalent Torque Method (RMS Torque)

**Problem Setup:** A motor is subjected to a sequence of $n$ load torques $T_{L1}, T_{L2}, \dots, T_{Ln}$ acting for durations $t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n$ respectively. The total cycle time is:

$$T = t_1 + t_2 + \dots + t_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$$

**Step 1 — Energy balance (loss equivalence).**
For a motor with armature resistance $R_a$ and constant field flux $\Phi$, the copper loss in each interval is $I_i^2 R_a$. Heat generated in the full cycle is:

$$Q_{\text{variable}} = R_a \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i^2 \, t_i$$

**Step 2 — Replace with a single equivalent current $I_{eq}$.**
The same total heat must be produced by a constant current $I_{eq}$ flowing for the full period $T$:

$$Q_{\text{constant}} = I_{eq}^2 \cdot R_a \cdot T$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions.**

$$I_{eq}^2 \cdot R_a \cdot T = R_a \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i^2 \, t_i$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $R_a$ and solve.**

$$I_{eq}^2 = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i^2 \, t_i$$

$$I_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i^2 \, t_i}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to equivalent torque (since $T_L \propto I$ for constant $\Phi$).**
Because $T_L = K_t \cdot I$ (with $K_t$ being the torque constant), and $K_t$ is common to all terms, it cancels under the square root:

$$T_{eq} = K_t \cdot I_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{Li}^2 \, t_i}$$

**Step 6 — Selection criterion.**
The motor rated torque $T_{\text{rated}} \geq T_{eq}$. The motor is selected from the catalog based on this $T_{eq}$ (and the speed class).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the RMS?** Heat dissipation in the armature is $I^2R$ — a **quadratic** function of current. Therefore the linear average is *useless*; only the **root-mean-square** preserves the correct total thermal stress on the insulation.

### 3.2 Derivation: Constant Power Load (Inverse Torque) — Active Load

**Given:** A load demands constant mechanical power $P_L$ over a speed range $\omega_{\min}$ to $\omega_{\max}$. The motor torque is:

**Step 1.** From $P_L = T_L \cdot \omega$:

$$T_L = \frac{P_L}{\omega}$$

**Step 2.** Differentiate to find the slope of the characteristic:

$$\frac{dT_L}{d\omega} = -\frac{P_L}{\omega^2}$$

**Step 3.** At $\omega = \omega_{\max}$ (highest speed), torque is **minimum**:
$$T_{L,\min} = \frac{P_L}{\omega_{\max}}$$

**Step 4.** At $\omega = \omega_{\min}$ (lowest speed), torque is **maximum**:
$$T_{L,\max} = \frac{P_L}{\omega_{\min}}$$

**Step 5.** Check via the ratio:
$$\frac{T_{L,\max}}{T_{L,\min}} = \frac{\omega_{\max}}{\omega_{\min}}$$

This explains why **winding / unwinding machines** (e.g., paper reel) require motors with **wide constant-power field-weakening range** — often 4:1 or even 6:1.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Matching Motor Curve to Load Curve

The **stability of an operating point** is determined by the slope condition at the intersection of motor curve $T_m(\omega)$ and load curve $T_L(\omega)$:

$$\text{Stable operating point} \iff \frac{dT_m}{d\omega} < \frac{dT_L}{d\omega}$$

| Load Type | Slope of $T_L$ | Required Motor Slope | Motor Type |
|-----------|----------------|----------------------|------------|
| Constant torque | 0 | $< 0$ (shunt motor) | DC shunt / IM cage |
| Quadratic (fan) | $2K_3\omega > 0$ | $< 2K_3\omega$ | DC series / IM cage |
| Constant power | $-P/\omega^2 < 0$ | $> -P/\omega^2$ | Ward-Leonard / V/f IM |
| Linear | $K_2 > 0$ | $< K_2$ | DC shunt |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In exam answers, always draw a **sketch** with $T$ on Y-axis and $\omega$ on X-axis, plot **both** $T_m$ and $T_L$ curves, and **shade the stable operating point**. This alone can fetch 2 to 3 easy marks.

### 3.4 Derivation: Flywheel Sizing for Impact Loads (S4 / S5)

**Given:** A punch press of maximum load torque $T_{L,\max}$ acts for time $t_1$, no-load (or motor-only) operation occurs for time $t_2$, in a cycle period $T = t_1 + t_2$.

**Step 1 — Energy supplied by motor during full cycle:**
$$W_{\text{motor}} = T_m \cdot \omega \cdot T$$

**Step 2 — Energy absorbed by load during full cycle (assume motor torque $T_m$ is constant):**
$$W_{\text{load}} = T_{L,\max} \cdot \omega \cdot t_1$$

**Step 3 — Energy difference goes to (or comes from) the flywheel:**

$$W_{\text{flywheel}} = W_{\text{motor}} - W_{\text{load}} = \omega \left( T_m \cdot T - T_{L,\max} \cdot t_1 \right)$$

**Step 4 — Equate to the kinetic energy change of the flywheel during the impact:**

$$\frac{1}{2} J \left( \omega_{\max}^2 - \omega_{\min}^2 \right) = \omega \cdot T_m \cdot T - T_{L,\max} \cdot t_1 \cdot \omega$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $J$ (moment of inertia):**

$$J = \frac{2 \cdot T_m \cdot T - 2 \cdot T_{L,\max} \cdot t_1}{\omega_{\max}^2 - \omega_{\min}^2} \cdot \omega$$

Using the slip/speed ratio $s = \dfrac{\omega_{\max} - \omega_{\min}}{\omega}$ (typically $s \approx 0.1$ to $0.2$ for a punch press):

$$J = \frac{T_m \cdot T - T_{L,\max} \cdot t_1}{\omega^2 \cdot s}$$

This is the **standard KTU formula** for flywheel selection in punch presses and rolling mills.
