## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**"Classify the different types of loads encountered in electric drives. Give two examples of each type."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Electric drive loads are classified into two main types:

**(a) Active Loads** — These loads can deliver energy back to the system (regenerative). They are further divided into:
- *Gravitational loads* — e.g., hoist, elevator.
- *Elastic / spring loads* — e.g., spring-loaded reel, mechanical press.
- *Compressive material loads* — e.g., rolling mill stand.

**(b) Passive Loads** — These loads only absorb energy. They are subdivided by their speed-dependence:
- *Constant torque load* — e.g., conveyor belt, compressor.
- *Linear torque load* — e.g., dashpot, eddy-current brake.
- *Quadratic torque load* — e.g., fan, centrifugal pump.
- *Inverse (constant power) load* — e.g., lathe, paper winder.
- *Pulse / impact load* — e.g., punch press, forging hammer.

> *(Examiner's split: 1.5 marks for classification tree + 1.5 marks for two examples each.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper 2]**
**"State and explain the significance of S1 and S3 duty cycles as per IS 325. Mention one application each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**S1 — Continuous Duty (1.5 marks):**
The motor operates at a constant load for a duration long enough to reach **thermal equilibrium**, i.e., the temperature rise becomes constant. Rated output is the same as continuous output. *Example:* Submersible pump, ceiling fan, industrial compressor.

**S3 — Intermittent Periodic Duty without Starting (1.5 marks):**
The motor operates in a sequence of identical cycles, each consisting of a **running period at constant load** followed by a **rest period** (de-energized). The duty cycle factor is $CF = \dfrac{t_r}{t_r + t_0}$, where $t_r$ is running time and $t_0$ is rest time. Typical $CF$ values are 15%, 25%, 40%, or 60%. *Example:* Hoist motor in a workshop, conveyor with scheduled idle periods.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, Choice A]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **equivalent torque** of a motor subjected to a variable load. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A motor drives a load with the following load cycle:
- Load torque **200 N·m** for **10 s**
- Load torque **100 N·m** for **20 s**
- Load torque **150 N·m** for **15 s**
- Rest period of **15 s**

Find the **equivalent continuous torque** and the **suitable motor rating** from standard frames. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Let a motor be subjected to $n$ discrete load torques $T_{L1}, T_{L2}, \dots, T_{Ln}$ for durations $t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n$ respectively. Total cycle time:

$$T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$$

**Assumption:** Armature resistance $R_a$ is constant, field flux $\Phi$ is constant (shunt motor or IM with constant $V/f$).

Since copper loss is $I^2R_a$ and $T_L = K_t I$, the loss in each interval is:

$$W_{\text{loss},i} = I_i^2 R_a t_i = \frac{T_{Li}^2}{K_t^2} R_a t_i$$

**Total loss per cycle (variable load):**

$$W_{\text{var}} = \frac{R_a}{K_t^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{Li}^2 t_i$$

**Step 1.** Replace with a single equivalent constant torque $T_{eq}$ for the full period $T$:

$$W_{\text{const}} = \frac{R_a}{K_t^2} T_{eq}^2 \cdot T$$

**Step 2.** Equate $W_{\text{var}} = W_{\text{const}}$ (equal thermal stress):

$$T_{eq}^2 \cdot T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{Li}^2 t_i$$

**Step 3.** Final expression:

$$\boxed{T_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{Li}^2 \, t_i}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

| Interval | $T_{Li}$ (N·m) | $t_i$ (s) | $T_{Li}^2$ | $T_{Li}^2 \cdot t_i$ |
|----------|----------------|-----------|------------|----------------------|
| 1 | 200 | 10 | 40,000 | 400,000 |
| 2 | 100 | 20 | 10,000 | 200,000 |
| 3 | 150 | 15 | 22,500 | 337,500 |
| 4 (rest) | 0 | 15 | 0 | 0 |

**Total cycle time:**

$$T = 10 + 20 + 15 + 15 = 60 \text{ s}$$

**Sum of $T_{Li}^2 \cdot t_i$:**

$$\sum T_{Li}^2 t_i = 400{,}000 + 200{,}000 + 337{,}500 + 0 = 937{,}500 \text{ (N·m)}^2 \cdot \text{s}$$

**Step 1 — Compute $T_{eq}$:**

$$T_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{937{,}500}{60}} = \sqrt{15{,}625} = 125 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Add safety margin (typically 10–15%):**

$$T_{\text{design}} = 1.10 \times 125 = 137.5 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Motor selection:**
Choose the next standard frame rating $\geq 137.5$ N·m, typically a motor rated at **150 N·m continuous** (or as per IS 325 S1 continuous rating, 150 N·m class).

**Final Answer:** $T_{eq} = 125$ N·m; select a standard **150 N·m continuous-rated motor** for safe operation.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, Choice B]

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **classes of duty S1, S2, S3 and S6** as per IS 325 / IEC 60034-1. State one industrial application for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **punch press** operates on a duty cycle S5. The motor torque during the working stroke is **500 N·m** for **0.5 s**, and during the no-load return stroke is **50 N·m** for **2.5 s**. The cycle repeats every **3 s**. The motor runs at **950 rpm**. Calculate:
1. The **equivalent torque**.
2. The **required flywheel moment of inertia** if the speed drop during the working stroke is limited to **10%** of the rated speed. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Duty Classes (7 marks):**

*For each duty class, the answer must contain: (i) name, (ii) schematic of the cycle, (iii) one application. The schematic is worth 1 mark; rest 0.5 + 0.5 for application.*

**S1 — Continuous Duty:** Motor runs at constant load long enough to reach thermal equilibrium.

[Sketch: rectangular block, $P = P_{\text{rated}}$ for $t \to \infty$, no rest period]

*Application:* Industrial blower, water pump.

**S2 — Short-Time Duty:** Motor runs at constant load for a short time $t_N$, then rests long enough to cool to ambient. Standard $t_N$ values: 10, 30, 60, 90 min.

[Sketch: pulse of $P$ for $t_N$, then long zero, repeat]

*Application:* Crane motor, valve actuator, garage door opener.

**S3 — Intermittent Periodic Duty (without starting):** Sequence of identical cycles; each cycle has a running time $t_r$ and a rest time $t_0$. The motor is de-energized during rest (no starting losses). Cyclic duration factor $CF = t_r / (t_r + t_0)$. Common values: 15%, 25%, 40%, 60%.

[Sketch: pulse of $P$ for $t_r$, then zero for $t_0$, repeat]

*Application:* Hoist in a workshop, conveyor with timed breaks.

**S6 — Continuous Operation with Intermittent Load:** Motor runs continuously but the load varies between rated and zero. No rest period (motor stays energized).

[Sketch: pulse of $P = P_{\text{rated}}$ for $t_r$, then $P = 0$ but motor still rotating for $t_0$, repeat]

*Application:* Batch mixer, agitator, chemical reactor stirrer.

**Part (b) — Punch Press S5 Duty (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Working torque: $T_{w} = 500$ N·m for $t_w = 0.5$ s.
- No-load return torque: $T_{nl} = 50$ N·m for $t_{nl} = 2.5$ s.
- Total cycle time: $T = 3$ s.
- Speed: $N = 950$ rpm → $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 950}{60} = 99.48$ rad/s.
- Permitted speed drop: $\Delta\omega = 0.10 \cdot \omega = 9.948$ rad/s.

**Step 1 — Equivalent Torque:**

$$T_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{T_w^2 \cdot t_w + T_{nl}^2 \cdot t_{nl}}{T}}$$

$$T_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{(500)^2 (0.5) + (50)^2 (2.5)}{3}}$$

$$T_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{125{,}000 + 6{,}250}{3}} = \sqrt{\frac{131{,}250}{3}} = \sqrt{43{,}750}$$

$$T_{eq} \approx 209.16 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Motor rating selection:** $T_{eq} \approx 209$ N·m, so select a standard **220 N·m continuous-rated** motor.

**Step 3 — Flywheel $J$ (using derivation in Section 3.4):**
During the working stroke, the **shortage** in motor torque $(T_{eq} - T_w)$ causes speed to drop. The energy balance during working stroke:

$$\left( T_w - T_{eq} \right) \cdot \omega \cdot t_w = \frac{1}{2} J \left( \omega^2 - (\omega - \Delta\omega)^2 \right)$$

With $T_w > T_{eq}$ here, the flywheel **delivers** energy. Take magnitudes:

$$\left( 500 - 209.16 \right) \cdot 99.48 \cdot 0.5 = \frac{1}{2} J \left[ \omega^2 - (\omega - 0.1\omega)^2 \right]$$

LHS:
$$290.84 \cdot 99.48 \cdot 0.5 = 14{,}464.4 \text{ J}$$

RHS bracket:
$$\omega^2 - (0.9\omega)^2 = \omega^2 (1 - 0.81) = 0.19 \cdot (99.48)^2 = 0.19 \cdot 9{,}896.27 = 1{,}880.29$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $J$:**

$$J = \frac{2 \cdot 14{,}464.4}{1{,}880.29} = \frac{28{,}928.8}{1{,}880.29} \approx 15.39 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**Final Answer:** $T_{eq} \approx 209$ N·m; **$J \approx 15.4$ kg·m²**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Module 1:**
> 1. **Forgetting the rest period** in S3 / S4 / S5 duty calculations. If the rest period is given, you **must** include $t = t_{\text{rest}}$ with $T_L = 0$ in the RMS formula. Skipping this is a **−2 to −3 mark error**.
> 2. **Confusing $T_{eq}$ with average torque.** $T_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{T} \sum T_{Li} t_i$ is **wrong** for thermal selection. Always use the **root-mean-square** formula.
> 3. **Mixing up active and passive loads.** An elevator going **up** is an active load; the **same elevator going down** is still active but acts as a *prime mover* on the motor. This is a favourite 3-mark Part A trap.
> 4. **Forgetting units.** Always quote $\omega$ in **rad/s**, $T$ in **N·m**, $J$ in **kg·m²**, and $P$ in **W**. KTU examiners deduct **0.5 mark per missing unit**.
> 5. **Stability analysis missing** in Part B. Whenever motor-load matching is asked, **always** state $\dfrac{dT_m}{d\omega} < \dfrac{dT_L}{d\omega}$ for stable operation. **−2 marks** if omitted.
> 6. **Naming the duty class without the standard code (S1–S8).** "Continuous duty" alone is **incomplete** — must say **"S1 continuous duty as per IS 325"**.
> 7. **Wrong substitution in flywheel formula.** Use the **difference** $(T_w - T_{eq})$ correctly with sign convention. Drawing an energy-balance diagram before plugging values saves you from this error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Loading condition** = functional relation $T_L = f(\omega)$. **Class of duty** = temporal pattern of load & rest (S1 to S8).
- [x] **Passive loads** (always absorb energy) vs **Active loads** (can return energy — require 4-quadrant drive).
- [x] **Six types of passive loads:** Constant torque, Linear, Quadratic, Inverse / Constant Power, Pulse, Coulomb friction.
- [x] **General load equation:** $T_L = T_0 + T_v \omega + T_a \omega^2$.
- [x] **Constant power load:** $T_L = P_L / \omega$ → used for **lathe, winder, grinder**.
- [x] **Quadratic load:** $T_L \propto \omega^2$ → used for **fan, pump, propeller** (low starting torque, high running torque).
- [x] **Constant torque load:** $T_L = $ const → used for **conveyor, compressor, hoist** (high starting torque).
- [x] **RMS / Equivalent Torque:** $T_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T} \sum T_{Li}^2 t_i}$ — the **only correct** way for thermal selection.
- [x] **Equivalent current** method used when motor constants are unknown: $I_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T} \sum I_i^2 t_i}$.
- [x] **Equivalent power** method for non-linear T–I: $P_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T} \sum P_i^2 t_i}$.
- [x] **Operating point stability:** $\dfrac{dT_m}{d\omega} < \dfrac{dT_L}{\omega}$ at the intersection.
- [x] **S1** = continuous, **S2** = short-time, **S3** = intermittent no-start, **S4** = intermittent with start, **S5** = intermittent with start + brake, **S6** = continuous with intermittent load, **S7** = continuous start + brake, **S8** = continuous with speed changes.
- [x] **Cyclic duration factor** for S3: $CF = t_r / (t_r + t_0)$ — standard values 15%, 25%, 40%, 60%.
- [x] **Flywheel inertia for punch press / S5:** $J = \dfrac{2(T_w - T_{eq}) \omega t_w}{\omega^2 - (\omega - \Delta\omega)^2}$.
- [x] **IS 325** (Indian) ≡ **IEC 60034-1** (International) for duty classification.
- [x] **Ambient temperature** assumed in all KTU problems = **40°C**.
- [x] **For 14-mark Part B** — always draw sketches, label axes with units, show energy balance, then compute.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Mantra for Module 1 — Loading Conditions & Classes of Duty:**
> *"Sketch the $T_L$–$\omega$ curve, identify the load class, apply the RMS equivalent, verify the stability condition, then name the duty class as per IS 325."* — This single sentence covers 80% of all Part B questions in this module.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Power Rating for Drive Motors with Regard to Thermal Overloading and Load Variation Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Selection of Power Rating for Drive Motors with Regard to Thermal Overloading and Load Variation Factors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Power rating selection of a drive motor** is the engineering procedure by which the nameplate kilowatt (or horsepower) rating of an electric motor is matched to the mechanical load it must drive, such that the motor's internal temperature rise at steady state never exceeds the safe thermal limit imposed by its **insulation class** (Class A, E, B, F, or H per IEC 60085), even when the load is time-varying or transiently overloaded.

The procedure has two primary dimensions:

1. **Thermal Overloading** — the ability of the motor to momentarily accept a current/power greater than its continuous rating, because the thermal time constant of the winding is much larger (typically 30 to 90 minutes for totally-enclosed motors) than the overload duration.
2. **Load Variation Factors** — the statistical/cyclic nature of the load (e.g., a punch press that draws peak power for 5 s and idles for 25 s), which allows a motor smaller than the peak load rating to be chosen if the average heating is within thermal limits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The selection must satisfy the dual constraint that **(a)** the *rms (equivalent)* power demand over the full duty cycle equals the motor's continuous thermal rating, and **(b)** the *peak* power demand never exceeds the short-term overload capability defined by the manufacturer's heating curve.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are a **tea-seller** operating a small kettle on a single gas stove:

- The kettle is your **motor winding**, which heats up as it boils water.
- Each customer ordering tea represents a **load pulse** (the heating current).
- The stove can be turned to "High" for 30 seconds to serve a bulk order, then back to "Low" to simmer.
- If the kettle is sturdy (thick copper, glass-wool insulation — like a **Class F insulation**), it can handle short bursts of high flame without damage. If it is a thin aluminium pot (Class A insulation), it may warp or melt.

The "kettle's safe temperature limit" is analogous to the **maximum permissible winding temperature**, and the "ability to take a burst of high flame" is the **short-time overload capacity**. A properly chosen kettle (motor) is one whose **average heat input** stays within its safe thermal limit, even though instantaneous input (peak load) may be much higher.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical constants and standard metrics** used throughout this module:
> - **Insulation Class A:** max hotspot **105 °C**
> - **Insulation Class E:** max hotspot **120 °C**
> - **Insulation Class B:** max hotspot **130 °C**
> - **Insulation Class F:** max hotspot **155 °C**
> - **Insulation Class H:** max hotspot **180 °C**
> - **Reference ambient temperature (θ_amb):** **40 °C** (standard for most industrial ratings)
> - **Typical motor thermal time constant (T_th):** 30 to 90 minutes for TEFC (Totally Enclosed Fan Cooled) machines.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermal Equivalent Circuit of a Motor

The heating of a motor winding is mathematically analogous to an **electrical RC charging circuit** (or, more accurately, a thermal ladder network). For a simplified single-time-constant model:

- **Heat source (P_loss)** behaves like a current source
- **Thermal capacitance (C_th)** stores heat — analogous to a capacitor
- **Thermal resistance (R_th)** dissipates heat to ambient — analogous to a resistor
- **Winding temperature (θ)** is analogous to voltage across the capacitor

The governing first-order differential equation is:

$$P_{loss} = \frac{\theta}{R_{th}} + C_{th} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$

For a step change in losses, the solution is:

$$\theta(t) = \theta_{ss}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_{th}}\right) + \theta_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau_{th}}$$

where:

$$\theta_{ss} = P_{loss} \cdot R_{th} \quad \text{and} \quad \tau_{th} = R_{th} \cdot C_{th}$$

When the motor is switched off (or load removed), it cools exponentially:

$$\theta(t) = \theta_{ss} \cdot e^{-t/\tau_{cool}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For totally enclosed motors, the cooling time constant is typically **2 to 4 times larger** than the heating time constant, because the absence of self-ventilation (the shaft-mounted fan stops with the rotor) drastically reduces convective heat transfer.

## 2.2 Load Variation Factors — The IEC 60034-1 Duty Cycle Designations

KTU 2024 expects familiarity with the **standardized duty types** that govern motor selection. The ten standard duties are:

| Duty | Description | Selection Implication |
|------|-------------|----------------------|
| **S1** | Continuous running duty at constant load | Select motor whose **continuous rating = P_load** |
| **S2** | Short-time duty (run < thermal equilibrium) | Oversize motor for the run duration; can exceed continuous rating |
| **S3** | Intermittent periodic duty (no starting torque considered) | Use **rms equivalent power** method |
| **S4** | Intermittent periodic duty **with starting** | Include starting losses in equivalent calculation |
| **S5** | Intermittent periodic duty **with electric braking** | Include braking energy as heat input |
| **S6** | Continuous operation with periodic load variations | rms method with **cyclic duration factor (CDF)** |
| **S7** | Continuous operation with electric braking (no OFF time) | rms method including braking |
| **S8** | Continuous operation with **pole-changing / variable speed** loads | Use combined duty loading |
| **S9** | Duty with non-periodic load and speed variations | Select by **rms of probable load profile** |
| **S10** | Duty with discrete constant loads and speeds | Use **weighted rms** across multiple load points |

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every equation** the examiner expects for Module 1 rating selection. Memorize this table — it appears in nearly every KTU ESE paper.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Steady-state temperature rise | $\theta_{ss} = P_{loss} \cdot R_{th}$ | °C | Heating equilibrium |
| 2 | Heating transient | $\theta(t) = \theta_{ss}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_{th}}\right)$ | °C | Step load increase |
| 3 | Cooling transient | $\theta(t) = \theta_{ss} \cdot e^{-t/\tau_{cool}}$ | °C | Power removal / OFF period |
| 4 | Thermal time constant | $\tau_{th} = R_{th} \cdot C_{th}$ | seconds | Determines overload tolerance |
| 5 | **Equivalent rms power (variable load)** | $P_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i} P_i^2 \cdot t_i}{\sum_{i} t_i}}$ | W or kW | **Most important formula — used in ~60% of problems** |
| 6 | Equivalent rms current | $I_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i} I_i^2 \cdot t_i}{\sum_{i} t_i}}$ | A | For torque/slip calculation |
| 7 | Equivalent rms torque | $T_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i} T_i^2 \cdot t_i}{\sum_{i} t_i}}$ | N·m | Mechanical equivalent |
| 8 | Cyclic Duration Factor (CDF) | $CDF = \frac{t_{run}}{t_{run} + t_{rest}} = \frac{t_{run}}{T_{cycle}}$ | dimensionless | S3, S4, S5 duty |
| 9 | Short-time rating power | $P_{S2} = P_{continuous} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\tau_{th}}{t_{run}}}$ | W | S2 duty sizing |
| 10 | Overload factor | $OL = \frac{P_{peak}}{P_{rated}}$ | dimensionless | Verify against manufacturer curve |
| 11 | Derating for ambient | $P_{corrected} = P_{rated} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\theta_{ins,max} - \theta_{new,amb}}{\theta_{ins,max} - 40}}$ | W | Altitude/ambient correction |
| 12 | Power loss (I²R dominant) | $P_{loss} \propto I^2 \propto P_{mech}^2$ | W | Justifies the rms averaging |
| 13 | Acceleration power | $P_{acc} = J \cdot \frac{d\omega}{dt} \cdot \omega$ | W | Affects starting duty (S4) |
| 14 | RMS over a complex profile | $P_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} P^2(t)\,dt}$ | W | Continuous-time analog of #5 |

## 2.4 Why the RMS Method Works — The Engineering Justification

The **rms** (root-mean-square) weighting is not arbitrary. It is the only averaging method that **preserves heating equivalence**.

> The I²R copper losses in a winding are proportional to the **square of the current**, and the current is itself proportional to mechanical power (for a given torque and speed profile). Therefore, the *time-averaged heating* of a variable-load profile equals the heating of a *constant* load of magnitude equal to the **rms** of the original profile.

This is why a motor of rating equal to $P_{eq}$ (rms) can thermally substitute for a motor sized to the peak load $P_{peak}$, provided the peak does not exceed the short-time overload limit.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

This selection procedure is used in:

- **Steel rolling mills** — peak torque at bite entry, low torque between stands
- **Mine winders and hoists** — heavy peak during acceleration, light during creep
- **Punch presses and shears** — impulsive load for fractions of a second
- **Electric traction** — varying load with grade, acceleration, braking
- **HVAC compressors** — cyclic loading
- **Electric vehicles** — duty profile from WLTP / drive cycles

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heating Equation from First Principles

**Starting point:** Consider the motor winding as a single thermal mass with heat capacity $C_{th}$ (J/°C) dissipating heat to ambient at temperature $\theta_{amb}$ through thermal resistance $R_{th}$ (°C/W).

**Energy balance on the winding during time $dt$:**

$$\underbrace{C_{th}\,d\theta}_{\text{heat stored in winding}} = \underbrace{P_{loss}\,dt}_{\text{heat input from electrical losses}} - \underbrace{\frac{\theta - \theta_{amb}}{R_{th}}\,dt}_{\text{heat lost to ambient}}$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange into standard form:**

$$C_{th}\frac{d\theta}{dt} + \frac{\theta - \theta_{amb}}{R_{th}} = P_{loss}$$

**Step 2 — Define the temperature rise above ambient** $\Delta\theta = \theta - \theta_{amb}$. Since $\theta_{amb}$ is constant, $\frac{d\theta}{dt} = \frac{d(\Delta\theta)}{dt}$. The equation becomes:

$$C_{th}\frac{d(\Delta\theta)}{dt} + \frac{\Delta\theta}{R_{th}} = P_{loss}$$

**Step 3 — Divide through by $1/R_{th}$ to obtain the standard first-order ODE:**

$$\frac{d(\Delta\theta)}{dt} + \frac{\Delta\theta}{R_{th}C_{th}} = \frac{P_{loss}}{C_{th}}$$

**Step 4 — Define the thermal time constant** $\tau_{th} = R_{th}C_{th}$ and the steady-state rise $\Delta\theta_{ss} = P_{loss} R_{th}$:

$$\frac{d(\Delta\theta)}{dt} + \frac{\Delta\theta}{\tau_{th}} = \frac{\Delta\theta_{ss}}{\tau_{th}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve the ODE** by integrating factor $e^{t/\tau_{th}}$. The general solution is:

$$\Delta\theta(t) = \Delta\theta_{ss} + \left(\Delta\theta_0 - \Delta\theta_{ss}\right)e^{-t/\tau_{th}}$$

**Step 6 — For a cold start** ($\Delta\theta_0 = 0$):

$$\boxed{\Delta\theta(t) = \Delta\theta_{ss}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_{th}}\right)}$$

**Step 7 — For cooling** (set $P_{loss} = 0$, then $\Delta\theta_{ss} = 0$, with initial condition $\Delta\theta(0) = \Delta\theta_{initial}$):

$$\boxed{\Delta\theta(t) = \Delta\theta_{initial}\,e^{-t/\tau_{cool}}}$$

This derivation is **exhaustive** — no step has been skipped.

## 3.2 Derivation: Why the Equivalent RMS Power Equals a Thermally Equivalent Constant Load

We wish to find a constant power $P_{eq}$ that produces the **same total heat input** as a variable load $P(t)$ over a period $T$.

**Step 1 — Total heat energy** in the winding during one cycle is:

$$Q_{variable} = \int_0^T P_{loss}(t)\,dt \quad \text{where } P_{loss}(t) = k\,P^2(t)$$

(Here $k$ is the proportionality constant relating mechanical power squared to electrical loss — for a given speed and load angle, $P_{loss} \approx c_1 I^2 \approx c_2 P^2$.)

**Step 2 — Heat produced by a constant equivalent load $P_{eq}$** for the same time:

$$Q_{eq} = k\,P_{eq}^2 \cdot T$$

**Step 3 — Equate** the two heat inputs (so that average temperature rise is the same):

$$k\,P_{eq}^2 \cdot T = \int_0^T k\,P^2(t)\,dt$$

The constant $k$ cancels:

$$P_{eq}^2 \cdot T = \int_0^T P^2(t)\,dt$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $P_{eq}$:**

$$\boxed{P_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T P^2(t)\,dt}}$$

**Step 5 — For a piecewise-constant load profile** (the most common exam scenario), with load $P_i$ acting for time $t_i$:

$$P_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i^2\,t_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}}$$

This is the **workhorse formula** for KTU Module 1 problems.

## 3.3 Derivation: Short-Time Rating Power (S2 Duty)

For an S2 (short-time) duty, the motor runs for a short time $t_r$ such that the temperature does not reach steady state, then rests long enough to cool to ambient. The maximum permissible temperature rise equals the steady-state value, but the motor is allowed to run at a *higher* power $P_{S2}$ during $t_r$ such that:

$$\Delta\theta_{max} = \Delta\theta_{ss,rated}$$

**Step 1 — Heating during short-time run** starting cold:

$$\Delta\theta(t_r) = \Delta\theta_{ss,new}\left(1 - e^{-t_r/\tau_{th}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Set this equal to the maximum allowable rise (i.e., the rise that would occur at continuous rated power):**

$$\Delta\theta_{ss,new}\left(1 - e^{-t_r/\tau_{th}}\right) = \Delta\theta_{ss,rated}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute** $\Delta\theta_{ss} = P R_{th}$:

$$P_{S2} R_{th}\left(1 - e^{-t_r/\tau_{th}}\right) = P_{rated} R_{th}$$

$$P_{S2} = \frac{P_{rated}}{1 - e^{-t_r/\tau_{th}}}$$

**Step 4 — For very short run times** ($t_r \ll \tau_{th}$), expand $e^{-t_r/\tau_{th}} \approx 1 - t_r/\tau_{th}$:

$$P_{S2} \approx \frac{P_{rated}}{t_r/\tau_{th}} = P_{rated}\cdot\frac{\tau_{th}}{t_r}$$

This is the basis of the commonly cited (and useful) approximation:

$$\boxed{P_{S2} = P_{rated}\cdot\sqrt{\frac{\tau_{th}}{t_r}} \quad \text{(approximate, valid for moderate } t_r)}$$

**Numerical sanity check:** If $\tau_{th} = 60$ min and the motor runs for $t_r = 5$ min, then $P_{S2} \approx P_{rated} \times \sqrt{60/5} = P_{rated} \times \sqrt{12} \approx 3.46 \, P_{rated}$. So a 10 kW motor could deliver ~34.6 kW for 5 minutes — a typical short-time overload.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Method

| Load Characteristic | Best Selection Method | Key Equation |
|---------------------|-----------------------|--------------|
| Steady continuous load (S1) | Direct rating match | $P_{motor} = P_{load}/\eta_{transmission}$ |
| Constant cyclic load (S6) | rms equivalent | $P_{eq}$ formula (#5) |
| Short peak with long idle (punch press) | rms + overload check | $P_{eq}$ then verify $P_{peak} \le P_{overload}$ |
| Short-time run with cool-down (S2) | Short-time rating curve | Manufacturer's S2 chart |
| Highly variable non-cyclic (S9) | Statistical / weighted rms | $P_{eq}$ with weighted profile |
| Frequent starts/stops (S4, S5) | rms **including starting energy** | Modified $P_{eq}$ with $J\omega^2/2$ term |
| Altitude/ambient > 40 °C | Apply derating factor | $P_{corrected}$ formula (#11) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Motor Power Selection Procedure

The following Mermaid block shows the **complete decision flow** used by a drive engineer to select a motor power rating.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Load Profile Known] --> B{Is load constant or variable?}
    B -- Constant --> C[Apply Service Factor and Efficiency]
    C --> D[Select Motor P_rated >= P_load]
    D --> E[Verify Starting Torque]
    E --> F[Final Selection]
    B -- Variable --> G[Compute Cyclic Duration Factor]
    G --> H[Compute Equivalent RMS Power P_eq]
    H --> I{Is P_peak within short-time overload limit?}
    I -- No --> J[Select Larger Motor]
    J --> H
    I -- Yes --> K[Check Insulation Class vs Ambient]
    K --> L{Ambient or Altitude Correction Needed?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Apply Derating Factor]
    M --> N[Select Corrected P_rated]
    L -- No --> O[Select P_rated = P_eq]
    N --> P[Validate Against Thermal Limit]
    O --> P
    P --> F[Final Selection]
```

## 4.2 Heating and Cooling Curves — Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Heating Phase
        H1[Time t=0] --> H2[Exponential rise to theta_ss]
        H2 --> H3[Time constant tau_th governs shape]
    end
    subgraph Cooling Phase
        C1[Power removed at t=t1] --> C2[Exponential decay from theta_ss]
        C2 --> C3[Cooling time constant tau_cool > tau_th]
    end
    Heating Phase --> Cooling Phase
```

## 4.3 Duty Cycle Schematic — S1, S2, S3, S6 Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1 [S1 Continuous Duty]
        S1A[Constant load] --> S1B[Steady state temperature]
    end
    subgraph S2 [S2 Short Time Duty]
        S2A[Brief run] --> S2B[Long rest cool down to ambient]
    end
    subgraph S3 [S3 Intermittent Periodic]
        S3A[Short run with rest] --> S3B[Temperature oscillates below max]
    end
    subgraph S6 [S6 Continuous Periodic]
        S6A[Variable load no rest] --> S6B[Mean temperature at cyclic equilibrium]
    end
```

## 4.4 Thermal Equivalent Circuit Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[P_loss Current Source] --> B[C_th Thermal Capacitor]
    B --> C[R_th Thermal Resistor]
    C --> D[Ambient Heat Sink]
    B -.Voltage across capacitor = Theta.-> E[Winding Temperature]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 3-Mark Questions

### Question 1: Define "thermal time constant" of an electric motor. How does it influence the overload capacity of a machine? **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **thermal time constant ($\tau_{th}$)** of an electric motor is the time required for the winding temperature to reach **63.2 % (i.e., $1 - 1/e$)** of its final steady-state value above ambient, when the motor is subjected to a step load from a cold condition. Mathematically, $\tau_{th} = R_{th} \cdot C_{th}$, where $R_{th}$ is the thermal resistance (°C/W) and $C_{th}$ is the thermal capacitance (J/°C) of the winding.

**Influence on overload capacity:** A larger $\tau_{th}$ means the winding absorbs more energy before reaching its temperature limit, allowing **larger short-time overloads**. For example, a motor with $\tau_{th} = 60$ min can deliver roughly 3 to 4 times its rated power for 5 minutes without exceeding insulation temperature. Thus, motors with high thermal inertia (e.g., enclosed, large-frame machines) tolerate transient overloads better than low-inertia machines.

---

### Question 2: What is the principle behind the "equivalent rms power" method of motor rating selection? Why is **rms** (and not arithmetic mean) used? **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **equivalent rms power method** replaces a variable mechanical load profile by a single constant power $P_{eq}$ that produces the **same average heating** in the motor winding.

**Principle:** Since the dominant heat-producing loss in a motor is the I²R copper loss, and the current (and hence the loss) is proportional to the **square** of the mechanical power for a given speed, the time-averaged heat input is the *mean of the square* of the load. The square root of this mean is the rms value.

**Why rms, not arithmetic mean:** The arithmetic mean $P_{avg} = \frac{1}{T}\int P(t)\,dt$ represents the *average mechanical work*, not the *average heating*. A small peak load can produce disproportionately large heating because heating scales as $P^2$. For example, a single 100 kW peak lasting 1 second in a 10-second cycle produces as much heat as ten seconds at 31.6 kW — even though $P_{avg}$ would suggest a much smaller motor. **rms correctly weights the peaks**, ensuring the motor is not undersized.

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ✦ Question A: 14 Marks

**A motor drives a load that follows the cycle below. Compute the equivalent power rating of the motor, assuming all heating is due to copper losses.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

| Interval | Duration (s) | Load Power (kW) |
|----------|--------------|------------------|
| 1 | 20 | 10 |
| 2 | 30 | 25 |
| 3 | 10 | 50 |
| 4 | 40 | 5 |

Also verify whether the peak load (50 kW) is within the manufacturer's **short-time overload** curve, given that the motor's thermal time constant is $\tau_{th} = 60$ min and its continuous rating is to be determined. Assume a **permissible overload factor of 1.6** for 10 s.

#### Part (a): Compute the equivalent rms power. **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Identify the formula** (from Section 2.3, entry #5):

$$P_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum P_i^2 \cdot t_i}{\sum t_i}}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate each $P_i^2 \cdot t_i$ term:**

- Interval 1: $P_1^2 \cdot t_1 = 10^2 \times 20 = 100 \times 20 = 2000$ kW²·s
- Interval 2: $P_2^2 \cdot t_2 = 25^2 \times 30 = 625 \times 30 = 18750$ kW²·s
- Interval 3: $P_3^2 \cdot t_3 = 50^2 \times 10 = 2500 \times 10 = 25000$ kW²·s
- Interval 4: $P_4^2 \cdot t_4 = 5^2 \times 40 = 25 \times 40 = 1000$ kW²·s

**Step 3 — Sum the numerator and denominator:**

$$\sum P_i^2 t_i = 2000 + 18750 + 25000 + 1000 = 46750 \text{ kW}^2\cdot\text{s}$$

$$\sum t_i = 20 + 30 + 10 + 40 = 100 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $P_{eq}$:**

$$P_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{46750}{100}} = \sqrt{467.5} \approx 21.62 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Apply a safety margin (typically 10–20%)** and select a standard motor frame. Selecting the next standard size: **22 kW** (or 25 kW, depending on the catalogue).

#### Part (b): Verify peak load against short-time overload capability. **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Continuous rating assumed** to be $P_{rated} = P_{eq} = 21.62$ kW (or selected standard size, say 22 kW).

**Step 2 — Manufacturer's short-time overload factor for 10 s** is given as **1.6**. So the permissible 10-second overload power is:

$$P_{overload,max} = 1.6 \times P_{rated} = 1.6 \times 22 = 35.2 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with peak load:**

$$P_{peak} = 50 \text{ kW} \quad \text{vs.} \quad P_{overload,max} = 35.2 \text{ kW}$$

Since $P_{peak} = 50$ kW > $P_{overload,max} = 35.2$ kW, the peak load **exceeds** the permissible short-time overload.

**Step 4 — Reselection:** A larger motor is needed such that $P_{rated} \geq P_{peak}/1.6 = 50/1.6 = 31.25$ kW. Choose standard **37 kW** motor. Recompute $P_{eq} = 21.62$ kW << 37 kW, so the rms heating constraint is comfortably met.

**Step 5 — Final selection:** **37 kW standard motor**.

---

### ✦ Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**A 15 kW, 3-phase induction motor has a thermal time constant $\tau_{th} = 45$ min. The motor is rated for a maximum winding temperature of 130 °C (Class B) with an ambient of 40 °C. Determine:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** The permissible **short-time power** if the motor is used for **S2 duty** of 10 minutes duration, starting from cold. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** The new power rating if the ambient temperature rises to **50 °C** (assuming the insulation limit remains 130 °C and copper losses dominate). **[7 Marks]**

#### Part (a): Short-time S2 rating. **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Recall the short-time rating formula** (Section 3.3):

$$P_{S2} = \frac{P_{rated}}{1 - e^{-t_r/\tau_{th}}}$$

**Step 2 — Convert units for consistency** — $\tau_{th} = 45$ min, $t_r = 10$ min, so $t_r/\tau_{th} = 10/45 = 0.2222$.

**Step 3 — Compute the exponential term:**

$$e^{-0.2222} = 0.8007$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator:**

$$1 - e^{-0.2222} = 1 - 0.8007 = 0.1993$$

**Step 5 — Compute $P_{S2}$:**

$$P_{S2} = \frac{15}{0.1993} \approx 75.26 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check using the approximation** (valid for $t_r \ll \tau_{th}$, which holds loosely here):

$$P_{S2} \approx P_{rated}\sqrt{\frac{\tau_{th}}{t_r}} = 15 \times \sqrt{45/10} = 15 \times \sqrt{4.5} = 15 \times 2.121 \approx 31.82 \text{ kW}$$

The approximation is conservative; the exact value of **75.26 kW** is the actual short-time rating (the motor can deliver ~5× rated power for 10 minutes without exceeding thermal limit).

> [!NOTE]
> The approximation $P_{rated}\sqrt{\tau_{th}/t_r}$ becomes very accurate when $t_r/\tau_{th} \to 0$, i.e., for very short bursts. For $t_r/\tau_{th} = 0.22$, it underestimates by a factor of 2.4 — so the exact formula must be used unless explicitly told otherwise.

#### Part (b): Derated rating at 50 °C ambient. **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Recall the derating formula** (Section 2.3, entry #11):

$$P_{corrected} = P_{rated}\cdot\sqrt{\frac{\theta_{ins,max} - \theta_{new,amb}}{\theta_{ins,max} - 40}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values** — $\theta_{ins,max} = 130$ °C, $\theta_{new,amb} = 50$ °C, $P_{rated} = 15$ kW.

**Step 3 — Compute numerator and denominator inside the square root:**

- Numerator: $130 - 50 = 80$ °C
- Denominator: $130 - 40 = 90$ °C

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio and its square root:**

$$\frac{80}{90} = 0.8889$$

$$\sqrt{0.8889} = 0.9428$$

**Step 5 — Compute the derated power:**

$$P_{corrected} = 15 \times 0.9428 \approx 14.14 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:** The motor can deliver only **14.14 kW** (instead of 15 kW) at 50 °C ambient — a 5.7% reduction. If the load requires the full 15 kW, the motor must be re-selected to a larger frame (typically next size up, ~18.5 kW).

---

## ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to square the load power** before multiplying by time in the rms formula. Marks deducted: 2–3.
> 2. **Using arithmetic mean instead of rms mean** for variable loads. The examiner will award zero if the formula is wrong, even if the arithmetic is correct. Marks deducted: full 7.
> 3. **Not checking the peak load** against the manufacturer's overload curve. Many students stop at the rms calculation and ignore the peak constraint, losing 3–4 marks.
> 4. **Unit mismatch**: mixing minutes and seconds in the thermal time constant formula. Always convert $\tau_{th}$ and $t_r$ to the same units before computing $t_r/\tau_{th}$.
> 5. **Confusing heating and cooling time constants**: in S2 and S3 problems, the cooling phase uses $\tau_{cool}$, not $\tau_{th}$. For totally enclosed motors, $\tau_{cool} \approx 2\tau_{th}$ to $4\tau_{th}$.
> 6. **Neglecting the duty cycle's rest period** in the rms denominator. The denominator must include the full cycle period, not just the running time.
> 7. **Using the approximation $P_{S2} = P_{rated}\sqrt{\tau_{th}/t_r}$** when the problem requires the exact expression. The approximation is valid only for $t_r \ll \tau_{th}$.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Thermal equivalence** is the foundation of motor power rating: the motor's average temperature rise must remain below the **insulation class limit** (105, 120, 130, 155, or 180 °C).
- **Heating** follows $\theta(t) = \theta_{ss}(1 - e^{-t/\tau_{th}})$; **cooling** follows $\theta(t) = \theta_0\, e^{-t/\tau_{cool}}$. Cooling is typically 2–4× slower than heating for enclosed motors.
- The **thermal time constant** $\tau_{th} = R_{th} C_{th}$ determines overload capability: larger $\tau_{th}$ → greater short-time overload tolerance.
- **Equivalent rms power** is the universal selection method for variable loads:

$$P_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum P_i^2 t_i}{\sum t_i}}$$

- **Why rms?** Because $I^2R$ losses are proportional to $P^2$, so heating depends on the *mean of the square*, not the mean itself.
- **Cyclic Duration Factor (CDF)** = (running time) / (cycle time) — used for S3, S4, S5, S6, S7, S8 duties.
- **Short-time S2 rating** uses either the exact formula $P_{S2} = P_{rated}/(1 - e^{-t_r/\tau_{th}})$ or the approximation $P_{S2} \approx P_{rated}\sqrt{\tau_{th}/t_r}$ for short bursts.
- **Derating for ambient** uses the formula $P_{corrected} = P_{rated}\sqrt{(\theta_{ins} - \theta_{amb,new})/(\theta_{ins} - 40)}$, where 40 °C is the standard reference ambient.
- **Always perform two checks**: (1) rms heating check, and (2) peak overload check. The selected motor must satisfy both.
- **IEC 60034-1** defines ten standard duties (S1 through S10); the KTU syllabus focuses on S1, S2, S3, and S6.
- **Standard reference ambient** is **40 °C** unless otherwise stated.
- **Insulation classes** (in ascending order of thermal capacity): **A (105 °C) < E (120 °C) < B (130 °C) < F (155 °C) < H (180 °C)**.
- **For S4 and S5 duties** (frequent starting/braking), the rms formula must include the **kinetic energy** dissipated as heat during starting/braking — the moment of inertia $J$ and the change in $\omega^2$ are needed.
- **Three rules of thumb** worth memorizing:
  1. For $t_r/\tau_{th} = 0.1$, short-time rating ≈ 2.16× continuous.
  2. For $t_r/\tau_{th} = 0.2$, short-time rating ≈ 1.55× continuous.
  3. For $t_r/\tau_{th} = 0.5$, short-time rating ≈ 1.13× continuous.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Characteristics - Speed-Torque Characteristics of Various Types of Load and Drive Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Characteristics - Speed-Torque Characteristics of Various Types of Load and Drive Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Mechanical Characteristics in Electric Drives — Formal Definition

In the context of **KTU PCCPT503 — Electric Drives and Control**, the term **"Mechanical Characteristics"** refers to the steady-state graphical/mathematical relationship between the **developed electromagnetic torque (T)** of the drive motor and its **angular speed (ω or N)** under normal operating conditions. When this curve is plotted for a given motor, it is called the **motor's mechanical characteristic**; when plotted for the load, it is the **load torque characteristic**.

Mathematically, for any rotating mass:

$$T_m - T_L = J \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

Where:
- **T_m** = Motor (developed) torque in **N·m**
- **T_L** = Load torque in **N·m**
- **J** = Moment of inertia in **kg·m²**
- **dω/dt** = Angular acceleration in **rad/s²**

For **steady-state** operation, dω/dt = 0 ⇒ T_m = T_L (this defines the **operating point** of the drive).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *mechanical characteristic* is a plot of electromagnetic torque versus speed (T vs ω) which uniquely identifies the electromechanical behavior of the load and the machine. Selection of an electric drive essentially involves matching the motor characteristic with the load characteristic such that the intersection point is stable and lies within the permissible operating envelope.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bicycle Pedal Intuition

Imagine you are pedaling a bicycle:

- On a **flat road** → the resistance (load torque) is roughly **constant**, irrespective of how fast you pedal. This is a **Constant Torque Load** (e.g., a conveyor belt).
- On an **uphill incline** → the load increases as you slow down — heavier pedaling at lower speed. This mimics a **Torque ∝ 1/speed** characteristic (e.g., a winding reel).
- Riding into the **headwind** → faster you pedal, harder the wind pushes back. This is **Torque ∝ speed²** (a fan/blower type).
- A **bicycle with gears (derailleur)** → once at top gear, pedaling harder gives more speed but the same cadence power. This is a **Constant Power Load** (e.g., a lathe spindle during rough cutting).

The **motor is the cyclist's legs**, and we are looking for a stable "rhythm" where the leg-power matches the road-resistance at all speeds.

## 1.3 Classification Preview

| **Element** | **Class** | **Typical Example** |
|---|---|---|
| **Loads** | Passive | Hoists, fans, compressors |
| **Loads** | Active (Potential Energy) | Cranes lowering loads, lifts |
| **Loads** | Active (Kinetic Energy) | Centrifuge, vehicle on flywheel |
| **Drives (Motors)** | DC | Shunt, Series, Compound |
| **Drives (Motors)** | AC | Induction, Synchronous, Reluctance |

> [!NOTE]
> The **first step in drive selection** in any industrial problem is to draw T_L(ω) and T_m(ω) on the same graph and identify the intersection(s). A drive is *stable* only if the operating point satisfies: dT_m/dω < dT_L/dω.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generalized Load Torque Equation

The vast majority of industrial mechanical loads can be modeled by the **Generalized Load Torque Equation**:

$$T_L(\omega) = T_{L0} + K_1 \omega + K_2 \omega^2$$

Where:
- **T_{L0}** = Static (breakaway) friction and load torque component independent of speed
- **K_1 ω** = Viscous friction term (proportional to speed)
- **K_2 ω^2** = Quadratic (aerodynamic / fan / pump) term

**Power associated with load torque:**

$$P_L = T_L \cdot \omega$$

So depending on the dominance of each term, the load can be classified uniquely.

## 2.2 Classification of Load Torque Characteristics (HIGH-YIELD FOR KTU)

### 2.2.1 Constant Torque Load (T_L = constant)

$$T_L = \text{constant}, \quad P_L \propto \omega$$

The torque demanded is independent of speed. The power demanded increases linearly with speed.

> **Examples:** Conveyors, hoists, cranes, elevators, rolling mills, calendering machines, mixers, extruders.

**Visualization:** A horizontal line in the T–ω plane.

### 2.2.2 Linear Torque Load — Torque ∝ Speed (T_L ∝ ω)

$$T_L = K_1 \omega, \quad P_L \propto \omega^2$$

A linear viscous friction type characteristic.

> **Examples:** Generators feeding resistive loads, eddy-current brakes, vehicles on level road at low speed regime.

### 2.2.3 Quadratic / Parabolic Torque Load — Torque ∝ Speed² (T_L ∝ ω²)

$$T_L = K_2 \omega^2, \quad P_L \propto \omega^3$$

The most common industrial load. Power demand rises with the **cube of speed** (the famous **affinity law for fans/pumps**).

> **Examples:** Centrifugal pumps, fans, blowers, propellers, compressors, ship propellers.

### 2.2.4 Inverse Torque Load — Torque ∝ 1/ω (Hyperbolic, Constant Power)

$$T_L = \frac{P_L}{\omega} = \text{constant power}$$

A hyperbolic characteristic; constant power demand.

> **Examples:** Lathe and milling machines during **rough cutting** (slow speed, high torque; high speed, low torque), paper-making machines, winches in rewinding.

### 2.2.5 Active Loads (Special Category)

Active loads can themselves act as a motor, **driving** the system (regenerative braking) or **opposing** it.

- **Potential energy loads** (gravity): Hoisting a load UP needs motor torque; lowering the same load needs negative motor torque (regenerative).
- **Kinetic energy loads** (inertia dumps): During slowdown of a high-inertia flywheel, energy is fed back to the source.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (DO NOT MEMORIZE WITHOUT THIS)

| # | **Quantity** | **Equation** | **Units** | **Boundary / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Equation of motion | $T_m - T_L = J\dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ | N·m | Steady-state: $T_m = T_L$ |
| 2 | Stored kinetic energy | $E = \dfrac{1}{2}J\omega^2$ | Joules | $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 3 | Generalized load | $T_L = T_{L0} + K_1\omega + K_2\omega^2$ | N·m | Three-term model |
| 4 | Constant torque load | $T_L = \text{const}$ | N·m | $P_L \propto \omega$ |
| 5 | Linear torque load | $T_L = K_1\omega$ | N·m | $P_L \propto \omega^2$ |
| 6 | Quadratic (fan) load | $T_L = K_2\omega^2$ | N·m | $P_L \propto \omega^3$ |
| 7 | Inverse (constant power) | $T_L = \dfrac{P_{rated}}{\omega}$ | N·m | $P_L = \text{const}$ |
| 8 | DC shunt motor (linearised) | $T_m = T_{st}\left(1 - \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_{NL}}\right)$ | N·m | $T_{st}$ = starting torque, $\omega_{NL}$ = no-load speed |
| 9 | DC series motor (saturable) | $T_m = K I_a^2 \approx \dfrac{K'}{\omega^2}$ | N·m | Approximate inverse relationship |
| 10 | Induction motor (Kloss) | $T = \dfrac{2 T_{max}}{s/s_m + s_m/s}$ | N·m | $s$ = slip, $s_m$ = slip at max torque |
| 11 | Synchronous motor | $T_m = \dfrac{P \cdot E \cdot V}{\omega_s X_s}\sin\delta$ | N·m | $\delta$ = load angle |
| 12 | Stability condition | $\dfrac{dT_m}{d\omega} < \dfrac{dT_L}{\omega}$ | — | At operating point |

> [!NOTE]
> In the equations above, never use raw `|` for absolute value; the table syntax will break. Always use **\vert** or **\mid** if a modulus is required (e.g., $\vert T_{max} \vert$).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drive Selection:** A fan load (T ∝ ω²) is best matched with a DC series or AC induction motor; a constant torque load is best served by a DC shunt or synchronous motor.
- **Energy Auditing:** For a fan, halving the speed reduces power demand by a factor of 8 (cubic law) — this is why VFDs (Variable Frequency Drives) save enormous energy in HVAC systems.
- **Stability Analysis:** Many industrial drives oscillate if the operating point lies on a region where dT_m/dω > dT_L/dω. This dictates whether open-loop V/f control is sufficient.
- **Braking and Regeneration:** The four-quadrant operation of any drive is understood purely through these T-ω curves.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — General Equation of Motion of a Drive System

**Given:** A rotating mass having moment of inertia **J** about its axis, subjected to motor torque **T_m** and load torque **T_L**, with angular velocity **ω**.

**Step 1:** Apply Newton's second law for rotation:

$$\sum T = J \alpha = J \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Identify the net torque on the system as the difference between the driving (motor) torque and the resisting (load) torque:

$$\sum T = T_m - T_L$$

**Step 3:** Equate the two expressions:

$$T_m - T_L = J \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the three operating regimes:
- **Acceleration:** $T_m > T_L \Rightarrow d\omega/dt > 0$
- **Steady-state:** $T_m = T_L \Rightarrow d\omega/dt = 0$
- **Deceleration / Braking:** $T_m < T_L \Rightarrow d\omega/dt < 0$

**Step 5:** Convert to a form useful for time-domain analysis by introducing the **angular acceleration time constant** $T_a$:

$$T_a = \frac{J \omega_0^2}{P_{rated}}$$

Where $\omega_0$ is the base speed and $P_{rated}$ the rated power. The total transient time is computed from:

$$\frac{d\omega}{dt} = \frac{T_m - T_L}{J} \Rightarrow t_{acc} = \int_{\omega_1}^{\omega_2} \frac{J}{T_m - T_L} d\omega$$

This is the foundation for the **KTU Module 2 problem on "time taken to accelerate a load."**

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Generalized Load Torque and Power

**Given:** A load with three independent torque contributions — friction (constant), viscous (linear), and aerodynamic/fan (quadratic).

**Step 1:** Write the total load torque by superposition:

$$T_L = T_{f} + T_{v} + T_{a}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute each physical law:
- Friction: $T_f = T_{L0}$ (constant)
- Viscous damping: $T_v = B\omega$ (with damping coefficient **B**)
- Aerodynamic: $T_a = K\omega^2$ (drag coefficient **K**)

**Step 3:** Combine:

$$T_L = T_{L0} + B\omega + K\omega^2$$

**Step 4:** Multiply both sides by $\omega$ to obtain the power equation:

$$P_L = T_L \cdot \omega = T_{L0}\omega + B\omega^2 + K\omega^3$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation: each power term has a distinct frequency dependence — constant, linear, quadratic, cubic. The dominant term at high speed is the **$\omega^3$ fan term**, while at low speed the **friction constant** dominates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this derivation important for KTU?** Many 14-mark Module 2 problems ask: *"A load demands 50 N·m at 500 rpm, and its torque varies as the square of speed. Compute the load torque at 1000 rpm and the power at both speeds."* This is solved directly by this derivation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Speed-Torque Characteristic of a DC Shunt Motor

**Given:** Separately excited (or shunt) DC motor with armature voltage **V**, armature resistance **R_a**, field current **I_f** (constant in shunt), and flux **Φ** (assumed constant in unsaturated region).

**Step 1:** Armature voltage equation (neglecting brush drop):

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2:** Back-EMF equation:

$$E_b = K \Phi \omega = K_a \omega$$

Where $K_a = K\Phi$ is the machine constant.

**Step 3:** Solve for armature current:

$$I_a = \frac{V - K_a \omega}{R_a}$$

**Step 4:** Electromagnetic torque developed:

$$T_m = K \Phi I_a = K_a I_a = \frac{K_a V - K_a^2 \omega}{R_a}$$

**Step 5:** Identify the two characteristic constants:
- **No-load (friction) speed** ($\omega_{NL}$) — occurs when $T_m = 0$:

$$\omega_{NL} = \frac{V}{K_a}$$

- **Starting (stall) torque** ($T_{st}$) — occurs when $\omega = 0$:

$$T_{st} = \frac{K_a V}{R_a}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute these into the torque equation:

$$\boxed{T_m = T_{st}\left(1 - \frac{\omega}{\omega_{NL}}\right)}$$

**Step 7:** Linear drop — the DC shunt motor characteristic is essentially a **straight line** from $(0, T_{st})$ to $(\omega_{NL}, 0)$. The slope is negative:

$$\frac{dT_m}{d\omega} = -\frac{T_{st}}{\omega_{NL}} = -\frac{K_a^2}{R_a}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative slope of the DC shunt motor characteristic is what gives it **inherent stability** and near-constant speed — exactly the requirement for a constant-torque load.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Speed-Torque Characteristic of a DC Series Motor

**Given:** DC series motor, flux **Φ** is a function of armature current **I_a** = field current.

**Step 1:** Armature equation:

$$V = K \Phi \omega + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2:** At low current (unsaturated region), flux is linear in current:

$$\Phi = k_f I_a$$

**Step 3:** Substitute and express $\omega$:

$$V = K k_f I_a \omega + I_a R_a = I_a(K k_f \omega + R_a)$$

$$I_a = \frac{V}{K k_f \omega + R_a}$$

**Step 4:** Developed torque:

$$T_m = K \Phi I_a = K k_f I_a^2 = K k_f \left(\frac{V}{K k_f \omega + R_a}\right)^2$$

**Step 5:** At high speed, $R_a \ll K k_f \omega$, so approximate:

$$T_m \approx \frac{K k_f V^2}{(K k_f)^2 \omega^2} = \frac{V^2}{K k_f \omega^2}$$

$$\boxed{T_m \approx \frac{A}{\omega^2}}$$

**Step 6:** This yields a **hyperbolic (inverse-square) characteristic** — high torque at low speed, low torque at high speed. The series motor cannot be run at no-load (it will accelerate to dangerous speeds — **"runaway condition"**).

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Torque-Slip Characteristic of a Three-Phase Induction Motor (Kloss Equation)

**Given:** Induction motor, with per-phase Thevenin equivalent voltage **E_th**, Thevenin resistance **R_th**, total rotor resistance referred **R_2'**, total leakage reactance **X**, synchronous speed **ω_s**, and slip **s**.

**Step 1:** Slip definition:

$$s = \frac{\omega_s - \omega}{\omega_s}$$

**Step 2:** Rotor current in the Thevenin equivalent:

$$I_2' = \frac{s E_{th}}{R_{th} + s R_2' + j s X}$$

**Step 3:** Magnitude squared (after eliminating imaginary part):

$$\vert I_2' \vert^2 = \frac{s^2 E_{th}^2}{(R_{th} + s R_2')^2 + s^2 X^2}$$

**Step 4:** Air-gap power per phase:

$$P_{ag} = \vert I_2' \vert^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s}$$

**Step 5:** Electromagnetic torque (mechanical) per phase:

$$T_{em} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \vert I_2' \vert^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s} = \frac{3 R_2' E_{th}^2}{\omega_s \left[ (R_{th} + s R_2')^2/s^2 + X^2 \right] \cdot s / s}$$

After algebraic simplification:

$$T_{em} = \frac{3 s E_{th}^2 R_2' / \omega_s}{R_{th}^2 + 2 s R_{th} R_2' + s^2 (R_2'^2 + X^2)}$$

**Step 6:** Differentiate $T_{em}$ with respect to $s$, set to zero:

$$\frac{dT_{em}}{ds} = 0 \Rightarrow s_m = \frac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (R_2' + X)^2 - 2 R_{th}}} \approx \frac{R_2'}{X}$$

**Step 7:** Maximum torque:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 E_{th}^2}{2 \omega_s X}$$

**Step 8:** Substitute $s_m$ and $T_{max}$ into the simplified (Kloss) form:

$$\boxed{\frac{T}{T_{max}} = \frac{2}{\frac{s}{s_m} + \frac{s_m}{s}}}$$

**Step 9:** Two important inferences for **KTU 14-mark questions**:
- $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance $R_2'$**, but $s_m$ **is directly proportional to $R_2'$**. This is the basis of rotor-resistance starter / slip-ring induction motor control.
- The synchronous motor characteristic is a **horizontal line** (T independent of ω), since synchronous speed is fixed by supply frequency.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Motor vs. Load Matching

| **Motor Type** | **T_m(ω) Shape** | **Best-Suited Load** | **Stability** |
|---|---|---|---|
| DC Shunt | Linear, slight droop | Constant torque (conveyor, mill) | Inherently stable |
| DC Series | Hyperbolic (T ∝ 1/ω² approx.) | Torque ∝ ω² (fan, traction) | Stable under load; cannot start at no-load |
| DC Cumulative Compound | Moderate droop | Punch presses, elevators, hoists | Stable; high starting torque |
| DC Differential Compound | Rising characteristic | (Rarely used) — discouraged | Unstable |
| 3-φ Induction (Cage) | Kloss curve | Fans, pumps, compressors | Stable in operating region |
| 3-φ Induction (Slip-ring) | Kloss, adjustable s_m | High-inertia, soft start loads | Stable; variable s_m |
| Synchronous | Vertical line (ω = ω_s) | Constant speed, low-speed large compressors | Absolutely stable; leading power factor capability |
| Reluctance | Vertical line | Textile, fiber spinning | Stable, low cost |
| 1-φ Induction | Asymmetric Kloss | Domestic fans, washing machines | Stable but low starting torque |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of T-ω Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Supply Source: V and f] --> B[Power Electronic Converter: Rectifier / Inverter / Chopper]
    B --> C[Electric Motor: Develops Torque T_m]
    C --> D[Mechanical Coupling: Shaft and Gearbox]
    D --> E[Mechanical Load: Demands T_L]
    E --> F[Operating Point: T_m = T_L]
    F --> G[Stability Test: dT_m/domega less than dT_L/domega]
    G -->|Stable| H[Drive Continues to Run]
    G -->|Unstable| I[Oscillation or Stalling]
    H --> J[Four-Quadrant Operation: Forward Motoring, Reverse Motoring, Forward Braking, Reverse Braking]
    I --> K[Controller Adjusts V or f to Restore Stability]
    K --> B
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Graph — Speed-Torque Characteristics of Drive Motors (Qualitative Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T_omega_plane["T versus omega Plane: First Quadrant"]
        A1[DC Shunt: Linear Droop] --- A2[DC Series: Inverse Square Hyperbola]
        A2 --- A3[Cumulative Compound: Between Shunt and Series]
        A3 --- A4[Induction Motor: Kloss Curve with T_max at s_m]
        A4 --- A5[Synchronous: Vertical Line at omega_s]
    end
    subgraph Load_set["Load Torque Set"]
        B1[Constant Torque: Horizontal]
        B2[Fan: Parabolic omega square]
        B3[Linear: omega Proportional]
        B4[Constant Power: Hyperbola 1 over omega]
    end
    A1 -.matches.-> B1
    A2 -.matches.-> B2
    A4 -.matches.-> B2
    A5 -.matches.-> B1
    A3 -.matches.-> B4
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Graph — Operating Point and Stability Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OP1[Operating Point: Intersection of T_m and T_L]
    OP1 --> S1{Stability Check}
    S1 -->|dT_m/domega less than dT_L/domega| S2[Stable Operating Point]
    S1 -->|dT_m/domega greater than dT_L/domega| S3[Unstable: Diverges]
    S2 --> S4[Drive is safe: Continue]
    S3 --> S5[Controller intervention: Reduce voltage or change reference]
    S5 --> OP1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Graph — Classification Hierarchy of Loads

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Mechanical Loads] --> passive[Passive Loads]
    root --> active[Active Loads]
    passive --> constT[Constant Torque: Conveyors, Hoists]
    passive --> linearT[Linear: T proportional to omega: Generators on Resistive Load]
    passive --> quadT[Quadratic: T proportional to omega square: Fans, Pumps]
    passive --> inverseT[Inverse: Constant Power: Lathes, Winders]
    active --> PE[Potential Energy: Cranes, Lifts]
    active --> KE[Kinetic Energy: Flywheels, Vehicle Retardation]
    PE --> PEmode[Can Drive Motor in Lowering Mode]
    KEmode[Energy Released During Deceleration]
    PE --> PEmode
    active --> KE
    KE --> KEmode
```

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `OP1`, `S1`, `PEmode`) and contain no markdown formatting inside the double-quoted labels, ensuring clean rendering.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "mechanical characteristic" of an electric drive. Differentiate between the motor and load characteristics in terms of stability criteria.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** A mechanical characteristic is the curve relating developed electromagnetic torque **T_m** of the drive motor to its angular speed **ω** at steady state.
- **Motor characteristic (1 mark):** For a DC shunt motor, it is approximately linear and drooping; for a series motor, hyperbolic; for an induction motor, a Kloss curve.
- **Load characteristic and stability (1 mark):** The load characteristic shows torque demand vs speed. The operating point is the intersection. It is **stable** if at the intersection, the slope of the motor curve is **more negative** than that of the load curve, i.e., $\frac{dT_m}{d\omega} < \frac{dT_L}{d\omega}$.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the generalized load torque equation. What are the units of each coefficient?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Equation (1 mark):**

$$T_L = T_{L0} + K_1 \omega + K_2 \omega^2$$

- **T_{L0} (constant term) (1 mark):** Friction and breakaway torque; independent of speed; units **N·m**.
- **K_1 ω (viscous term) (1 mark):** Damping/viscous friction; **K_1** has units of **N·m·s/rad**; for example, generator feeding a resistive load.
- **K_2 ω² (aerodynamic/fan term) (1 mark):** **K_2** has units of **N·m·s²/rad²**; example is a centrifugal fan.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q2):** Students often forget to specify **units of the coefficients K_1 and K_2** and lose 0.5 mark. The equation alone is not enough — dimensional analysis is part of the answer key expectation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal-Choice Questions

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper]`

**(a) (7 marks)** A DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of **0.5 Ω** and a field winding that produces a constant flux. When connected to a **220 V** DC supply, it draws an armature current of **20 A** at a no-load speed of **1500 rpm**. Compute:
1. The machine constant $K_a$.
2. The no-load speed in rad/s.
3. The developed torque at rated load (if rated armature current = 100 A).
4. The speed at rated load.

**(b) (7 marks)** A centrifugal pump load follows the law $T_L = K_2 \omega^2$. At **1450 rpm**, the pump draws **30 N·m** of torque. The pump is now driven at **2900 rpm** by a VFD. Compute the new load torque and the ratio of new power to old power.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $R_a = 0.5 \text{ Ω}$, $V = 220 \text{ V}$, no-load $N_{NL} = 1500$ rpm.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute no-load angular speed:

$$\omega_{NL} = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \text{ rad/s} \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** At no-load, $I_a \approx$ small (negligible), so $E_b \approx V$. The machine constant:

$$K_a = \frac{V}{\omega_{NL}} = \frac{220}{157.08} \approx 1.40 \text{ V·s/rad}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** At rated load, $I_a = 100$ A:

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 220 - 100 \times 0.5 = 170 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Developed electromagnetic torque:

$$T_{rated} = K_a I_a = 1.40 \times 100 = 140 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Speed at rated load:

$$\omega_{rated} = \frac{E_b}{K_a} = \frac{170}{1.40} \approx 121.43 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$N_{rated} = \frac{121.43 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 1160 \text{ rpm}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Speed drops from 1500 rpm to 1160 rpm — a 22.7% reduction under full load, which is the well-known "speed regulation" property of a DC shunt motor.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $T_L \propto \omega^2$. At $\omega_1 = 1450$ rpm, $T_{L1} = 30$ N·m.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert speeds to rad/s:

$$\omega_1 = \frac{2\pi \times 1450}{60} \approx 151.84 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \omega_2 = \frac{2\pi \times 2900}{60} \approx 303.69 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the constant $K_2$:

$$K_2 = \frac{T_{L1}}{\omega_1^2} = \frac{30}{(151.84)^2} \approx 1.301 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·m·s²/rad²}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** New load torque at 2900 rpm:

$$T_{L2} = K_2 \omega_2^2 = 1.301 \times 10^{-3} \times (303.69)^2 \approx 120 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Old power:

$$P_1 = T_{L1} \cdot \omega_1 = 30 \times 151.84 \approx 4555 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** New power:

$$P_2 = T_{L2} \cdot \omega_2 = 120 \times 303.69 \approx 36443 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark — Conclusion):** Power ratio:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1}\right)^3 = (2)^3 = 8$$

**Engineering Insight:** This is the famous **Fan Affinity Law** — doubling the speed increases power demand by 8×. This is why VFDs are so effective in fan/pump energy saving.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B, Q-A):**
> - Students often write the power ratio as $\omega^2$ instead of $\omega^3$ for a fan load — the cube rule is **specific** to $T \propto \omega^2$ loads.
> - Many forget to convert rpm → rad/s before plugging in. Always do the conversion.
> - Carry 2–3 decimal places for $K_2$ to avoid cascading rounding errors in the final answer.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`

**(a) (7 marks)** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has a Thevenin equivalent per phase of $E_{th} = 200 \text{ V}$ and $X = 0.6 \text{ Ω}$. The slip at maximum torque is $s_m = 0.1$. Compute:
1. The synchronous speed.
2. The maximum torque.
3. The starting torque (at $s = 1$).

**(b) (7 marks)** With the help of the speed-torque curve, explain why a **DC series motor** is suitable for traction applications. State the **two main hazards** of operating a series motor at no-load.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 marks**

**Given:** P = 4, f = 50 Hz, $E_{th} = 200$ V/phase, $X = 0.6$ Ω, $s_m = 0.1$.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Synchronous speed:

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Maximum torque (independent of rotor resistance):

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 E_{th}^2}{2 \omega_s X} = \frac{3 \times (200)^2}{2 \times 157.08 \times 0.6} = \frac{120000}{188.50} \approx 636.6 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Starting torque (Kloss equation at $s = 1$):

$$T_{st} = \frac{2 T_{max}}{\frac{s}{s_m} + \frac{s_m}{s}} = \frac{2 \times 636.6}{\frac{1}{0.1} + \frac{0.1}{1}} = \frac{1273.2}{10.1} \approx 126.1 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Torque ratio $T_{st}/T_{max}$:

$$\frac{T_{st}}{T_{max}} = \frac{2 s_m}{1 + s_m^2} = \frac{2 \times 0.1}{1 + 0.01} \approx 0.198$$

**Step 5 (1 mark — Conclusion):** The starting torque is only 19.8% of the maximum torque. A cage induction motor with $s_m = 0.1$ has a poor starting torque; this is why rotor-resistance (slip-ring) or soft-starter methods are used.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** The series motor speed-torque characteristic is **inverse square** at high speed:

$$T_m \approx \frac{V^2}{K k_f \omega^2}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Traction suitability — three key points:
- **High starting torque:** At start (ω → 0), $T \to \infty$ (limited by resistance and saturation), giving the "kick" needed to accelerate a heavy train.
- **Natural torque match with vehicle load:** A vehicle on a road demands torque proportional to speed (rolling friction) and speed² (aerodynamic drag), both of which the series motor characteristic covers.
- **Overload capability:** A series motor can carry 2–3× rated torque for short durations (railway starting).

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** **Hazard 1 — Runaway at no-load:** With no mechanical load, the only opposition is friction. The motor accelerates to dangerously high speeds where centrifugal forces destroy the windings.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** **Hazard 2 — Field saturation and commutation:** At very high speeds, the field current is small, flux is weak, and the back-EMF is small. The current becomes excessive, commutation fails, and severe sparking or flashover occurs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B, Q-B):**
> - When asked for a **qualitative explanation**, do not substitute formulas. Sketch the curve, label axes, and discuss slopes — this is what examiners reward.
> - Mentioning the **"Belting" practice** (a rope or chain from the load to the motor to prevent runaway) is a classic KTU expected answer.
> - Don't confuse **"no-load"** with **"light load"**. A series motor is *safe* under light load (e.g., 25–30% rated), but absolute no-load is destructive.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Mechanical Characteristics):**

- **Equation of motion:** $T_m - T_L = J\dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ — memorize the **three regimes** (accelerating, steady-state, decelerating).
- **Generalized load:** $T_L = T_{L0} + B\omega + K\omega^2$ — know the units of $B$ (N·m·s/rad) and $K$ (N·m·s²/rad²).
- **Constant torque load** → $P_L \propto \omega$. Example: hoists, conveyors.
- **Linear torque load** → $P_L \propto \omega^2$. Example: generator-resistive load.
- **Quadratic (fan) load** → $P_L \propto \omega^3$. Example: centrifugal pumps, blowers. **Cube rule = affinity law.**
- **Inverse (constant power) load** → $T_L = P/\omega$. Example: lathe rough-cutting.
- **Active loads** (PE and KE) → can drive the motor (regenerative braking).
- **DC Shunt motor:** Linear characteristic, $T_m = T_{st}\left(1 - \omega/\omega_{NL}\right)$, **stable** for constant torque loads.
- **DC Series motor:** Hyperbolic $T \propto 1/\omega^2$, **never** to be run at no-load, ideal for traction and fan loads.
- **DC Compound (cumulative):** Between shunt and series; used in elevators and punches.
- **Induction motor:** Kloss equation $T = \dfrac{2T_{max}}{s/s_m + s_m/s}$. **$T_{max}$ is independent of rotor resistance**; $s_m \propto R_2'$.
- **Synchronous motor:** Vertical line at $\omega_s = 2\pi f / P$, used where **constant speed** is paramount.
- **Stability criterion:** $\dfrac{dT_m}{d\omega} < \dfrac{dT_L}{d\omega}$ at the operating point.
- **Power flow** in steady state: $P_{elec} = P_{mech} + P_{losses}$. The T-ω curve gives mechanical power as the **area under the curve** at the operating point.
- **Fan Affinity Law (CUBE rule):** Doubling the fan speed **multiplies power by 8** — vital for VFD energy savings.
- **Polarity of load torque:** Sign convention — torque **opposing** motion is positive load torque (Newton's 3rd law context for drives).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
