The shunt field is shown connected from F to the negative rail (NOT through the NVR coil). The starting resistance, studs, handle, and OLRC remain as in the 3-point starter.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the working of a star-delta starter with a neat diagram. State its merits and demerits.**

#### Model Solution:

**1. Concept:**
A star-delta starter is a **reduced-voltage starting method** for 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motors that are designed to run normally in **delta connection**. During starting, the stator windings are reconnected in **star**, so each phase receives **V_L / √3** instead of V_L.

**2. Circuit Components:**
- **Main Contactor K₁:** Connects the 3-phase supply to the motor terminals (always closed during operation).
- **Star Contactor K₂:** Shorts the three "tail" terminals (W₂, U₂, V₂) together to form the star point.
- **Delta Contactor K₃:** Connects the tail terminals to the line side, forming the delta connection.
- **Timer Relay T:** Pre-set to switch from star to delta after a defined acceleration time (typically 5 to 30 seconds).
- **Overload Relay OLR:** For protection against sustained overcurrent.

**3. Sequence of Operation:**
- **Step 1:** Press START → K₁ energizes and holds via its own aux contact. Supply reaches the motor terminals.
- **Step 2:** K₂ also energizes (interlocked with K₃ to prevent simultaneous closure). Star connection is established. Motor accelerates under reduced voltage.
- **Step 3:** Timer T starts counting. After the preset time, T's NC contact opens → K₂ de-energizes (star point opens).
- **Step 4:** T's NO contact closes → K₃ energizes → delta connection established → motor runs at full voltage.
- **Step 5:** To stop, press STOP → K₁ de-energizes → K₃ de-energizes → motor coasts to rest.

**4. Merits:**
- **Cheap and simple** — no autotransformer, no resistor bank.
- **Starting current reduced to 1/3** of the DOL value → less voltage dip in the supply.
- **Starting torque also reduced to 1/3** — suitable for lightly loaded starting.
- **No heat dissipation** in external resistors (the motor itself handles the lower energy).
- **High reliability** — proven technology, used in millions of industrial installations.

**5. Demerits:**
- **Reduced starting torque (1/3)** — cannot start against a heavy load (e.g., fully loaded compressor).
- **Open transition causes a current surge** during the star-to-delta switchover (closed-transition type is more expensive).
- **Requires 6 cable ends** to be brought out from the motor (all three phase ends must be accessible).
- **Not suitable for high-inertia loads** that require a long acceleration time.
- **Not suitable for motors with a star-connected design** (only delta-design motors can be used).

**6. Typical Applications:**
- **Centrifugal pumps, fans, blowers, compressors (unloaded), conveyor belts (lightly loaded), mixers, agitators** — typically in the 5 kW to 30 kW range.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A 3-phase induction motor has a starting current of 5 times the full-load current and a starting torque of 1.5 times the full-load torque when started direct-on-line. Calculate the starting current and starting torque when started using a star-delta starter. If the full-load current is 40 A and full-load torque is 200 N·m, find the new starting values.**

#### Model Solution:

**Given:**
- DOL starting current = 5 × I_FL
- DOL starting torque = 1.5 × T_FL
- I_FL = 40 A
- T_FL = 200 N·m

**Step 1: DOL Starting Current**
$$I_{start,DOL} = 5 \times 40 = 200 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: DOL Starting Torque**
$$T_{start,DOL} = 1.5 \times 200 = 300 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3: Star-Delta Starting Current**
Using the derived relation $I_{start,star} = I_{start,DOL} / 3$:

$$I_{start,star} = \frac{200}{3} = 66.67 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4: Star-Delta Starting Torque**
Using the derived relation $T_{start,star} = T_{start,DOL} / 3$:

$$T_{start,star} = \frac{300}{3} = 100 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5: Comparative Table**

| Quantity | DOL Starting | Star-Delta Starting | Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|
| Starting current | 200 A | 66.67 A | 1/3 |
| Starting torque | 300 N·m | 100 N·m | 1/3 |

**Step 6: Conclusion / Engineering Interpretation**
The starting current is reduced from 200 A to 66.67 A (a saving of **133.33 A**), which is a major relief for the supply network. However, the starting torque is reduced to **100 N·m**, which is **only 50% of the full-load torque (200 N·m)**. Hence this motor using a star-delta starter **cannot start against a full-load starting torque** — it can only start against loads requiring ≤ 100 N·m. This confirms the application domain of star-delta starting to **lightly loaded industrial drives**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistakes Students Make:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the √3 factor** — Many students incorrectly write $I_{star} = I_{DOL} / \sqrt{3}$, but the correct ratio is **1/3 (not 1/√3)**. The 1/√3 factor appears in **phase current vs line current** in delta connection, but the final **line current ratio is 1/3** because in delta $I_{line} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{phase}$.
>
> 2. **Confusing phase voltage with line voltage** — In star, $V_{phase} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$, NOT $V_{phase} = V_L$.
>
> 3. **Saying "starting torque is 1/√3 times DOL"** — Torque varies as $V^2$, so the ratio is $(1/\sqrt{3})^2 = 1/3$. This is a **highly common mistake** and costs 2-3 marks.
>
> 4. **Not drawing the interlock between K2 and K3** — A star-delta starter MUST have an electrical interlock between the star and delta contactors to prevent a **dead short circuit across the supply** (this is a serious safety hazard). Examiners specifically look for this.
>
> 5. **In 3-point starter diagrams, omitting the NVR coil connection** — The NVR coil is in series with the shunt field, and this is the **key distinguishing feature** from the 4-point starter. Marks are lost if this is not clearly shown.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to mention "nuisance tripping"** as the limitation of the 3-point starter — This is a direct syllabus question and is often the first thing the examiner looks for.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Preparation)**

### 🔹 DC Motor Starters
- ⭐ **Necessity:** Starting current without starter = V/R_a → 5-7× rated. MUST be limited.
- ⭐ **3-Point Starter:** NVR coil in series with shunt field → nuisance trips on field weakening.
- ⭐ **4-Point Starter:** NVR coil through R_NVR between L and N → decoupled from field.
- ⭐ **Drum Controller:** For series motors in traction/crane applications. Functions: starting, reversing, braking.
- ⭐ **NVR:** Spring returns handle to OFF if supply fails → no-volt protection.
- ⭐ **OLRC:** Releases handle on overcurrent → overload protection.

### 🔹 Star-Delta Starter
- ⭐ **$I_{L,star} = I_{L,DOL} / 3$** — Line current reduced to 1/3.
- ⭐ **$T_{start,star} = T_{start,DOL} / 3$** — Starting torque reduced to 1/3.
- ⭐ **Reason:** $T \propto V^2$ and $V_{phase,star} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$, so $T$ scales as $1/3$.
- ⭐ **3 contactors** + 1 timer: K1 (main), K2 (star), K3 (delta), T (timer).
- ⭐ **Interlock** between K2 and K3 is MANDATORY (prevents short circuit).
- ⭐ **Application:** Lightly loaded drives (fans, blowers, pumps).
- ⭐ **NOT suitable for:** Heavy starting loads, star-wound motors, high-inertia drives.
- ⭐ **Open transition vs Closed transition:** Open = simple, has current surge; Closed = smooth, needs extra resistor.

### 🔹 Key Formulae to Memorize
- ⭐ $I_{a,start} = V / R_a$ (DC motor)
- ⭐ $N_{sync} = 120f / P$ (Induction motor)
- ⭐ $V_{ph,star} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$
- ⭐ $T \propto V^2$

### 🔹 Common Numerical Answers
- ⭐ Star-delta starting current = 1/3 of DOL
- ⭐ Star-delta starting torque = 1/3 of DOL
- ⭐ Star-delta starting current/torque ratio is **ALWAYS 1/3** for BOTH quantities

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Types of D.C Motor Starters, Typical Control Circuits for Shunt and Series Motors, Star-Delta Starters. Best of luck for your KTU 2024 ESE preparation!**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Auto-Transformer Starters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Auto-Transformer Starters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Auto-Transformer Starter** is a reduced-voltage starting method used for three-phase squirrel-cage induction motors in which a step-down **auto-transformer** is temporarily inserted in series between the supply lines and the motor stator terminals during the starting period. The auto-transformer has **multiple fixed tappings** (commonly at **50 %, 65 %, and 80 %** of the line voltage) that allow the applied stator voltage to be reduced to a desired fraction `x` of the rated supply voltage, thereby limiting the magnitude of the inrush current drawn from the mains during motor acceleration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> An auto-transformer starter is an electrical starter that uses a multi-tapped autotransformer to apply a reduced voltage to the motor terminals at start, and then disconnects the transformer and connects the motor directly to the full supply voltage once the motor has accelerated to a safe operating speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification under KTU Module 3 (Starting Methods):**
> 1. Direct-On-Line (DOL) Starter
> 2. Stator Resistance / Reactor Starter
> 3. **Auto-Transformer Starter** ← (Current Topic)
> 4. Star-Delta Starter
> 5. Rotor Resistance Starter (for Slip-Ring motors)
> 6. Soft Starters (Modern Semiconductor-based)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a high-pressure water pipeline feeding a fire-hose. If you open the valve fully, the hose jerks violently and is hard to hold. Instead, you partially close a **flow-control valve** to let only a fraction of the water through at first, allowing the hose to fill and stabilize. Once the flow is steady, you **fully open** the main valve and remove the control valve.

In this analogy:
- The **water pipeline** = three-phase AC mains.
- The **fire-hose** = induction motor stator.
- The **flow-control valve** = multi-tapped auto-transformer.
- **Partially closed position** = reduced tap (e.g., 50 % tap).
- **Fully open position** = direct-on-line (run contactor closes, transformer is isolated).

So the auto-transformer acts as a **"voltage regulator"** that gently feeds the motor until it picks up speed, after which the motor is connected directly to the mains.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Values (must memorize):**
> - Standard Tappings: **50 %, 65 %, 80 %** of supply voltage.
> - Typical motor starting current (DOL): **5 to 7 times** full-load current.
> - Auto-transformer is **short-time rated** (typically 15–30 s duty), not for continuous use.

---

## 1.3 Why Reduced-Voltage Starting is Needed

When an induction motor is started **Direct-On-Line (DOL)**, the following problems occur:

| Problem | Consequence |
|---|---|
| Huge inrush current (5–7 × FLC) | Voltage dip in supply, affecting other loads |
| Mechanical shock to shaft & coupling | Reduced life of bearings, belts, gears |
| High starting torque (typically 1.5–2.5 × T_fl) | Damage to conveyor belts, gear trains, pumps |
| Utility penalty on poor power factor | Loss of revenue / contract violation |

The auto-transformer starter addresses **all four** issues simultaneously by reducing the applied voltage during the transient starting interval.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Construction & Operating Sequence

A three-phase auto-transformer starter typically uses **three contactors** and **one three-phase auto-transformer** with star-connected windings having multiple tappings.

### Components:
1. **Contactor S (Start):** Connects the supply to the primary of the auto-transformer.
2. **Contactor N (Neutral / Star-point):** Closes the star-point of the auto-transformer windings to form a closed magnetic path.
3. **Contactor M (Main / Run):** Bypasses the auto-transformer and connects the motor directly to the supply.
4. **Auto-transformer winding** with tappings at desired fractions `x` of the line voltage.

### Operating Sequence (Step-by-Step):

| Step | Action | Contactor State | Motor Voltage |
|------|--------|-----------------|---------------|
| **1 (OFF state)** | All contactors open | S = OFF, N = OFF, M = OFF | 0 V (motor disconnected) |
| **2 (Start position)** | S and N close; M open | S = ON, N = ON, M = OFF | `x·V_L` (reduced) |
| **3 (Transition)** | N opens first (autotransformer disconnected) | S = ON, N = OFF, M = OFF | Brief open-circuit, motor coasting |
| **4 (Run position)** | M closes; S and N open | S = OFF, N = OFF, M = ON | `V_L` (full voltage) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The transition from Step 2 → Step 4 is critical. **Contactor N must open *before* M closes** to prevent the auto-transformer from being short-circuited by the main contactor, which would cause massive circulating currents. This interlock is implemented using **mechanical and electrical interlocks** between contactors.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundation (The "Why" Behind the Formulas)

Let:
- `V_L` = Line voltage of the 3-phase supply (V).
- `I_sc` = Short-circuit (blocked-rotor) current of the motor at **full voltage** (A).
- `T_sc` = Starting torque of the motor at **full voltage** (N·m).
- `x` = **Tap ratio** = (Reduced voltage applied to motor) / (Full line voltage). So `0 < x < 1`.

### Voltage Relationship:
The auto-transformer steps down the supply voltage to the motor by the factor `x`:

$$
V_m = x \cdot V_L
$$

### Current Relationship (Key Insight):
Because the auto-transformer is a magnetic device, the **current on the motor side is related to the current on the supply side by the turns ratio**. By applying transformer action (Ampere-turns balance), the line current drawn from the supply is **NOT** `x · I_sc`, but rather `x² · I_sc`.

> [!NOTE]
> This `x²` relationship is the **most tested concept** in KTU exams on auto-transformer starters. Many students mistakenly write `x · I_sc` for line current, which costs full marks.

### Torque Relationship:
Since starting torque is proportional to the **square of the applied voltage**:

$$
T_{st,\text{reduced}} = x^2 \cdot T_{sc}
$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|----------|--------|---------|------|---------|
| 1 | Tap ratio | $x$ | $x = V_m / V_L$ | dimensionless | Typical: 0.50, 0.65, 0.80 |
| 2 | Motor voltage at start | $V_m$ | $V_m = x \cdot V_L$ | Volts (V) | Reduced applied voltage |
| 3 | Motor current at start | $I_m$ | $I_m = x \cdot I_{sc}$ | Amperes (A) | Current through stator windings |
| 4 | **Line current from supply** | $I_L$ | $I_L = x^2 \cdot I_{sc}$ | Amperes (A) | ⭐ Most important formula |
| 5 | Starting torque (reduced) | $T_{st}$ | $T_{st} = x^2 \cdot T_{sc}$ | N·m | Proportional to $V^2$ |
| 6 | Ratio of line currents (Auto vs DOL) | $I_{L,\text{auto}} / I_{L,\text{DOL}}$ | $x^2$ | dimensionless | Performance metric |
| 7 | Ratio of torques (Auto vs DOL) | $T_{\text{auto}} / T_{\text{DOL}}$ | $x^2$ | dimensionless | Same as current ratio |
| 8 | Power drawn from supply | $P_{\text{supply}}$ | $\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | Watts (W) | Includes reduced V *and* reduced I |
| 9 | Auto-transformer kVA rating | $S_{\text{AT}}$ | $x \cdot S_{\text{motor,sc}}$ | kVA | Short-time rating only |
| 10 | Torque loss factor | $\eta_T$ | $1 - x^2$ | dimensionless | Fraction of starting torque lost |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** Both *line current reduction* and *starting torque reduction* follow the **same `x²` law**. This is unique to the auto-transformer starter — in **star-delta** starting, current is reduced by `1/√3` but torque is reduced by `1/3`.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Why Auto-Transformer Starter is Preferred |
|---|---|
| **Large centrifugal pumps** | Smooth, jolt-free acceleration protects impeller and seal |
| **Industrial fans and blowers** | High load inertia requires gradual speed buildup |
| **Compressors (refrigeration, air)** | Prevents pressure surges in the discharge line |
| **Conveyor belts (mining, cement)** | Avoids belt slippage and material spillage |
| **Crushers and mills** | Cushioned start reduces gear-box stress |
| **High-inertia flywheels** | Allows motor to develop speed before applying full load |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 scheme, this starter is recommended for motors rated **above 5 HP (≈ 3.7 kW)** where DOL starting is not permitted by electricity board regulations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Line-Current Relationship

This is the **most critical derivation** for KTU 2024 examinations. We will derive why the line current drawn from the supply is `x² · I_sc` and not `x · I_sc`.

### Step 1: Setup the Equivalent Circuit

Consider one phase of the three-phase auto-transformer. Let the primary (supply side) have `N_1` turns and the secondary (motor side) have `N_2` turns, with the tap chosen such that:

$$
\frac{N_2}{N_1} = x
$$

### Step 2: Apply Voltage Transformation

The voltage on the motor side (secondary) is `x` times the supply voltage (primary):

$$
V_2 = \frac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot V_1 = x \cdot V_L
$$

### Step 3: Stator Current at Reduced Voltage

When a reduced voltage of `V_2 = x · V_L` is applied to the motor, the rotor is initially at standstill (slip `s = 1`). The motor impedance `Z` per phase is the same as at full voltage. So by Ohm's law, the current flowing **into the motor stator terminal** is:

$$
I_2 = \frac{V_2}{Z} = \frac{x \cdot V_L}{Z} = x \cdot \frac{V_L}{Z} = x \cdot I_{sc}
$$

This is the current on the **secondary (motor) side**.

### Step 4: Apply Current Transformation (Ampere-Turns Balance)

For an ideal transformer, the MMF balance requires:

$$
N_1 \cdot I_1 = N_2 \cdot I_2
$$

Therefore, the current on the **primary (supply) side** is:

$$
I_1 = \frac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot I_2 = x \cdot I_2
$$

### Step 5: Substitute to Get the Line Current

Substituting the expression for `I_2` from Step 3:

$$
I_1 = x \cdot I_2 = x \cdot (x \cdot I_{sc})
$$

$$
\boxed{I_L = x^2 \cdot I_{sc}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Line-by-line Interpretation:**
> - `V_2 = x · V_L` → Voltage to motor is reduced linearly by `x`.
> - `I_2 = x · I_sc` → Motor winding current is reduced linearly by `x`.
> - `I_1 = x · I_2 = x² · I_sc` → **Supply line current is reduced quadratically by `x²`.**

### Step 6: Derive the Torque Relationship

The torque developed by an induction motor at standstill is given by:

$$
T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V^2 \cdot R_2 / s}{(R_2/s)^2 + (X_2)^2}
$$

At `s = 1` (starting condition), `R_2/s = R_2`. So:

$$
T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}
$$

Therefore, torque is **proportional to the square of the applied voltage**:

$$
T_{st} \propto V^2
$$

When the voltage is reduced from `V_L` to `x · V_L`, the starting torque becomes:

$$
\boxed{T_{st,\text{auto}} = x^2 \cdot T_{st,\text{DOL}}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

**Problem:**
A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 15 kW squirrel-cage induction motor has a full-load current of 28 A, a DOL starting current of 6 × FLC, and a DOL starting torque of 2 × full-load torque. It is started using an auto-transformer starter with a **65 % tap**.

Calculate:
- (i) The starting current drawn from the supply.
- (ii) The starting torque developed.
- (iii) The percentage reduction in supply current and starting torque compared to DOL.

### Given Data

$$
V_L = 400 \text{ V}, \quad I_{FL} = 28 \text{ A}, \quad I_{sc} = 6 \times 28 = 168 \text{ A}, \quad T_{sc} = 2 \times T_{FL}, \quad x = 0.65
$$

### (i) Starting Current from Supply

$$
I_L = x^2 \cdot I_{sc} = (0.65)^2 \cdot 168
$$

$$
I_L = 0.4225 \cdot 168 = 70.98 \text{ A}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_L \approx 71 \text{ A}}
$$

### (ii) Starting Torque Developed

$$
T_{st} = x^2 \cdot T_{sc} = (0.65)^2 \cdot 2 \cdot T_{FL}
$$

$$
T_{st} = 0.4225 \cdot 2 \cdot T_{FL} = 0.845 \cdot T_{FL}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{st} \approx 0.845 \, T_{FL}}
$$

### (iii) Percentage Reduction

**Current reduction:**

$$
\text{Reduction} = \frac{I_{sc} - I_L}{I_{sc}} \times 100 = \frac{168 - 71}{168} \times 100 = 57.74 \, \% \quad \text{(reduction)}
$$

**Torque reduction:**

$$
\text{Reduction} = \frac{T_{sc} - T_{st}}{T_{sc}} \times 100 = \frac{2 \, T_{FL} - 0.845 \, T_{FL}}{2 \, T_{FL}} \times 100 = 57.75 \, \% \quad \text{(reduction)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Both current and torque are reduced by the **same percentage** (57.75 % in this case). This confirms the `x²` law: an auto-transformer starter is a "**balanced trade-off**" — it sacrifices starting torque to gain a corresponding reduction in starting current.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison with Other Starters

This table is **frequently asked** in KTU exams as a 2-mark or 7-mark comparison question.

| Parameter | DOL | Stator Reactor | **Auto-Transformer** | Star-Delta |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage applied to motor | $V_L$ | $x \cdot V_L$ | $x \cdot V_L$ | $V_L / \sqrt{3}$ |
| Motor current | $I_{sc}$ | $x \cdot I_{sc}$ | $x \cdot I_{sc}$ | $I_{sc}/\sqrt{3}$ |
| **Line current from supply** | $I_{sc}$ | $x \cdot I_{sc}$ | $x^2 \cdot I_{sc}$ | $I_{sc}/\sqrt{3}$ |
| **Starting torque** | $T_{sc}$ | $x^2 \cdot T_{sc}$ | $x^2 \cdot T_{sc}$ | $T_{sc}/3$ |
| Number of contactors | 1 | 2 | 3 | 3 |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | High | Medium |
| Smoothness of start | Poor (jerky) | Good | **Best** | Acceptable |
| Tapping flexibility | None | Yes (continuous) | **Yes (discrete taps)** | Fixed |
| Applicable motor type | Any (≤ 5 HP) | Any | Squirrel-cage | Squirrel-cage (6 terminals) |
| Power loss during start | 0 | $I^2R$ in reactor | Minimal (in transformer) | 0 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power-Circuit Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the functional architecture of a typical 3-contactor auto-transformer starter. It maps the **electrical connections** between the supply, the three contactors (S, N, M), the auto-transformer, and the induction motor.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Supply["THREE PHASE SUPPLY R Y B 400 V 50 Hz"]

    subgraph ContactorBank["CONTACTOR BANK WITH MECHANICAL INTERLOCKS"]
        S_CONT["CONTACTOR S START"]
        N_CONT["CONTACTOR N NEUTRAL STAR POINT"]
        M_CONT["CONTACTOR M MAIN RUN"]
    end

    subgraph AutoTx["THREE PHASE AUTO TRANSFORMER SHORT TIME RATED"]
        Tap50["TAP 50 PERCENT"]
        Tap65["TAP 65 PERCENT"]
        Tap80["TAP 80 PERCENT"]
        Common["COMMON PRIMARY TERMINAL"]
    end

    Motor["THREE PHASE SQUIRREL CAGE INDUCTION MOTOR"]

    Supply -->|R Y B Lines| S_CONT
    S_CONT -->|R Y B to Primary| Common
    Common --> Tap50
    Common --> Tap65
    Common --> Tap80
    Tap50 --> N_CONT
    Tap65 --> N_CONT
    Tap80 --> N_CONT
    N_CONT -->|Closes Star Point of AT| StarReturn["STAR POINT FORMED"]
    Tap50 -->|Reduced Voltage| Motor
    Tap65 -->|Reduced Voltage| Motor
    Tap80 -->|Reduced Voltage| Motor
    StarReturn -.->|Auto TX active| Motor
    Supply -.->|Bypass Path| M_CONT
    M_CONT -->|Full Voltage Run| Motor
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:**
> - The **solid arrows** show the path when the starter is in **Start Position** (Contactors S and N closed, M open).
> - The **dashed arrows** show the path when the starter transitions to **Run Position** (Contactor M closed, S and N open).
> - The **interlocks** between S, N, and M are not shown in the power circuit but exist in the control circuit to prevent simultaneous closure of S+N with M.

---

## 4.2 State-Transition Diagram (Operational Logic)

The Mermaid state diagram below captures the **timed state transitions** of the three contactors during the entire starting sequence.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OFF_STATE
    OFF_STATE --> START_PRESSED : Operator Pushes Start Button
    START_PRESSED --> START_POSITION : Contactor S closes AND Contactor N closes
    START_POSITION --> TRANSITION : Timer T1 expires typically 5 to 10 seconds
    TRANSITION --> RUN_POSITION : Contactor N opens first AND Contactor M closes
    RUN_POSITION --> OFF_STATE : Operator Pushes Stop Button
    TRANSITION --> START_POSITION : Contactor M fails to close
    OFF_STATE --> [*]
```

**State Explanations:**

| State | Contactor S | Contactor N | Contactor M | Motor Voltage |
|-------|-------------|-------------|-------------|---------------|
| **OFF_STATE** | OFF | OFF | OFF | Disconnected |
| **START_POSITION** | ON | ON | OFF | `x · V_L` (reduced) |
| **TRANSITION** | ON | OFF | OFF | Open circuit, motor coasts |
| **RUN_POSITION** | OFF | OFF | ON | `V_L` (full voltage) |

---

## 4.3 Tap-Ratio Decision Tree

Use this decision tree to select the correct auto-transformer tap based on the load type:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Identify Load Type"] --> B{"Is Load High Inertia? e.g. Flywheel Centrifuge"}
    B -->|Yes| C["Use 80 Percent Tap Higher Voltage More Torque"]
    B -->|No| D{"Is Load Medium Inertia? e.g. Pump Fan"}
    D -->|Yes| E["Use 65 Percent Tap Balanced Compromise"]
    D -->|No| F["Use 50 Percent Tap Minimum Voltage Minimum Current"]
    C --> G["Verify Starting Torque > Load Torque at all speeds"]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{"Torque Sufficient?"}
    H -->|Yes| I["Proceed With Selected Tap"]
    H -->|No| J["Increase to Next Higher Tap"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Hint:** The 65 % tap is the most commonly used in industrial practice because it provides a balanced trade-off — current reduced to about 42 % of DOL, and torque also reduced to about 42 % of DOL, while still being sufficient for most general-purpose loads.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any three advantages of an auto-transformer starter over a star-delta starter for starting squirrel-cage induction motors.**

**Model Answer (3 points × 1 mark each):**

1. **Tapping flexibility:** Auto-transformer starters offer multiple discrete tappings (50 %, 65 %, 80 %), allowing the user to select the most suitable voltage level for a given load. Star-delta starters have a **fixed** voltage reduction of `1/√3` (≈ 57.7 %).

2. **Superior torque-to-current ratio:** In an auto-transformer starter, both starting current and starting torque are reduced by the same factor `x²`. In a star-delta starter, the current is reduced by `1/√3` (≈ 57.7 %) but the torque is reduced by `1/3` (≈ 66.7 %). Hence, the auto-transformer retains **more torque per ampere** of starting current.

3. **Smoother starting and lower losses:** The auto-transformer has very low internal losses compared to the series resistors/reactors used in other reduced-voltage starters. The acceleration is smoother, and the supply disturbance (voltage dip) is minimal.

4. *(Bonus point)* No need for a 6-terminal motor; can be used with standard 3-terminal squirrel-cage motors.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Why is the line current drawn from the supply during auto-transformer starting equal to `x² · I_sc` and not `x · I_sc`? Explain briefly.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

When the auto-transformer is at tap ratio `x`, the voltage applied to the motor terminals is `x · V_L`. The motor current at this reduced voltage is `I_motor = x · I_sc` (by Ohm's law).

However, the auto-transformer is a **magnetic coupling device**. By the principle of **Ampere-turns balance** in a transformer, the primary (supply side) current is related to the secondary (motor side) current by the turns ratio:

$$
I_{supply} = x \cdot I_{motor} = x \cdot (x \cdot I_{sc}) = x^2 \cdot I_{sc}
$$

This **double-step reduction** (`x` in voltage → `x` in motor current → `x²` in supply current) is the fundamental reason for the quadratic relationship. The auto-transformer thus acts as a "current magnifier" in the reverse direction — small motor current corresponds to an even smaller supply current.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question B — Choice A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a three-phase auto-transformer starter for a squirrel-cage induction motor. Derive the expressions for starting current and starting torque in terms of the tap ratio `x`. A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 10 HP (≈ 7.5 kW) squirrel-cage induction motor with full-load current 22 A, DOL starting current 6 × FLC, and DOL starting torque 2.5 × full-load torque, is started using an auto-transformer with 65 % tap. Calculate: (a) the starting current drawn from the supply, and (b) the starting torque developed.** **(14 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### **Part (a): Working Principle and Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Sketch (3 marks):**
Draw a three-line diagram showing the three-phase supply, three contactors S (start), N (neutral), and M (main), a star-connected three-phase auto-transformer with tappings, and the motor terminals. Show the contact positions in both start and run modes. (Refer to the Block Diagram in SECTION 4.1.)

**Operating Principle (2 marks):**
- **Start mode:** When the start button is pressed, contactors S and N close. The supply feeds the primary of the auto-transformer; the motor receives reduced voltage `x · V_L` from the tap.
- **Run mode:** After a preset time delay (5–10 s), contactor N opens (auto-transformer is isolated), and then contactor M closes, connecting the motor directly to the full supply voltage `V_L`.

**Derivation of starting current and torque (2 marks):**

$$
V_m = x \cdot V_L
$$

$$
I_m = x \cdot I_{sc} \quad \text{(by Ohm's law on motor)}
$$

$$
I_L = x \cdot I_m = x^2 \cdot I_{sc} \quad \text{(by transformer current relation)}
$$

$$
T_{st} = x^2 \cdot T_{sc} \quad \text{(since } T \propto V^2\text{)}
$$

### **Part (b): Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $V_L = 400$ V
- $I_{FL} = 22$ A
- $I_{sc} = 6 \times 22 = 132$ A
- $T_{sc} = 2.5 \times T_{FL}$
- $x = 0.65$

**(a) Starting current from supply (3.5 marks):**

$$
I_L = x^2 \cdot I_{sc} = (0.65)^2 \cdot 132
$$

$$
I_L = 0.4225 \cdot 132 = 55.77 \text{ A}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_L \approx 55.77 \text{ A}}
$$

**(b) Starting torque developed (3.5 marks):**

$$
T_{st} = x^2 \cdot T_{sc} = (0.65)^2 \cdot 2.5 \cdot T_{FL}
$$

$$
T_{st} = 0.4225 \cdot 2.5 \cdot T_{FL} = 1.056 \cdot T_{FL}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{st} \approx 1.056 \, T_{FL}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Wrong formula for line current:** Writing `I_L = x · I_sc` instead of `x² · I_sc` → **Lose 2 marks**.
> 2. **Missing interlock explanation:** Failing to mention that contactor N must open *before* M closes → **Lose 1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing motor current with line current:** Computing the current through the motor winding (which is `x · I_sc`) and reporting it as "supply current" → **Lose 1 mark**.
> 4. **Not labeling the sketch:** Unlabeled contactors, transformer, and motor terminals → **Lose 1.5 marks** (3-mark sketch).

---

### **Question B — Choice B (Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: (a) Compare the auto-transformer starter with the star-delta starter in terms of starting current, starting torque, number of contactors, and tapping flexibility. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz, 20 HP (≈ 15 kW) squirrel-cage induction motor has a full-load current of 26 A, DOL starting current of 5.5 × FLC, and DOL starting torque of 2 × full-load torque. The motor is to be started using an auto-transformer with 80 % tap. Calculate: (i) the starting current drawn from the supply, (ii) the starting torque, and (iii) comment on whether the motor will start successfully if the load torque at standstill is 1.2 × full-load torque.** **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### **Part (a): Comparison Table (7 Marks)**

| Parameter | Auto-Transformer Starter | Star-Delta Starter |
|---|---|---|
| Voltage applied to motor at start | $x \cdot V_L$ (variable via tap) | $V_L / \sqrt{3} \approx 0.577 \, V_L$ (fixed) |
| Motor current at start | $x \cdot I_{sc}$ | $I_{sc} / \sqrt{3}$ |
| **Line current from supply** | $x^2 \cdot I_{sc}$ | $I_{sc} / \sqrt{3}$ |
| **Starting torque** | $x^2 \cdot T_{sc}$ | $T_{sc} / 3$ |
| Number of contactors | 3 (S, N, M) | 3 (Main, Star, Delta) |
| Tapping flexibility | Yes (50, 65, 80 %) | No (fixed) |
| Need for 6-terminal motor | No | Yes |
| Cost | Higher (auto-transformer) | Lower |
| Smoothness of start | Best | Good |
| Typical rating | 5 to 200 HP | 5 to 50 HP |
| Open-circuit transition | Yes (brief) | Yes (brief) |
| Power loss during start | Negligible | Negligible |
| Power factor during start | Better | Slightly poorer |

**(1 mark for each correctly-filled row, with 7 rows for full 7 marks.)**

### **Part (b): Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $V_L = 415$ V, $I_{FL} = 26$ A
- $I_{sc} = 5.5 \times 26 = 143$ A
- $T_{sc} = 2 \times T_{FL}$
- $x = 0.80$
- $T_{\text{load,standstill}} = 1.2 \times T_{FL}$

**(i) Starting current from supply (2 marks):**

$$
I_L = x^2 \cdot I_{sc} = (0.80)^2 \cdot 143 = 0.64 \cdot 143 = 91.52 \text{ A}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_L \approx 91.52 \text{ A}}
$$

**(ii) Starting torque (2 marks):**

$$
T_{st} = x^2 \cdot T_{sc} = (0.80)^2 \cdot 2 \cdot T_{FL} = 0.64 \cdot 2 \cdot T_{FL} = 1.28 \cdot T_{FL}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{st} = 1.28 \, T_{FL}}
$$

**(iii) Will the motor start? (3 marks):**

For the motor to start successfully, the developed starting torque must be **greater than** the load torque at standstill:

$$
T_{st} > T_{\text{load,standstill}}
$$

$$
1.28 \, T_{FL} > 1.2 \, T_{FL} \quad \checkmark
$$

Since the condition is satisfied (with a margin of 0.08 $T_{FL}$), **the motor will start successfully**, but the margin is small (≈ 6.25 %). If the load torque were higher, a different tap would be needed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Choice B:**
> 1. **Skipping the comparison table:** Writing paragraphs instead of a tabular comparison → Lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Not computing the comparison in identical units:** Comparing `x²` directly to `1/3` without numerical examples → Lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Failing to comment on the start/no-start condition:** Just computing numbers without the final qualitative conclusion → **Lose 2 marks** in part (iii).
> 4. **Forgetting the margin calculation:** Reporting only "yes" or "no" without quantifying the margin → Lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Auto-Transformer Starters**

- **Definition:** Reduced-voltage starter using a multi-tapped auto-transformer in series during start; bypassed in run.

- **Standard tappings:** **50 %, 65 %, 80 %** of supply voltage (memorize these).

- **Most-tested formulas (must memorize verbatim):**
  - $V_m = x \cdot V_L$
  - $I_m = x \cdot I_{sc}$ (motor winding current)
  - $I_L = x^2 \cdot I_{sc}$ (supply line current) ← **MOST CRITICAL**
  - $T_{st} = x^2 \cdot T_{sc}$

- **Why `x²`?** Ampere-turns balance in transformer: `I_supply = x · I_motor = x · (x · I_sc)`.

- **Both current AND torque reduce by the same factor `x²`** — unique signature of auto-transformer starter (NOT true for star-delta, where torque reduces more).

- **Contactors required:** 3 (S = Start, N = Neutral/Star, M = Main/Run).

- **Interlock rule:** Contactor N must open *before* M closes (to avoid short-circuiting the auto-transformer).

- **Auto-transformer is short-time rated** (15–30 s duty); not for continuous operation.

- **Comparison edge:** Auto-transformer = best smoothness; Star-delta = cheapest; DOL = simplest but most stressful.

- **Applications:** Large pumps, fans, compressors, conveyors, crushers — typically motors above **5 HP (≈ 3.7 kW)**.

- **Advantage over star-delta:** No need for 6-terminal motor; tap selection allows optimization for any load.

- **Disadvantage:** Higher cost (requires an actual transformer); physically larger than star-delta; open-circuit transition can cause a small inrush at the moment of transfer to M.

- **Numerical trap:** In KTU problems, always check whether the **load torque at standstill** is less than the **developed starting torque** before concluding "motor will start." The condition is $T_{st} \geq T_{\text{load}}$ with a **safety margin of at least 10–15 %**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soft Starters for Induction Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Soft Starters for Induction Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Soft Starter** is a solid-state power electronic device used to control the acceleration of a three-phase **squirrel-cage induction motor** by gradually ramping up the supply voltage from an initial low value to the full rated voltage during the starting transient. It employs back-to-back connected **Thyristors (Silicon Controlled Rectifiers – SCRs)** in each phase, configured as an **AC voltage controller (AC-AC converter)**, and regulates the RMS output voltage by varying the **firing angle (α)** of the thyristors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A soft starter is a *reduced-voltage starting device* that uses *phase-angle control* of anti-parallel thyristor pairs to limit the **inrush current** and **starting torque** of an induction motor, eliminating the mechanical and electrical stresses associated with **Direct-On-Line (DOL)** starting.

### 1.2 The Problem with Direct-On-Line (DOL) Starting

When an induction motor is started directly across the line, the following adverse effects occur:

| Parameter | DOL Starting Value | Acceptable Limit |
|---|---|---|
| Starting Current | **5 to 8 times** Full-Load Current (FLC) | ≤ 2.5 × FLC |
| Starting Torque | **2 to 3 times** Full-Load Torque (FLT) | Often excessive (mechanical shock) |
| Voltage Sag | Severe dip in supply | Causes nuisance tripping |
| Mechanical Stress | High coupling shock | Damages belts, gears, shafts |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Accelerator Pedal of a Car

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a soft starter as the **"accelerator pedal"** of your car. Imagine pressing the pedal instantly to the floor — the car jerks violently, tires screech, and passengers lurch forward. Now imagine *gradually pressing* the accelerator — the car moves smoothly, and passengers feel no shock. The soft starter does exactly the same thing for an induction motor: it **ramps the voltage gradually**, just like a smooth accelerator press, ensuring jerk-free acceleration from standstill to rated speed.

### 1.4 Key Functions of a Modern Soft Starter

1. **Voltage Ramping** – Smooth rise of terminal voltage.
2. **Current Limiting** – Clamps the inrush current below a preset threshold.
3. **Soft Stop** – Gradual voltage decay, avoiding water-hammer in pumps.
4. **Energy Saving** – In light-load conditions, reduces voltage to lower losses.
5. **Protection** – Built-in overload, phase loss, and phase reversal protection.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Phase Angle Control

In a soft starter, each phase contains a pair of **anti-parallel (inverse-parallel) thyristors (T1, T2)**. During the positive half-cycle, T1 conducts after receiving a gate pulse at angle **α**; during the negative half-cycle, T2 conducts at **(π + α)**. By delaying the firing instant, the **conduction angle** is reduced, and hence the RMS output voltage falls.

The conduction of each thyristor is terminated naturally at the **current zero-crossing** of the respective half-cycle, since the induction motor is an **R-L load (lagging power factor)**.

### 2.2 Modes of Soft Starting

| Mode | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Voltage Ramp (VR)** | Linear voltage rise over a preset time (e.g., 5 s) | General-purpose fans, conveyors |
| **Current Limit (CL)** | Holds current below a fixed ceiling (e.g., 3 × FLC) | High-inertia loads (crushers, mills) |
| **Voltage Ramp + Current Limit** | Hybrid mode: ramp first, then limit | Pumps, compressors |
| **Kick Start** | Brief voltage pulse (70–80%) to break static friction, then ramp | Loaded conveyors, mixers |
| **Soft Stop** | Gradual voltage decay to zero | Pumps (avoid water hammer) |
| **Energy Save** | Auto-voltage reduction at light load | HVAC, fan applications |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Soft Starters**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$V_{o(rms)} \;=\; V_s \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left[(\pi - \alpha) \;+\; \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}$$ | RMS output voltage of single-phase AC voltage controller feeding R-load |
| 2 | $$V_{o(rms)} \;=\; V_s \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{\alpha}{\pi} - \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$$ | Simplified form for resistive load (R-load) |
| 3 | $$I_{or} \;=\; \dfrac{V_s}{Z}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left[(\pi - \alpha) - \phi \;+\; \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha - \sin 2(\alpha - \phi)}{2}\right]}$$ | RMS load current (R-L load), where Z = √(R² + ω²L²) |
| 4 | $$T_e \;=\; \dfrac{3}{\omega_s}\cdot\dfrac{V^{2} \cdot \dfrac{R_r^{\prime}}{s}}{\left(R_s + \dfrac{R_r^{\prime}}{s}\right)^{2} + (X_s + X_r^{\prime})^{2}}$$ | Electromagnetic torque of induction motor at slip s |
| 5 | $$\dfrac{T_{st}}{T_{FL}} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{V_{st}}{V_{FL}}\right)^{2} \cdot s_{FL}$$ | Starting torque ratio (proportional to voltage squared) |
| 6 | $$\dfrac{I_{st}}{I_{FL}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{st}}{V_{FL}}$$ | Starting current ratio (approximately linear with voltage) |
| 7 | $$\alpha_{\min} \;\approx\; \phi \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega L_s}{R_s}\right)$$ | Minimum firing angle (below which no conduction occurs for R-L load) |
| 8 | $$P_{\text{save}} \;\approx\; P_{\text{core,rated}}\!\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{rated}}\right)^{2}\right]$$ | Approximate core (iron) loss savings in Energy-Save mode |

> **Where:** $V_s$ = supply RMS voltage, $V_o$ = output RMS voltage, $\alpha$ = firing angle, $\phi$ = load phase angle, $R_s$ = stator resistance, $R_r^{\prime}$ = referred rotor resistance, $X_s$ = stator reactance, $X_r^{\prime}$ = referred rotor reactance, $s$ = slip, $\omega_s$ = synchronous angular speed.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

Soft starters are extensively used in:
- **Pumps** (centrifugal, submersible) — avoid water hammer.
- **Fans and Blowers** — reduce belt slippage and bearing wear.
- **Conveyor Belts** — prevent jerking and product spillage.
- **Compressors** — limit starting torque spikes.
- **Mixers and Agitators** — break static friction gradually.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — RMS Output Voltage for a Single-Phase AC Voltage Controller (R-Load)

Let the supply voltage be:
$$v_s(t) \;=\; V_m \sin(\omega t)$$

Each thyristor conducts from $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\pi$ (for the positive half-cycle) and from $(\pi + \alpha)$ to $2\pi$ (for the negative half-cycle). For a resistive load, $v_o(t) = v_s(t)$ during conduction and 0 otherwise.

**Step 1: Define the output over one full cycle (0 to 2π).**

$$v_o(\omega t) \;=\; \begin{cases} 0, & 0 \le \omega t \le \alpha \\ V_m \sin(\omega t), & \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi \\ 0, & \pi \le \omega t \le (\pi + \alpha) \\ V_m \sin(\omega t), & (\pi + \alpha) \le \omega t \le 2\pi \end{cases}$$

**Step 2: Write the RMS definition over a full period $T$ (or $2\pi$ in $\omega t$ units).**

$$V_{o(rms)}^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o^{2}(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 3: Substitute the non-zero intervals only.**

$$V_{o(rms)}^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\!\left[\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^{2} \sin^{2}(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) + \int_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi} V_m^{2} \sin^{2}(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)\right]$$

**Step 4: Use the half-wave symmetry identity** $\sin^{2}(\theta) = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{o(rms)}^{2} &= \dfrac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi}\!\left[\int_{\alpha}^{\pi}\!\dfrac{1 - \cos 2\omega t}{2}\,d(\omega t) + \int_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi}\!\dfrac{1 - \cos 2\omega t}{2}\,d(\omega t)\right] \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{V_m^{2}}{4\pi}\!\left[\left.\left(\omega t - \dfrac{\sin 2\omega t}{2}\right)\right|_{\alpha}^{\pi} + \left.\left(\omega t - \dfrac{\sin 2\omega t}{2}\right)\right|_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the limits.**

First integral:
$$\left(\pi - \dfrac{\sin 2\pi}{2}\right) - \left(\alpha - \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right) \;=\; (\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}$$

Second integral:
$$\left(2\pi - \dfrac{\sin 4\pi}{2}\right) - \left(\pi + \alpha - \dfrac{\sin 2(\pi + \alpha)}{2}\right) \;=\; (\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}$$

(Total value of $\sin$ terms is the same due to periodicity.)

**Step 6: Combine.**

$$V_{o(rms)}^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{V_m^{2}}{4\pi}\cdot 2\!\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right] \;=\; \dfrac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi}\!\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]$$

**Step 7: Convert to supply RMS** ($V_s = V_m / \sqrt{2}$, hence $V_m^{2} = 2 V_s^{2}$).

$$V_{o(rms)}^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{2V_s^{2}}{2\pi}\!\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right] \;=\; \dfrac{V_s^{2}}{\pi}\!\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]$$

**Step 8: Take the square root — the final result.**

$$\boxed{\,V_{o(rms)} \;=\; V_s \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limiting Cases:** When $\alpha = 0$, the expression under the square root equals 1, giving $V_{o(rms)} = V_s$ (full voltage, equivalent to DOL). When $\alpha \to \pi$, the term $(\pi - \alpha) \to 0$ and $\sin 2\alpha \to 0$, so $V_{o(rms)} \to 0$ (no output).

### 3.2 Derivation — Torque Variation with Reduced Voltage

The per-phase equivalent circuit of the induction motor referred to the stator gives:

$$I_{r}^{\prime} \;=\; \dfrac{V}{\left(R_s + \dfrac{R_r^{\prime}}{s}\right) + j(X_s + X_r^{\prime})}$$

**Step 1: Compute the air-gap power per phase.**

$$P_{ag} \;=\; I_{r}^{\prime 2}\cdot\dfrac{R_r^{\prime}}{s}$$

**Step 2: Compute the developed electromagnetic torque.**

$$T_e \;=\; \dfrac{P_{ag,\text{total}}}{\omega_s} \;=\; \dfrac{3}{\omega_s}\cdot\dfrac{V^{2}\cdot(R_r^{\prime}/s)}{\left(R_s + R_r^{\prime}/s\right)^{2} + (X_s + X_r^{\prime})^{2}}$$

**Step 3: Apply the reduced starting voltage** $V_{st} = k \cdot V_{FL}$, where $k < 1$.

$$\dfrac{T_{st,\text{soft}}}{T_{st,\text{DOL}}} \;=\; k^{2} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{V_{st}}{V_{FL}}\right)^{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Since torque varies as the **square** of voltage, a soft starter set at 60% voltage delivers only **36%** of the DOL starting torque. The current, however, varies **linearly** with voltage, giving ~60% of the DOL starting current. This asymmetric trade-off is the *heart* of soft-starter design.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | DOL | Star-Delta | Auto-Transformer | Soft Starter |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage Tap (Fixed) | 100% | 58% (1/√3) | 50–80% (taps) | 30–95% (continuous) |
| Starting Current (×FLC) | 5–8 | 2–3 | 2–3 | 1.5–3 |
| Starting Torque (×FLT) | 2–3 | 0.33–0.5 | 0.25–0.6 | 0.15–0.9 |
| Torque Adjustment | None | Fixed | Stepped (3–4) | Continuous |
| Open Transition | — | Yes (current surge) | Possible | Smooth (no surge) |
| Soft Stop Available | No | No | No | Yes |
| Energy Saving Mode | No | No | No | Yes |
| Cost (Relative) | 1× | 1.2× | 1.5× | 2.5–3× |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Three-Phase Soft Starter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[R SUPPLY] --> B1[ANTI PARALLEL SCR PAIR T1 T2]
    A2[Y SUPPLY] --> B2[ANTI PARALLEL SCR PAIR T3 T4]
    A3[B SUPPLY] --> B3[ANTI PARALLEL SCR PAIR T5 T6]
    B1 --> C1[MOTOR TERMINAL U]
    B2 --> C2[MOTOR TERMINAL V]
    B3 --> C3[MOTOR TERMINAL W]
    C1 --> D[INDUCTION MOTOR LOAD]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[VOLTAGE SENSOR PT]
    D --> F[CURRENT SENSOR CT]
    E --> G[MICROCONTROLLER DSP CONTROL UNIT]
    F --> G
    G --> H[GATE PULSE GENERATOR WITH OPTICAL ISOLATION]
    H --> B1
    H --> B2
    H --> B3
    G --> I[KEYPAD AND LCD HMI INTERFACE]
    G --> J[PROTECTION MODULE OVERLOAD AND PHASE LOSS]
    K[EXTERNAL COMMAND START STOP] --> G
```

### 4.2 Power Circuit Topology (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INCOMER [THREE PHASE SUPPLY]
        R[R PHASE]:::src
        Y[Y PHASE]:::src
        B[B PHASE]:::src
    end
    subgraph SSTAGE [THYRISTOR MODULE WITH HEAT SINK]
        T1[T1 SCR]:::scr
        T2[T2 SCR]:::scr
        T3[T3 SCR]:::scr
        T4[T4 SCR]:::scr
        T5[T5 SCR]:::scr
        T6[T6 SCR]:::scr
    end
    subgraph OUTGO [TO MOTOR TERMINALS]
        U[TERMINAL U]:::out
        V[TERMINAL V]:::out
        W[TERMINAL W]:::out
    end
    R --- T1
    T1 --- T2
    T2 --- U
    Y --- T3
    T3 --- T4
    T4 --- V
    B --- T5
    T5 --- T6
    T6 --- W
    classDef src fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef scr fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Control Strategy Flowchart — Voltage Ramp with Current Limit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START COMMAND RECEIVED] --> A1[INITIALIZE FIRING ANGLE ALPHA = 150 DEG]
    A1 --> A2[APPLY GATE PULSES TO ALL SIX SCRS]
    A2 --> A3{MEASURE MOTOR CURRENT I AND SPEED N}
    A3 --> A4{CURRENT I GREATER THAN I LIMIT}
    A4 -- YES --> A5[INCREASE ALPHA BY 5 DEG]
    A5 --> A3
    A4 -- NO --> A6{CURRENT I LESS THAN I LIMIT BY MARGIN}
    A6 -- YES --> A7[DECREASE ALPHA BY 2 DEG]
    A7 --> A8{ALPHA REACHED ALPHA MIN}
    A8 -- NO --> A3
    A8 -- YES --> A9[BYPASS CONTACTOR CLOSES FULL VOLTAGE APPLIED]
    A6 -- NO --> A3
    A9 --> A10[SOFT START COMPLETE INDICATOR ON]
    A10 --> A11[STOP COMMAND OR TIMER]
    A11 --> A12[SOFT STOP RAMP ALPHA INCREASES TO 170 DEG]
    A12 --> A13[MOTOR DECELERATES TO STANDSTILL]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*
**Define a soft starter. List any two advantages of using a soft starter for an induction motor over direct-on-line starting.**

**Model Answer:**
A soft starter is a solid-state power electronic device using back-to-back connected thyristors in each phase to gradually ramp the supply voltage applied to an induction motor during starting, by varying the firing angle α.
**Advantages:** (i) Limits starting inrush current to 1.5–3 × FLC (vs. 5–8 × FLC in DOL); (ii) Reduces mechanical shock and prolongs motor/bearing life; (iii) Eliminates voltage dip in the supply; (iv) Provides smooth soft-stop to prevent water hammer in pumps.
*(Any two — 1.5 marks each)*

**Q2.** *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]*
**Why is the starting torque of an induction motor reduced when started through a soft starter? Justify with a mathematical relationship.**

**Model Answer:**
In a soft starter, the terminal voltage applied to the motor is reduced below the rated value ($V_{st} < V_{FL}$). Since the electromagnetic torque of an induction motor is **proportional to the square of the applied voltage**:

$$\dfrac{T_{st,\text{soft}}}{T_{st,\text{DOL}}} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{V_{st}}{V_{FL}}\right)^{2}$$

If $V_{st} = 0.6 V_{FL}$, then $T_{st} = (0.6)^{2} = 0.36$ times the DOL starting torque — a drastic reduction. Hence the starting torque decreases. *(2 marks for relation, 1 mark for justification.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A** *(14 Marks)* — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 3]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a three-phase AC voltage-controller-based soft starter for an induction motor. Discuss the role of the firing angle α. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole squirrel-cage induction motor draws a starting current of 6 × FLC and develops 2 × FLT under direct-on-line starting. It is started using a soft starter set at a firing angle corresponding to 60% of rated voltage. Calculate (i) the new starting current, (ii) the new starting torque, and (iii) comment on the suitability of this setting for a load requiring 1.2 × FLT for breakaway. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**
A three-phase soft starter consists of **three pairs of anti-parallel thyristors (six SCRs)**, one pair in each phase, connected between the supply and the motor terminals. The thyristor pairs are triggered by **gate pulses** generated by a microcontroller/DSP-based control unit, with each SCR pair firing symmetrically in its respective half-cycle.

The **firing angle α** measured from the supply voltage zero-crossing determines the portion of the AC cycle during which the thyristors conduct. By increasing α from 0° toward 180°, the conduction window shrinks, the RMS output voltage reduces, and consequently the starting current and torque are limited.

As the motor accelerates, the controller **gradually reduces α** (in a "voltage ramp" mode) until the motor reaches rated speed, after which a **bypass contactor** is closed to remove the thyristors from the conduction path — eliminating the on-state losses.

**Model Solution to (b):**

*Given:* $V_{st} = 0.6 V_{FL}$, $I_{DOL} = 6 \times I_{FL}$, $T_{DOL} = 2 \times T_{FL}$

**(i) New starting current:**
Starting current is **linearly** proportional to voltage.

$$I_{st,\text{soft}} \;=\; 0.6 \times I_{DOL} \;=\; 0.6 \times 6 I_{FL} \;=\; 3.6\, I_{FL}$$

**(ii) New starting torque:**
Starting torque is proportional to the **square** of voltage.

$$T_{st,\text{soft}} \;=\; (0.6)^{2} \times T_{DOL} \;=\; 0.36 \times 2 T_{FL} \;=\; 0.72\, T_{FL}$$

**(iii) Suitability for breakaway torque of 1.2 × FLT:**

The required breakaway torque is $1.2\, T_{FL}$, but the soft starter delivers only $0.72\, T_{FL}$. Since $0.72 < 1.2$, the motor **will NOT start** under this soft-start setting. The setting must be increased (firing angle reduced) so that $V_{st} / V_{FL} \ge \sqrt{1.2/2} = 0.775$, i.e., a voltage of at least **77.5%** of rated. A *kick-start* pulse may also be employed to break static friction.

---

#### **Question B** *(14 Marks)* — *[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 3]*

**(a)** Explain the voltage-ramp mode and current-limit mode of operation of a soft starter. Which mode is preferred for high-inertia loads and why? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the expression for the RMS output voltage of a single-phase AC voltage controller feeding a resistive load, when the thyristors are fired at an angle α. Hence compute the output voltage for α = 60° and a 230 V, 50 Hz supply. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**
- **Voltage-Ramp Mode:** The controller linearly increases the RMS output voltage from an initial value (e.g., 30%) to 100% over a preset time (typically 3–10 seconds). The acceleration is smooth and jerk-free. This is the **most commonly used mode** for conveyors, fans, and pumps.
- **Current-Limit Mode:** The controller monitors the line current continuously and adjusts α to keep the current at or below a preset ceiling (typically 2.5–3 × FLC). Once the motor accelerates sufficiently, α is reduced to apply full voltage. This mode is **preferred for high-inertia loads** (e.g., cement mills, flywheels) because it prevents the current from exceeding the supply's capacity, which would otherwise cause voltage sag or nuisance tripping. The constant current also produces a constant accelerating torque (after the square-law region), enabling controlled run-up.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1:** The supply voltage is $v_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, where $V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.27$ V.

**Step 2:** The thyristors conduct from $\alpha$ to $\pi$ (positive half) and from $(\pi + \alpha)$ to $2\pi$ (negative half). For R-load, $v_o = v_s$ during conduction and zero otherwise.

**Step 3:** RMS over a complete period:

$$V_{o(rms)}^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^{2} \sin^{2}(\omega t)\,d(\omega t)\cdot 2 \;=\; \dfrac{V_m^{2}}{\pi}\!\left[\dfrac{(\pi - \alpha)}{2} + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{4}\right]$$

**Step 4:** In terms of $V_s = V_m/\sqrt{2}$:

$$V_{o(rms)} \;=\; V_s \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $V_s = 230$ V and $\alpha = 60° = \pi/3$ rad:

$$\begin{aligned}
(\pi - \alpha) &= \pi - \pi/3 = 2\pi/3 \approx 2.0944 \\
\sin 2\alpha &= \sin(120°) = \sin(60°) = 0.8660 \\
\dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} &= 0.4330 \\
\text{Sum} &= 2.0944 + 0.4330 = 2.5274 \\
\dfrac{\text{Sum}}{\pi} &= 0.8044
\end{aligned}$$

$$V_{o(rms)} \;=\; 230 \times \sqrt{0.8044} \;=\; 230 \times 0.8969 \;\approx\; 206.3\,\text{V}$$

**Result:** The output RMS voltage is **≈ 206.3 V**, i.e., **89.7%** of the supply voltage.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing current and torque scaling:** A shocking number of students use $T \propto V$ (linear) instead of $T \propto V^{2}$ (quadratic) — this alone costs 2–3 marks in numericals.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the bypass contactor:** Examiners expect you to mention that after successful acceleration, the SCRs are bypassed via a mechanical contactor to avoid continuous conduction losses. Omitting this is a -1 mark deduction.
> 3. **Using `|x|` inside a markdown table:** The pipe symbol breaks the table parser. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in KTU answer sheets.
> 4. **Stating the wrong minimum firing angle:** For an R-L load, $\alpha_{min} = \phi$ (load phase angle), not 0°. Starting below $\phi$ yields no conduction — a key conceptual question.
> 5. **Ignoring the closed-loop nature:** Modern soft starters use a feedback loop (current and/or speed). A "block diagram" that omits the sensors (CT, PT, speed encoder) is considered incomplete.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Soft Starter = AC-AC voltage controller** using 6 SCRs in anti-parallel pairs.
- **Phase-angle control** of SCR firing regulates the RMS output voltage.
- RMS output for R-load: $V_{o(rms)} = V_s \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}$
- **Starting current** ∝ V (linear); **Starting torque** ∝ V² (quadratic).
- **Modes:** Voltage Ramp, Current Limit, Kick Start, Soft Stop, Energy Save.
- **Preferred mode for high-inertia loads** → **Current-Limit Mode**.
- **Preferred mode for pumps** → Voltage Ramp with **Soft Stop** (avoids water hammer).
- **Minimum firing angle for R-L load:** $\alpha_{min} = \phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega L_s / R_s)$.
- **Bypass contactor** is engaged after acceleration to reduce on-state losses.
- **Energy-Save mode** lowers iron losses (core loss ∝ V²) during light-load operation.
- **Comparison with VFD:** Soft starter is *cheaper* but cannot vary frequency — speed control is not possible. For variable-speed applications, use a **VFD (Variable Frequency Drive)**.
- **Typical KTU 14-mark question structure:** Block diagram (3 m) + Operating principle (4 m) + Numerical on $V_o$ or torque (4 m) + Comparison (3 m).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Three Phase Squirrel and Slip Ring Induction Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Three Phase Squirrel and Slip Ring Induction Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Three-Phase Induction Motor (TIM)** is a self-starting, robust, and highly reliable alternating current (AC) electromechanical energy conversion device that operates on the principle of **electromagnetic induction** discovered by Michael Faraday. It is classified under asynchronous machines because the rotor never quite reaches the speed of the rotating magnetic field; the perpetual difference between these speeds is termed **slip**.

For the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCPT503), the classification of three-phase induction motors is mandatory. They are broadly classified into two distinct categories based on rotor construction:

1. **Squirrel Cage Induction Motor (SCIM)** – A motor where the rotor consists of conducting bars short-circuited at both ends by end rings, resembling a "squirrel cage." It is the most widely manufactured electrical machine in the world (over **90%** of industrial motors).
2. **Slip Ring Induction Motor (SRIM)** – Also called a **Wound Rotor Induction Motor (WRIM)**, where the rotor winding is similar to the stator winding, and its terminals are brought out via slip rings and brushes to external rheostats for starting and speed control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (Module 3):** Students must be able to compare construction, working principle, slip, torque, starting methods, and speed control of both Squirrel Cage and Slip Ring Induction Motors. Numerical problems on the equivalent circuit, slip, and torque are high-weightage.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing still and holding a strong bar magnet. A loose iron nail nearby does nothing because the magnetic field is stationary. Now, imagine you start **spinning** the magnet in a circle. The magnetic flux lines "cut" through the nail, inducing eddy currents in it. By **Lenz's Law**, these induced currents create their own opposing magnetic field, which causes the nail to be **dragged along** behind the rotating magnet, chasing it.

- The **stator** (with its 3-phase supply) is the spinning magnet.
- The **rotor** is the nail (or short-circuited cage) being dragged along.
- The nail never catches up to the magnet — this lag is the **slip**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the rotor "chased" and not "pushed" forward?** Because the induced currents in the rotor always oppose the relative motion (Lenz's Law). The rotor must always rotate *slower* than the rotating magnetic field to keep the relative velocity (and thus the induced EMF) non-zero. If the rotor reached synchronous speed, there would be no relative motion, no induced EMF, no current, and therefore no torque.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Supply Frequency (f)**: In India, the standard industrial frequency is **f = 50 Hz**.
- **Number of Poles (P)**: Must be an **even integer** (2, 4, 6, 8, 10, ...).
- **Synchronous Speed (Ns)**: Determined solely by supply frequency and number of poles.
- **Rotor Speed (Nr)**: Always less than Ns for motoring operation.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Identity:** The stator of an induction motor is *identical* in construction for both SCIM and SRIM. The classification depends **entirely on the rotor construction**. This is a common viva question in KTU lab exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Comparison

#### A. Common Stator Construction (Both SCIM and SRIM)
- **Stator Frame**: Cast iron or fabricated steel, holds the core.
- **Stator Core**: Laminated silicon steel (to reduce eddy current losses), with semi-closed slots on the inner periphery.
- **Stator Winding**: 3-phase, distributed, double-layer (in most cases) winding placed in the slots. The three phases are displaced by **120° electrical** in space.

#### B. Squirrel Cage Rotor
- **Rotor Core**: Laminated silicon steel cylindrical core.
- **Rotor Bars**: Uninsulated **copper** (small machines) or **aluminum** (most common) bars placed axially inside the rotor slots.
- **End Rings**: The bars are permanently short-circuited at both ends by conducting end rings. The assembly looks like a **hamster/pet squirrel's exercise wheel**, hence the name.
- **No External Connection**: The rotor is a permanently short-circuited, sealed unit. No slip rings, no brushes, no external resistance.

#### C. Slip Ring (Wound) Rotor
- **Rotor Core**: Laminated silicon steel.
- **Rotor Winding**: 3-phase insulated winding, similar to the stator winding, connected in **star** internally. The rotor is *not* short-circuited internally.
- **Slip Rings**: Three brass or copper rings mounted on the shaft, each connected to one end of the 3-phase rotor winding.
- **Brushes**: Carbon brushes ride on the slip rings, providing external electrical access.
- **External Resistance**: A 3-phase star-connected rheostat is connected via the brushes to vary the effective rotor resistance.

### 2.2 Working Principle — The Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF)

When a balanced 3-phase AC supply is given to the stator, the three time-displaced currents (each displaced by **120°** in time) flowing through spatially displaced windings (each displaced by **120°** in space) together produce a magnetic flux of **constant magnitude** that rotates at synchronous speed.

**Why constant magnitude?** At any instant, the vector sum of the three phase MMFs equals **1.5 × Φm**, where **Φm** is the maximum flux per pole. This is mathematically proven using the sum of three sinusoids spaced 120° apart.

### 2.3 Slip — The Heart of Induction Motor Operation

The **slip (s)** is defined as the relative speed between the rotating magnetic field and the rotor, expressed as a fraction (or per-unit) of synchronous speed:

$$s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

Where:
- $N_s$ = Synchronous speed of the rotating magnetic field (in RPM)
- $N_r$ = Actual mechanical rotor speed (in RPM)
- The **per-unit slip** ranges from **0 (no-load ideal)** to **1 (standstill)** for motoring mode.

**Operating Regions based on Slip:**

| Region | Slip Range | Rotor Speed | Operating Mode |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Motoring** | $0 < s < 1$ | $0 < N_r < N_s$ | Normal forward running |
| **Plugging (Braking)** | $1 < s < 2$ | Negative | Reverse field, fast braking |
| **Generating** | $s < 0$ | $N_r > N_s$ | Induction generator (e.g., wind turbines) |
| **Standstill** | $s = 1$ | $N_r = 0$ | Starting condition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **At starting condition, s = 1.** This is critical because all the rotor parameters (EMF, reactance, frequency) are evaluated at s = 1 for starting calculations.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | RPM | f in Hz, P = number of poles |
| 2 | Synchronous Angular Velocity | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{4\pi f}{P}$ | rad/s | Used in torque equation |
| 3 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | per-unit (pu) | Range 0 to 1 in motoring |
| 4 | Rotor Speed | $N_r = N_s(1 - s)$ | RPM | Derived from slip definition |
| 5 | Rotor Frequency | $f_r = s \, f$ | Hz | Frequency of induced rotor EMF |
| 6 | Rotor EMF (per phase) | $E_{2s} = s \, E_2$ | V | E₂ = standstill rotor EMF |
| 7 | Rotor Reactance (per phase) | $X_{2s} = s \, X_2$ | Ω | X₂ = standstill rotor reactance |
| 8 | Rotor Impedance (per phase) | $Z_{2s} = \sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$ | Ω | Magnitude of rotor impedance |
| 9 | Rotor Current (per phase) | $I_2 = \dfrac{sE_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}}$ | A | Phasor form |
| 10 | Power Factor of Rotor | $\cos\phi_2 = \dfrac{R_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}}$ | dimensionless | Slip-dependent |
| 11 | Gross Mechanical Power | $P_m = 3 I_2^2 R_2 \dfrac{(1-s)}{s}$ | W | Air-gap power × (1-s) |
| 12 | Gross Torque | $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$ | N·m | Universal torque equation |
| 13 | Slip at Max Torque | $s_{mT} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ | pu | For SCIM: usually ~0.1-0.2 |
| 14 | Maximum Torque | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 E_2^2}{2 \, \omega_s \, X_2}$ | N·m | **Independent of R₂** |
| 15 | Starting Torque | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$ | N·m | Slip s = 1 |
| 16 | Full-load Slip | $s_{fl} = \dfrac{R_2}{\sqrt{(R_2/X_2)^2 + \text{ratios}}}$ | pu | Depends on load |

> [!TIP]
> **Maximum Torque Invariance Theorem (KTU Favorite):** The maximum torque developed by an induction motor is **independent of rotor resistance R₂**. However, the *slip* at which maximum torque occurs is *directly proportional* to R₂. This is the theoretical foundation for adding external resistance in SRIM starting.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Induction motors are the **workhorse of industry**:
- **SCIM**: Pumps, fans, compressors, conveyors, electric vehicles (modern EVs use squirrel cage motors driven by VFDs).
- **SRIM**: Cranes, hoists, elevators, large wind turbine generators (DFIG), and high-inertia loads requiring soft starting with high starting torque.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Synchronous Speed and Rotor EMF Frequency

#### Step 1: Establishing Synchronous Speed
Consider a 2-pole machine. In one complete cycle of the AC supply, the N-pole sweeps across one mechanical revolution. For a P-pole machine, the magnetic field must sweep across P/2 cycles for one mechanical revolution.

$$\text{Time for one cycle} = \dfrac{1}{f} \text{ seconds}$$

$$\text{Number of cycles per revolution} = \dfrac{P}{2}$$

$$N_s = \dfrac{\text{Number of cycles/sec}}{\text{Cycles per rev}} = \dfrac{f}{P/2} = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$$

> **Logic:** A 4-pole motor at 50 Hz gives $N_s = 120 \times 50 / 4 = 1500$ RPM. A 6-pole motor at 50 Hz gives 1000 RPM. This is why induction motors run at "discrete" speeds, not continuously variable speeds (without a VFD).

#### Step 2: Rotor Frequency
The rotor "sees" a relative speed of $(N_s - N_r)$ RPM. The frequency of EMF induced in the rotor is proportional to this relative speed:

$$f_r = \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \dfrac{(N_s - N_r)}{60}$$

Substituting $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$:

$$f_r = \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \dfrac{s \, N_s}{60} = s \cdot \left(\dfrac{P N_s}{120}\right) = s \cdot f$$

#### Step 3: Rotor EMF and Reactance
Since the rotor EMF is proportional to the rate of change of flux, and the relative velocity is s times the synchronous velocity:

$$E_{2s} = s \cdot E_2 \quad \text{(where } E_2 \text{ is the standstill rotor EMF at } s = 1\text{)}$$

$$X_{2s} = 2\pi f_r L_2 = 2\pi (s f) L_2 = s \cdot (2\pi f L_2) = s \cdot X_2$$

> **Logic:** At standstill (s = 1), rotor frequency equals stator frequency (50 Hz). At full load (s ≈ 0.04), rotor frequency drops to ~2 Hz. This is why slip-ring rotor windings are often designed for lower frequencies.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Universal Torque Equation

#### Step 1: Air-Gap Power
The total power transferred across the air gap from stator to rotor (per phase, assuming star) is:

$$P_{g, \text{phase}} = E_{2s} \cdot I_2 \cdot \cos\phi_2$$

The total air-gap power for 3 phases:

$$P_g = 3 \cdot E_{2s} \cdot I_2 \cdot \cos\phi_2$$

#### Step 2: Substitute Rotor Current
From Ohm's law applied to the rotor circuit:

$$I_2 = \dfrac{E_{2s}}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + X_{2s}^2}} = \dfrac{sE_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}}$$

#### Step 3: Compute Power Factor
The rotor circuit power factor:

$$\cos\phi_2 = \dfrac{R_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}}$$

#### Step 4: Substitute Back into Air-Gap Power

$$P_g = 3 \cdot \dfrac{sE_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}} \cdot \dfrac{sE_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}}$$

$$P_g = \dfrac{3 s^2 E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$$

> **Correction note:** Standard textbook derivation gives $P_g = \dfrac{3 s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$ (the second 's' in numerator is often missed by students; it should be single 's' if we use $I_2^2 R_2/s$ logic, see Step 5).

#### Step 5: Clean Derivation via Rotor Copper Loss
The rotor copper loss per phase is $I_2^2 R_2$. Total rotor copper loss:

$$P_{cu, \text{rotor}} = 3 I_2^2 R_2 = \dfrac{3 s^2 E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$$

The fraction of air-gap power lost as rotor copper loss is $s$:

$$P_{cu, \text{rotor}} = s \cdot P_g \implies P_g = \dfrac{P_{cu, \text{rotor}}}{s} = \dfrac{3 s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$$

#### Step 6: Mechanical Power Developed

$$P_m = P_g - P_{cu, \text{rotor}} = (1 - s) P_g = \dfrac{3 s E_2^2 R_2 (1-s)}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$$

#### Step 7: Gross Torque Developed

$$T_g = \dfrac{P_m}{\omega_r} = \dfrac{P_m}{\omega_s (1-s)} = \dfrac{P_g}{\omega_s}$$

$$\boxed{T_g = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}}$$

> **Logic:** Since $P_g / \omega_s$ is independent of slip in the denominator chain, gross torque can be directly obtained from air-gap power divided by synchronous speed. This is a powerful shortcut for solving KTU problems.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Condition for Maximum Torque

#### Step 1: Differentiate Torque w.r.t. Slip
Differentiate $T_g$ with respect to s and equate to zero. We maximize only the slip-dependent fraction:

$$\dfrac{d}{ds}\left[\dfrac{s R_2}{R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2}\right] = 0$$

Using the quotient rule:

$$\dfrac{R_2 (R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2) - s R_2 (2s X_2^2)}{(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2)^2} = 0$$

#### Step 2: Simplify the Numerator
Set numerator = 0:

$$R_2 (R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2) - 2 s^2 R_2 X_2^2 = 0$$

$$R_2^3 + R_2 s^2 X_2^2 - 2 s^2 R_2 X_2^2 = 0$$

$$R_2^3 - s^2 R_2 X_2^2 = 0$$

$$R_2^2 = s^2 X_2^2$$

$$\boxed{s_{mT} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}}$$

#### Step 3: Substitute Back to Find Maximum Torque
Substitute $s = R_2 / X_2$ into the torque equation:

$$R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2 = R_2^2 + \left(\dfrac{R_2^2}{X_2^2}\right) X_2^2 = 2 R_2^2$$

$$T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{(R_2/X_2) \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2}{2 R_2^2} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^2}{2 X_2}$$

$$\boxed{T_{max} = \dfrac{3 E_2^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}}$$

> **Critical Insight:** $T_{max}$ has **no R₂** in the formula. This is why adding external rotor resistance in SRIM shifts the *slip* at which max torque occurs (toward higher values, useful for starting heavy loads) without changing the *value* of maximum torque.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Torque-Slip Characteristic Equation (Alternate Form)

Starting from $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 E_2^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}$ and dividing $T_g$ by $T_{max}$:

$$\dfrac{T_g}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{2 s (R_2/X_2)}{1 + (sX_2/R_2)^2}$$

Let $a = R_2/s_{mT}X_2$ ... no, let $a = s/s_{mT}$:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{T_g}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{2 a}{1 + a^2} \quad \text{where } a = \dfrac{s}{s_{mT}}}$$

> This is a very useful form for KTU numericals where the slip at maximum torque and the slip at operating point are both given. The student only needs to find the ratio 'a'.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Induction Motor Drive

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[3 Phase AC Mains Supply 50Hz] --> B[Stator Winding 3 Phase Distributed]
    B --> C[Rotating Magnetic Field Ns RPM]
    C --> D[Air Gap Flux Linkage]
    D --> E[Rotor Conductors]
    E --> F{Induced Rotor EMF at Slip s}
    F --> G[Rotor Current I2]
    G --> H[Torque Te Proportional to Phi times I2 times cos phi2]
    H --> I[Mechanical Rotation at Nr]
    I --> J[Load Coupled to Shaft]
    J --> K[Back EMF and Slip Feedback]
    K --> C
```

### 4.2 Constructional Comparison: SCIM vs SRIM

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph M1[Stator Assembly Common to Both]
        M1A[Stator Frame Cast Iron]
        M1B[Stator Core Laminated Silicon Steel]
        M1C[3 Phase Stator Winding]
    end
    subgraph M2[Squirrel Cage Rotor SCIM]
        M2A[Rotor Core Laminated]
        M2B[Copper or Aluminum Bars in Slots]
        M2C[End Rings Short Circuiting Both Ends]
        M2D[No External Connection Permanently Sealed]
    end
    subgraph M3[Slip Ring Rotor SRIM]
        M3A[Rotor Core Laminated]
        M3B[3 Phase Insulated Rotor Winding Star Connected]
        M3C[Three Slip Rings on Shaft]
        M3D[Carbon Brushes with External 3 Phase Rheostat]
    end
```

### 4.3 Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit (Rotor Side Referred to Stator)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stator Phase Voltage V1] --> B[Stator Resistance R1]
    B --> C[Stator Leakage Reactance X1]
    C --> D[Magnetising Branch Xm parallel Rc]
    D --> E[Rotor Side Referred R2 dash]
    E --> F[Rotor Side Referred X2 dash]
    F --> G[Load Resistor R2 dash times 1-s over s]
    G --> H[Equivalent Mechanical Output]
```

> **Note on Equivalent Circuit:** The term $\dfrac{R_2'}{s} = R_2' + \dfrac{R_2'(1-s)}{s}$ models the mechanical load. The component $\dfrac{R_2'(1-s)}{s}$ represents the equivalent electrical resistance that consumes power equal to the gross mechanical power developed. The term $R_2'$ represents the rotor copper loss.

### 4.4 Torque-Slip Characteristic Map (Three Regions)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Slip Region s less than 0] --> B[Induction Generator Mode Rotor faster than Field]
    C[Slip Region 0 less than s less than 1] --> D[Motoring Mode Normal Operation]
    E[Slip Region s greater than 1] --> F[Plugging Braking Mode]
    G[Slip s equals 0] --> H[No Torque Synchronous Speed Theoretical]
    I[Slip s equals 1] --> J[Starting Torque Tst]
    K[Slip s equals R2 over X2] --> L[Maximum Torque Tmax]
```

> **Reading the Curve:** Near synchronous speed (s ≈ 0), torque is small. As slip increases, torque rises rapidly, reaches a **maximum at $s = R_2/X_2$**, then falls. At s = 1 (standstill), the torque is the **starting torque** $T_{st}$. For normal operation, the motor runs on the **stable region** between s = 0 and $s = s_{mT}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Differentiate between Squirrel Cage and Slip Ring Induction Motors based on construction, starting method, and typical applications.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Squirrel Cage IM | Slip Ring IM |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Rotor Type** | Permanently short-circuited copper/aluminum bars | 3-phase insulated winding, star connected |
| **Slip Rings & Brushes** | Absent | Present (3 slip rings + 3 brushes) |
| **External Resistance** | Cannot be added | Can be added via slip rings |
| **Starting Torque** | Low to moderate (1 to 1.5 times full-load torque) | High (up to 2.5 times full-load torque) |
| **Starting Current** | Very high (5 to 7 times rated) | Can be limited using external resistance |
| **Speed Control** | Limited (VFD, pole changing, cascade) | Possible via rotor resistance variation |
| **Maintenance** | Low (rugged, sealed) | High (brushes need replacement) |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Applications** | Pumps, fans, compressors, conveyors | Cranes, hoists, elevators, high-inertia loads |

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Define slip in a three-phase induction motor. What is the slip at (i) synchronous speed, (ii) standstill, and (iii) full load? Why is slip never zero in practice?*

**Model Answer:**

Slip (s) is defined as the **difference between synchronous speed ($N_s$) and actual rotor speed ($N_r$), expressed as a fraction of $N_s$**:
