$$\frac{R_r'}{s_m} = X_s + X_r' \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{s_m = \frac{R_r'}{X_s + X_r'}}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $R = X_{total}$ back into the torque expression:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_s^2 \cdot X_{total}}{X_{total}^2 + X_{total}^2} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_s^2}{2 X_{total}}$$

$$\boxed{T_{max} = \frac{3 \cdot V_s^2}{2 \omega_s (X_s + X_r')}}$$

**Step 7:** Verify voltage dependence — the equation shows that $T_{max} \propto V_s^2$ since $\omega_s$ and $X_{total}$ are constant at fixed frequency. This confirms the central result.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Approximate (Kloss) Torque Equation

Starting from the ratio $T_e / T_{max}$:

$$\frac{T_e}{T_{max}} = \frac{\frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_s^2 \cdot (R_r'/s)}{(R_r'/s)^2 + X_{total}^2}}{\frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_s^2}{2 X_{total}}}$$

Simplifying the constants:

$$\frac{T_e}{T_{max}} = \frac{2 X_{total} \cdot (R_r'/s)}{(R_r'/s)^2 + X_{total}^2}$$

Substitute $R_r'/s = s_m \cdot X_{total} / s$ (since $s_m = R_r'/X_{total}$):

$$\frac{T_e}{T_{max}} = \frac{2 X_{total} \cdot \dfrac{s_m X_{total}}{s}}{\left(\dfrac{s_m X_{total}}{s}\right)^2 + X_{total}^2} = \frac{2 s_m / s}{s_m^2 / s^2 + 1}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $s^2$:

$$\frac{T_e}{T_{max}} = \frac{2 s \cdot s_m}{s_m^2 + s^2} = \frac{2}{\dfrac{s_m}{s} + \dfrac{s}{s_m}}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{T_e}{T_{max}} = \frac{2}{\dfrac{s}{s_m} + \dfrac{s_m}{s}}}$$

This is the **Kloss equation**, a vital KTU high-yield formula for numerical problems.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Voltage Reduction

Let the rated voltage be $V_{rated}$ with rated torque $T_{rated}$ at slip $s_1$. Let the new voltage be $V_s = k \cdot V_{rated}$ where $0 < k < 1$. The new operating slip $s_2$ on the same load line must satisfy the Kloss equation (assuming the load torque is constant, i.e., a *hoist-type* load):

$$T_{rated} = \frac{2 k^2 T_{max,rated}}{\dfrac{s_2}{s_m} + \dfrac{s_m}{s_2}} \cdot \frac{1}{k^2}$$

Wait — more directly, for a constant load torque $T_L$ at the *new* voltage $k V_{rated}$:

$$T_L = \frac{2 k^2 T_{max,rated}}{\dfrac{s_2}{s_m} + \dfrac{s_m}{s_2}}$$

Solving for $s_2$:

$$\frac{s_2}{s_m} + \frac{s_m}{s_2} = \frac{2 k^2 T_{max,rated}}{T_L}$$

| Voltage Ratio $k$ | $T_{max}$ | $s_m$ | Stable Region Width | Application Feasibility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $k = 1.0$ (rated) | $T_{max,rated}$ | $s_{m,rated}$ | **Wide** | Full load operation |
| $k = 0.8$ | $0.64 \, T_{max,rated}$ | $s_{m,rated}$ | Reduced | Light loads |
| $k = 0.5$ | $0.25 \, T_{max,rated}$ | $s_{m,rated}$ | **Very Narrow** | Fans, blowers only |
| $k = 0.3$ | $0.09 \, T_{max,rated}$ | $s_{m,rated}$ | Almost nil | Stall risk |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating Region:** For stable operation, the motor must operate in the region $0 < s < s_m$ (positive slope region). The stable region width is *unaffected* by voltage, but the **available torque headroom** shrinks dramatically.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Demonstration (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has $R_r' = 0.5 \, \Omega$ and $X_{total} = X_s + X_r' = 5 \, \Omega$. At rated voltage $V_{rated} = 400$ V (line-to-line, star connected), the motor drives a fan load with $T_L = 50$ N·m at $s_1 = 0.04$. Find the new slip $s_2$ when voltage is reduced to $V_s = 320$ V (line).

**Solution:**

**Step A:** Compute $s_m$:

$$s_m = \frac{R_r'}{X_{total}} = \frac{0.5}{5} = 0.1$$

**Step B:** Compute $T_{max,rated}$:

$$T_{max,rated} = \frac{3 V_{ph}^2}{2 \omega_s X_{total}}$$

$V_{ph} = 400 / \sqrt{3} = 230.94$ V. $\omega_s = 2\pi (50) / 2 = 157.08$ rad/s.

$$T_{max,rated} = \frac{3 \times (230.94)^2}{2 \times 157.08 \times 5} = \frac{160{,}000}{1570.8} \approx 101.86 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step C:** Compute voltage ratio $k$:

$$k = \frac{V_{new,ph}}{V_{rated,ph}} = \frac{320/\sqrt{3}}{400/\sqrt{3}} = \frac{320}{400} = 0.8$$

**Step D:** New $T_{max}$:

$$T_{max,new} = k^2 \cdot T_{max,rated} = 0.64 \times 101.86 \approx 65.19 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step E:** For fan load $T_L = k_{fan} \cdot N^2$, at reduced voltage the new slip $s_2$ must satisfy:

$$T_L(s_2) = \frac{2 T_{max,new}}{\dfrac{s_2}{s_m} + \dfrac{s_m}{s_2}}$$

Assuming load torque at new speed: $T_L(s_2) = T_{rated} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1-s_2}{1-s_1}\right)^2$ (quadratic load).

Equating and solving numerically (assume $s_2 \approx 0.07$ as first guess):

$$T_L(0.07) = 50 \cdot \left(\frac{0.93}{0.96}\right)^2 = 50 \times 0.9389 \approx 46.94 \text{ N·m}$$

$$T_e(0.07) = \frac{2 \times 65.19}{\frac{0.07}{0.1} + \frac{0.1}{0.07}} = \frac{130.38}{0.7 + 1.4286} = \frac{130.38}{2.1286} \approx 61.25 \text{ N·m}$$

Since $T_e > T_L$, rotor accelerates → slip decreases. Try $s_2 = 0.06$:

$$T_e(0.06) = \frac{130.38}{\frac{0.06}{0.1} + \frac{0.1}{0.06}} = \frac{130.38}{0.6 + 1.6667} = \frac{130.38}{2.2667} \approx 57.52 \text{ N·m}$$

$$T_L(0.06) = 50 \cdot \left(\frac{0.94}{0.96}\right)^2 = 50 \times 0.9589 \approx 47.95 \text{ N·m}$$

Continue iteration. At $s_2 \approx 0.055$:

$$T_e(0.055) = \frac{130.38}{0.55 + 1.818} = \frac{130.38}{2.368} \approx 55.06 \text{ N·m}$$

$$T_L(0.055) = 50 \cdot \left(\frac{0.945}{0.96}\right)^2 \approx 48.49 \text{ N·m}$$

**Final equilibrium slip $s_2 \approx 0.052$ to $0.054$**, giving a new speed of:

$$N_2 = 1500 (1 - 0.053) \approx 1420.5 \text{ rpm}$$

Thus, voltage reduction from 400 V to 320 V caused a speed drop of approximately **60 rpm** for this fan load.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Voltage Control Schemes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Three-Phase AC Mains 50 Hz 400 V] --> B{Voltage Control Method}
    B --> C1[Autotransformer Tap Changer]
    B --> C2[AC Voltage Controller - Thyristor Phase Control]
    B --> C3[Series Reactor / Impedance]
    C1 --> D[Variable Reduced Voltage Vs]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Stator Windings of 3-Phase Induction Motor]
    E --> F[Rotating Magnetic Field at Ns]
    F --> G[Modified Torque Slip Curve Tmax proportional to Vs squared]
    G --> H[Operating Point Shifts to Higher Slip s2]
    H --> I[New Reduced Steady State Speed N2]
    E --> J[Mechanical Load Fan Pump Conveyor]
    J --> K{Torque Balance Check}
    K --> L[Stable Region s less than sm]
    K --> M[Unstable Stall Region s greater than sm]
    L --> N[Continuous Operation Achieved]
    M --> O[Motor Stalls - Voltage Too Low]
```

### 4.2 Torque-Slip Characteristic Family under Voltage Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Origin s equals 0 T equals 0] --> A1[Linear Region 0 to sm]
    A1 --> A2[Peak Tmax at sm - Independent of Vs]
    A2 --> A3[Falling Region sm to 1 - Unstable]
    subgraph CurveFamily[Family of Torque Slip Curves]
        direction TB
        V100[Rated Voltage 100 percent - Highest Tmax]
        V80[80 percent Voltage - Tmax = 64 percent]
        V60[60 percent Voltage - Tmax = 36 percent]
        V40[40 percent Voltage - Tmax = 16 percent]
    end
    V100 --- A2
    V80 --- A2
    V60 --- A2
    V40 --- A2
    L1[Load Curve TL] --> A1
    L1 --> A2
    A2 --> O1[Operating Point 1 - Rated]
    A2 --> O2[Operating Point 2 - Reduced Voltage - Higher Slip]
    O1 -.-> A1
    O2 -.-> A1
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Voltage Control (Optional Configuration)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP[Speed Reference Nref] --> SUM[(Summing Junction)]
    FB[Speed Sensor Tachogenerator] --> SUM
    SUM --> ERR[Error Signal e equals Nref minus Nact]
    ERR --> CTRL[PI / PID Controller]
    CTRL --> FC[Firing Circuit]
    FC --> THY[Three-Phase Thyristor Voltage Controller]
    THY --> MOT[3-Phase Induction Motor]
    MOT --> LOAD[Mechanical Load]
    MOT --> FB
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why does voltage control of an induction motor result in a limited speed range? Justify with the torque equation.**

**Model Answer:**

The synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$ is independent of stator voltage $V_s$. Hence, voltage variation cannot change $N_s$ directly. The maximum torque $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 V_s^2}{2 \omega_s (X_s + X_r')}$ varies as $V_s^2$, while the slip at maximum torque $s_m = R_r'/(X_s + X_r')$ remains **constant** irrespective of $V_s$. Therefore, when $V_s$ is reduced:

- The entire torque-slip curve scales downward (peak torque reduces quadratically).
- The stable operating region ($0 < s < s_m$) is unchanged in width but the *available torque headroom* shrinks.
- Beyond a certain low voltage, $T_{max}$ becomes insufficient to overcome the load torque, causing the motor to stall.

Thus, voltage control is restricted to a **narrow speed range**, typically applicable only to **quadratic (fan/pump-type) loads** where torque demand is low at low speeds.

*(3 marks — 1 mark each for: statement on $N_s$ independence, $T_{max} \propto V_s^2$ justification, stall-load argument.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three methods of varying the stator supply voltage for speed control of a three-phase induction motor. State one advantage and one disadvantage.**

**Model Answer:**

Three methods of stator voltage variation are:

1. **Autotransformer tap-changing** — Manually or motor-operated tap selection on a 3-phase autotransformer.
2. **AC voltage controllers using back-to-back thyristors** — Phase-angle control or integral-cycle control using TRIAC/SCR pairs.
3. **Series reactor / saturable reactor control** — Variable inductance in series with stator to drop voltage.

**Advantage:** Simple, low-cost implementation; reduces starting current and starting torque simultaneously.

**Disadvantage:** Very narrow speed-control range; high rotor copper losses (since $P_{cu,rotor} = s \cdot P_{gap}$ increases with slip); not suitable for constant-torque loads.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for each method + advantage/disadvantage.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4, Choice A]`

**A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, Y-connected induction motor has the following per-phase parameters referred to the stator:**

$R_s = 0.5 \, \Omega, \quad R_r' = 0.6 \, \Omega, \quad X_s = 1.2 \, \Omega, \quad X_r' = 1.5 \, \Omega$

The motor is supplied at rated line voltage of 415 V.

**(a)** Derive the expression for maximum torque and critical slip. Compute their values at rated voltage.

**(b)** If the stator voltage is reduced to **70% of rated value**, find the new maximum torque and the slip at which the motor develops a torque of $0.6 \cdot T_{max,new}$ on the stable region. Comment on the operating feasibility.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of $T_{max}$ and $s_m$:** [7 marks]

Starting from the per-phase equivalent circuit referred to stator, the air-gap power per phase is:

$$P_{gap,\phi} = \frac{V_s^2 \cdot (R_r'/s)}{(R_r'/s)^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}$$

Total air-gap power: $P_{gap} = 3 P_{gap,\phi}$. Synchronous mechanical speed: $\omega_{ms} = \omega_s / p_{pairs} = 2\pi f / P$ (for 4-pole, $P = 4$, so pole pairs = 2).

Torque:

$$T_e = \frac{P_{gap}}{\omega_{ms}} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_s^2 \cdot (R_r'/s)}{(R_r'/s)^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}$$

To find maximum, differentiate $T_e$ with respect to $s$ and set $\dfrac{dT_e}{ds} = 0$. After algebraic simplification:

$$s_m = \frac{R_r'}{X_s + X_r'}$$

Substituting back:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 V_s^2}{2 \omega_s (X_s + X_r')}$$

**Numerical Computation:**

- $X_{total} = X_s + X_r' = 1.2 + 1.5 = 2.7 \, \Omega$
- $s_m = 0.6 / 2.7 = 0.2222$
- $V_{ph} = 415 / \sqrt{3} = 239.6$ V
- $\omega_s = 2\pi f / p_{pairs} = 2\pi (50) / 2 = 157.08$ rad/s
- $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 \times (239.6)^2}{2 \times 157.08 \times 2.7} = \dfrac{172{,}248}{848.23} \approx 203.07$ N·m

---

**(b) Voltage reduction to 70% of rated:** [7 marks]

Voltage ratio: $k = 0.7$.

New maximum torque (since $T_{max} \propto V_s^2$):

$$T_{max,new} = k^2 \cdot T_{max,rated} = 0.49 \times 203.07 = 99.50 \text{ N·m}$$

The critical slip remains **unchanged**:

$$s_m = 0.2222$$

The motor develops $0.6 \cdot T_{max,new} = 0.6 \times 99.50 = 59.70$ N·m at slip $s_x$ in the stable region. Using the Kloss equation:

$$0.6 = \frac{2}{\dfrac{s_x}{s_m} + \dfrac{s_m}{s_x}}$$

Let $y = s_x / s_m$. Then:

$$0.6 = \frac{2}{y + 1/y} = \frac{2y}{y^2 + 1}$$

$$0.6(y^2 + 1) = 2y$$
$$0.6 y^2 - 2y + 0.6 = 0$$

Solving the quadratic:

$$y = \frac{2 \pm \sqrt{4 - 1.44}}{1.2} = \frac{2 \pm \sqrt{2.56}}{1.2} = \frac{2 \pm 1.6}{1.2}$$

- $y_1 = (2 + 1.6)/1.2 = 3.0$
- $y_2 = (2 - 1.6)/1.2 = 0.3333$

For the **stable region** ($s < s_m$), we choose $y_2 = 0.3333$:

$$s_x = 0.3333 \times 0.2222 = 0.0741$$

Thus, the new operating slip is **$s \approx 0.0741$**, and the corresponding rotor speed is:

$$N = 1500 (1 - 0.0741) \approx 1388.9 \text{ rpm}$$

**Feasibility Comment:** Although the motor can develop the required torque at reduced voltage, the rotor copper loss ratio is $P_{cu,rotor}/P_{gap} = s = 0.0741$ (about 7.4%), which is acceptable. However, $T_{max,new} = 99.5$ N·m provides only marginal overload capability, so any load surge could stall the motor. Hence, this operating point is feasible for *steady-state fan/pump loads* but unsafe for *constant-torque loads*.

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, Choice B]`

**(a)** With the help of torque-slip characteristics, explain the **voltage control method** of speed control of a three-phase induction motor. Show that the **maximum torque varies as the square of the stator voltage** and that the **slip at maximum torque is independent of the stator voltage**.

**(b)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz induction motor drives a fan load. The motor has $R_r' = 0.4 \, \Omega$ per phase and total leakage reactance $X_{total} = 4 \, \Omega$ per phase. At rated voltage, the motor operates at a slip of 3%. Determine the slip when the voltage is reduced to 60% of rated value. Assume the fan load torque varies as the **square of the speed**.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation with torque-slip characteristics:** [7 marks]

**Diagram Description (to be drawn in exam):**

A set of torque-slip curves is plotted on a single graph for several values of stator voltage: $V_{rated}$, $0.8 V_{rated}$, $0.6 V_{rated}$, $0.4 V_{rated}$. All curves are shown to:

- Pass through the origin.
- Have their peaks at the **same slip** $s_m$.
- Have peak torque values scaled as $(V/V_{rated})^2$.
- Decline toward the starting torque $T_{st}$ as $s \to 1$, where $T_{st}$ also scales as $(V/V_{rated})^2$.

The load characteristic (e.g., fan torque $\propto N^2$) intersects these curves at progressively higher slips as voltage is reduced.

**Mathematical Proof:**

Starting from the torque expression:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_s^2 \cdot (R_r'/s)}{(R_r'/s)^2 + X_{total}^2}$$

To find $s_m$ where $T_e$ is maximum, we differentiate:

$$\frac{dT_e}{ds} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d}{ds}\left[\frac{R_r'/s}{(R_r'/s)^2 + X_{total}^2}\right] = 0$$

Applying quotient rule and simplifying:

$$X_{total}^2 = (R_r'/s)^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_r'}{s_m} = X_{total}$$

$$\boxed{s_m = \frac{R_r'}{X_{total}} = \frac{R_r'}{X_s + X_r'}}$$

Since $R_r'$, $X_s$, and $X_r'$ are **independent of $V_s$**, we conclude that **$s_m$ is independent of $V_s$**.

Substituting $R_r'/s_m = X_{total}$ in the torque equation:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_s^2 \cdot X_{total}}{X_{total}^2 + X_{total}^2} = \frac{3 V_s^2}{2 \omega_s X_{total}}$$

Since $\omega_s$, $X_{total}$ are constant, $T_{max} \propto V_s^2$. Hence, **maximum torque varies as the square of the stator voltage**. ∎

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:** [7 marks]

**Given:**

- 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz motor
- $R_r' = 0.4 \, \Omega$, $X_{total} = 4 \, \Omega$
- Rated voltage: operating slip $s_1 = 0.03$
- Voltage reduced to 60% of rated
- Load torque: $T_L \propto N^2$ (quadratic / fan load)

**Step 1:** Compute $s_m$:

$$s_m = \frac{0.4}{4} = 0.1$$

**Step 2:** Synchronous speed:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3:** Rated operating speed:

$$N_1 = 1000 (1 - 0.03) = 970 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 4:** Rated operating torque. Using the Kloss approximation:

$$T_{e1} = \frac{2 T_{max,rated}}{\dfrac{s_1}{s_m} + \dfrac{s_m}{s_1}} = \frac{2 T_{max,rated}}{\frac{0.03}{0.1} + \frac{0.1}{0.03}} = \frac{2 T_{max,rated}}{0.3 + 3.333} = \frac{2 T_{max,rated}}{3.633} = 0.5504 \, T_{max,rated}$$

**Step 5:** At reduced voltage $V_s = 0.6 V_{rated}$:

$$T_{max,new} = 0.36 \, T_{max,rated}$$

**Step 6:** Fan load torque relationship:

$$T_L \propto N^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{L2} = T_{L1} \cdot \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = T_{L1} \cdot \left(\frac{1 - s_2}{1 - s_1}\right)^2 = T_{L1} \cdot \left(\frac{1 - s_2}{0.97}\right)^2$$

**Step 7:** At new operating point, $T_{e2} = T_{L2}$ (steady-state). Motor torque at slip $s_2$:

$$T_{e2} = \frac{2 \times 0.36 \, T_{max,rated}}{\dfrac{s_2}{0.1} + \dfrac{0.1}{s_2}} = \frac{0.72 \, T_{max,rated}}{10 s_2 + \frac{0.1}{s_2}}$$

Setting $T_{e2} = T_{L2}$ and using $T_{L1} = 0.5504 \, T_{max,rated}$:

$$0.5504 \cdot T_{max,rated} \cdot \left(\frac{1-s_2}{0.97}\right)^2 = \frac{0.72 \, T_{max,rated}}{10 s_2 + \frac{0.1}{s_2}}$$

Cancel $T_{max,rated}$:

$$0.5504 \cdot \frac{(1-s_2)^2}{0.9409} = \frac{0.72}{10 s_2 + 0.1/s_2}$$

$$0.5849 \cdot (1-s_2)^2 = \frac{0.72}{10 s_2 + 0.1/s_2}$$

$$(10 s_2 + 0.1/s_2) \cdot 0.5849 \cdot (1-s_2)^2 = 0.72$$

Try $s_2 = 0.06$:

LHS = $(0.6 + 1.667) \times 0.5849 \times (0.94)^2 = 2.267 \times 0.5849 \times 0.8836 = 1.1716$

RHS = 0.72 → LHS > RHS, so $s_2$ should be **larger** (motor torque at $s_2 = 0.06$ exceeds load torque, rotor accelerates, slip decreases — wait, the opposite is true, so retry).

Reconsider: at $s_2 = 0.06$, the equation gives LHS = 1.17 > RHS = 0.72, meaning the equality is not satisfied with the assumed form. The LHS is too large, indicating the assumed slip is too high (as $s$ increases, $10s + 0.1/s$ initially increases from $s \approx 0.1$ minimum). We need $s_2$ such that the LHS drops to 0.72.

Try $s_2 = 0.04$:

LHS = $(0.4 + 2.5) \times 0.5849 \times (0.96)^2 = 2.9 \times 0.5849 \times 0.9216 = 1.564$

Still too high.

Try $s_2 = 0.05$:

LHS = $(0.5 + 2.0) \times 0.5849 \times (0.95)^2 = 2.5 \times 0.5849 \times 0.9025 = 1.320$

Try $s_2 = 0.08$:

LHS = $(0.8 + 1.25) \times 0.5849 \times (0.92)^2 = 2.05 \times 0.5849 \times 0.8464 = 1.015$

Try $s_2 = 0.10$:

LHS = $(1.0 + 1.0) \times 0.5849 \times (0.90)^2 = 2.0 \times 0.5849 \times 0.81 = 0.948$

Try $s_2 = 0.12$:

LHS = $(1.2 + 0.833) \times 0.5849 \times (0.88)^2 = 2.033 \times 0.5849 \times 0.7744 = 0.921$

Hmm, the LHS is approaching a minimum around $s_2 = s_m = 0.1$, then rising. But our target is 0.72, which is below 0.92. The motor **cannot develop sufficient torque** at this reduced voltage to drive the fan load to its required point.

This means at $V_s = 0.6 V_{rated}$, the motor will operate at the **maximum possible slip** where motor torque equals the fan load torque at each speed. Practically, the motor will find the equilibrium near the peak of the curve.

Setting $T_{e2} = T_{L2}$ directly with an explicit quadratic:

Let $u = s_2$. Equation:

$$0.5849(1-u)^2 \left(10u + \frac{0.1}{u}\right) = 0.72$$

Expanding numerically using a computer algebra system or by further iteration:

The solution lies near $u \approx 0.18$ to $0.20$ (in the unstable region of the original curve, but the fan load curve intersects the reduced-voltage curve in a different geometry).

The physically valid solution is the **stable intersection**. Recalculating carefully — the LHS at $s_2 = 0.18$:

LHS = $(1.8 + 0.556) \times 0.5849 \times (0.82)^2 = 2.356 \times 0.5849 \times 0.6724 = 0.926$

Still not reaching 0.72. The conclusion: **at 60% voltage, the motor cannot drive the original fan load** — the stall condition occurs.

For a valid KTU answer, present the methodology clearly and state:

> **Conclusion:** At $V_s = 0.6 V_{rated}$, the maximum available torque $T_{max,new} = 0.36 T_{max,rated}$ is insufficient to meet the original load torque requirement ($0.5504 T_{max,rated}$). The motor will **stall**, and voltage control to this extent is not feasible for this loading condition. The minimum voltage ratio required is $k_{min} = \sqrt{0.5504} = 0.7419$ (i.e., voltage must be ≥ 74.2% of rated).

$$\boxed{k_{min} = 0.742 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{s,min} \approx 0.742 \times V_{rated}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 3** in the torque equation for a 3-phase machine. The factor 3 is mandatory — losing it loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Using line voltage instead of phase voltage** in torque calculations. Always convert: $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$ for star connection.
> 3. **Confusing $s_m$ with operating slip $s$** — they are different quantities. $s_m$ is a parameter of the machine; $s$ is the actual operating point.
> 4. **Forgetting that $s_m$ is independent of $V_s$** — this is the most heavily tested relationship.
> 5. **Ignoring load torque variation** in constant-torque vs. quadratic-load scenarios — misleads the final answer.
> 6. **Not specifying stable vs. unstable region** when solving Kloss-equation quadratics — always select the lower root for $s < s_m$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [✓] **Synchronous speed $N_s$ depends only on $f$ and $P$** — voltage control does *not* change $N_s$.
- [✓] **Maximum torque $T_{max} \propto V_s^2$** — quadratic dependence on stator voltage.
- [✓] **Critical slip $s_m = R_r'/(X_s + X_r')$** — *independent* of $V_s$.
- [✓] Voltage control is a **scalar**, low-performance method — ideal for **quadratic loads** (fans, pumps, blowers).
- [✓] Three practical implementations: **autotransformer, AC voltage controller (thyristor), series reactor**.
- [✓] **Slip range is narrow** in voltage control because $T_{max}$ reduces drastically with voltage reduction.
- [✓] **Rotor copper loss = $s \cdot P_{gap}$** — higher slip = more rotor losses, reducing efficiency.
- [✓] **Starting torque also varies as $V_s^2$** — voltage control provides soft-start capability.
- [✓] The stable operating region is $0 < s < s_m$; instability sets in beyond $s_m$.
- [✓] **Kloss equation** $T_e/T_{max} = 2/({s/s_m + s_m/s})$ is the workhorse formula for KTU numericals.
- [✓] V/f control (VFD) is a *separate* technique — do **not** confuse with plain voltage control.
- [✓] Practical use cases: ceiling fans, small pumps, conveyor light-load starting, traction auxiliaries.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage/Frequency Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Voltage/Frequency Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Voltage/Frequency Control (V/f Control)**, also widely referred to as **VVVF Control (Variable Voltage Variable Frequency)** or **Constant Volts per Hertz Control**, is the most prevalent scalar speed-control strategy employed for three-phase AC induction motors (squirrel-cage and wound-rotor) in modern industrial variable-frequency drives (VFDs). The fundamental operating principle dictates that the **stator supply voltage and frequency are varied simultaneously in a fixed proportional ratio** so as to maintain the **air-gap flux (φ) at its rated value** across the entire sub-synchronous operating speed range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** V/f control is examined under the broad unit *"Speed Control of AC Drives"* and is often combined with closed-loop vector-control comparisons. Examiners typically test (a) the *reason for simultaneous variation*, (b) the *shape of the V/f profile*, and (c) the *torque-speed envelope* in the constant-torque, constant-power, and field-weakening regions.

### 1.2 The Governing Induced EMF Equation

For any AC machine, the per-phase RMS value of the air-gap induced EMF is given by the well-known transformer EMF equation:

$$E_{1} = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi \, k_{w}$$

Where:
- $E_{1}$ = RMS stator induced EMF per phase (V)
- $f$ = supply frequency (Hz)
- $N$ = number of turns per phase
- $\phi$ = air-gap flux per pole (Wb)
- $k_{w}$ = winding factor (typically 0.95 to 0.98)

Since stator terminal voltage $V_{1} \approx E_{1}$ (the small stator impedance drop is neglected at steady state for the conceptual analysis), we obtain:

$$\phi \; \propto \; \frac{V_{1}}{f}$$

This proportional relationship is the *mathematical heart* of V/f control.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bicycle Gear Metaphor"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Why V and f Must Change Together:**
>
> Imagine riding a bicycle. If you want to ride **slowly** on a steep hill, you shift to a **low gear** — your legs move quickly (high frequency) but with low force (low voltage). If you want to ride **fast** on a flat road, you shift to a **high gear** — your legs move slowly (low frequency) but with great force (high voltage). Your "pedaling rate" represents the **frequency (f)**, and your "force per pedal stroke" represents the **voltage (V)**. The product gives your speed.
>
> Now, an induction motor's rotor "magnetisation" (flux) is analogous to the cyclist's **muscle tension** in the legs. If the supply frequency drops (slower pedaling) but the voltage stays the same, the flux swells dangerously high — equivalent to over-tensing the muscle, which causes heating, saturation, and mechanical strain. Conversely, if the frequency rises but voltage is held low, flux collapses — like a limp, powerless leg stroke. **The flux (muscle tension) is happiest only when the V/f ratio is preserved.** That is the entire philosophy of V/f control.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Rated V/f ratio | **3.8 V/Hz** (typical for 415 V, 50 Hz motor) | Reference point for linear profile |
| Air-gap flux $\phi$ | Maintained at **rated value** (≈ 0.5–0.8 Wb) | Saturation limit |
| Boost voltage | **2 % to 10 %** of rated V at low f | Compensates stator IR drop |
| Field-weakening threshold | Typically above **50 Hz** (base frequency) | Constant-power region begins |
| Maximum usable frequency | **2× to 3×** base frequency | Mechanical / inverter limits |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

The operation of V/f control can be decomposed into the following logical stages:

- **Step 1 — Frequency Command:** The desired speed reference $\omega_{r}^{*}$ from the operator (or the outer speed-control loop) is converted into a corresponding synchronous speed demand using $\omega_{s} = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ (where $P$ is the number of poles).
- **Step 2 — Voltage Synthesis:** The reference voltage $V_{1}^{*}$ is generated by the rule $V_{1}^{*} = \left(\dfrac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}}\right) \times f^{*}$, enforcing the constant V/f ratio.
- **Step 3 — PWM Modulation:** A voltage-source inverter (VSI) employing **Sinusoidal Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM)** or **Space Vector Modulation (SVM)** synthesises this reference voltage at the commanded frequency.
- **Step 4 — Boost Compensation:** At very low frequencies (below 3–5 Hz), the stator resistance drop $I_{1} R_{1}$ becomes a significant fraction of $V_{1}$. A small voltage "boost" is added to maintain flux.
- **Step 5 — Field Weakening:** Above rated frequency, $V_{1}$ is clamped at the inverter DC-bus limit. Only $f$ continues to rise, causing flux — and hence torque — to fall.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Maintain Constant Flux?** Because electromagnetic torque is **directly proportional to flux**: $T \propto \phi \cdot I_{2} \cdot \cos \psi_{2}$. If flux drops, torque collapses at the same slip, and the drive loses its load-handling ability. If flux rises above rated, the iron saturates, drawing huge magnetising current and overheating the machine.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{1} = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi \, k_{w}$ | Induced EMF per phase | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $\phi = \dfrac{V_{1}}{4.44 \, f \, N \, k_{w}}$ | Flux expressed in terms of V & f | Weber (Wb) |
| 3 | $\dfrac{V}{f} = \text{constant}$ | **Core V/f control law** | V/Hz ratio preserved |
| 4 | $N_{s} = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | RPM |
| 5 | $s = \dfrac{N_{s} - N_{r}}{N_{s}}$ | Slip (per unit) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_{s}} \cdot \dfrac{s \, E_{1}^{2} \, R_{2}}{R_{2}^{2} + (s \, X_{2})^{2}}$ | **Electromagnetic torque** | Nm (approximate, $X_{2} = 2\pi f L_{2}$) |
| 7 | $s_{m} = \dfrac{R_{2}}{X_{2}} = \dfrac{R_{2}}{2\pi f \, L_{2}}$ | Slip at maximum torque | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{2 \, \omega_{s}} \cdot \dfrac{E_{1}^{2}}{X_{2}} = \dfrac{3}{2 \, \omega_{s}} \cdot \dfrac{E_{1}^{2}}{2\pi f \, L_{2}}$ | Maximum (pull-out) torque | Nm |
| 9 | $T_{max} \propto \dfrac{E_{1}^{2}}{f^{2}}$ but $E_{1}/f$ constant ⟹ $T_{max} \approx \text{const}$ | **Constant torque in V/f region** | Valid for $f \le f_{rated}$ |
| 10 | $P_{out} \approx T \cdot \omega \approx \text{constant}$ for $f > f_{rated}$ | **Constant power region** | Valid for $f > f_{rated}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Tip for Tables:** Inside markdown tables, never use the vertical bar `|` for absolute values or conditionals. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., write $E_{1}\vert_{rated}$ as $E_{1}\,\vert_{\,rated}$ or use words like "evaluated at").

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

V/f control is the **workhorse** of HVAC (heating, ventilation, air-conditioning) systems, conveyor belts, pumps, fans, and centrifugal compressors — applications where the load torque varies as the **square of speed** ($T_{L} \propto \omega^{2}$). The simplicity of the algorithm (no rotor-flux sensors, no Park/Clarke transformations required for the basic scheme) makes it the lowest-cost, most reliable industrial solution. Modern VFDs from ABB, Siemens (Sinamics), Schneider (Altivar), and Delta implement V/f as the **default open-loop mode** before the user enables sensorless or closed-loop vector control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why V/f Must Be Held Constant

**Starting Point:** The per-phase equivalent circuit of an induction motor in steady state, with all quantities referred to the stator.

**Step 1 — Write the stator KVL equation:**

$$V_{1} = I_{1}(R_{1} + jX_{1}) + E_{1}$$

**Step 2 — Neglect the stator impedance drop (valid for the conceptual / no-load / light-load analysis):**

$$V_{1} \approx E_{1}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the transformer EMF equation $E_{1} = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi \, k_{w}$:**

$$V_{1} \approx 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi \, k_{w}$$

**Step 4 — Solve explicitly for the air-gap flux:**

$$\phi \approx \dfrac{V_{1}}{4.44 \, f \, N \, k_{w}}$$

**Step 5 — To keep $\phi$ at its rated design value $\phi_{rated}$:**

$$\phi_{rated} = \dfrac{V_{rated}}{4.44 \, f_{rated} \, N \, k_{w}} = \dfrac{V_{1}}{4.44 \, f \, N \, k_{w}}$$

**Step 6 — Cross-multiply and simplify (the constant $4.44 \, N \, k_{w}$ cancels):**

$$\dfrac{V_{1}}{f} = \dfrac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}} = \text{constant}$$

This is the **canonical V/f control law**. The flux is held constant *iff* this ratio is preserved.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Proof That Maximum Torque Is Constant in the V/f Region

**Step 1 — Express rotor leakage reactance as a function of frequency:**

$$X_{2} = 2 \pi f L_{2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the maximum-torque formula:**

$$T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{2 \, \omega_{s}} \cdot \dfrac{E_{1}^{2}}{X_{2}} = \dfrac{3}{2 \, \omega_{s}} \cdot \dfrac{E_{1}^{2}}{2 \pi f L_{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Since $\omega_{s} = 2 \pi f / P \cdot (\text{mechanical conversion})$, simplify:**

$$T_{max} = \dfrac{3 \, P}{8 \pi^{2} f^{2} L_{2}} \cdot E_{1}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the V/f law: $E_{1} = k \, f$ where $k = V_{rated}/f_{rated}$. Substituting:**

$$T_{max} = \dfrac{3 \, P}{8 \pi^{2} f^{2} L_{2}} \cdot (k \, f)^{2} = \dfrac{3 \, P \, k^{2}}{8 \pi^{2} L_{2}} = \text{constant}$$

**Conclusion:** The pull-out torque is **independent of frequency** in the V/f region. The *entire family* of torque-speed curves has the **same peak torque** but the *peak occurs at different slips* (because $s_{m} = R_{2}/X_{2} = R_{2}/(2 \pi f L_{2})$ shrinks as $f$ rises — the "breakdown knee" migrates rightward).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Constant-Power Region (Field Weakening)

Above rated frequency, the inverter cannot raise the voltage beyond the DC-bus limit (typically $V_{1,max} \approx V_{rated}$). So $V_{1}$ saturates and only $f$ increases.

**Step 1 — With $V_{1}$ clamped:**

$$\phi = \dfrac{V_{1}}{4.44 \, f \, N \, k_{w}} \propto \dfrac{1}{f}$$

**Step 2 — Therefore flux falls as $1/f$.**

**Step 3 — Torque $T \propto \phi^{2} \cdot s$ in the low-slip linear region. With $\phi \propto 1/f$ and the operating slip forced to be small (saturating the drive at maximum available torque):**

$$T \propto \dfrac{1}{f^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Output power:**

$$P = T \cdot \omega_{m} = T \cdot \left(\dfrac{2 \pi f}{P}\right) \propto \dfrac{1}{f^{2}} \cdot f = \dfrac{1}{f}$$

**Step 5 — In practice, the drive operates along the *constant-power envelope* by electronic control: as $f$ rises, slip is allowed to increase to extract maximum available torque, keeping $T \cdot \omega$ roughly constant until mechanical limits or pull-out occur.** The idealised envelope is therefore:

$$P_{out} \approx \text{constant} \quad \text{for} \quad f > f_{rated}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Operating Regions Summary

| Region | Frequency Range | Voltage Behaviour | Flux $\phi$ | Torque $T$ | Power $P$ | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Low-speed boost** | 0 to 3 Hz | $V$ boosted by $V_{boost} + (V/f)\cdot f$ | ≈ rated (compensated) | ≈ constant (de-rated) | Low | Crawl, position hold |
| **Constant-Torque** | 3 Hz to 50 Hz (rated) | $V$ rises linearly with $f$ | Constant | **Constant** | Rises with $\omega$ | Conveyors, hoists, extruders |
| **Constant-Power** | 50 Hz to ~100 Hz | $V$ clamped at rated | Falls as $1/f$ | Falls as $1/f$ | **Constant** | Machine-tool spindles, EVs |
| **Series / High-speed** | Above 100 Hz | $V$ clamped | Falls rapidly | Falls rapidly | Falls | Limited use |

### 3.5 The V/f Profile (Linear with Boost)

The complete open-loop V/f characteristic, as a function of frequency $f$, is mathematically:

$$V_{1}(f) = \begin{cases} V_{boost} + \left(\dfrac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}}\right) f & 0 \le f \le f_{rated} \\[1em] V_{rated} & f > f_{rated} \end{cases}$$

Where $V_{boost}$ is typically 2 %–10 % of $V_{rated}$ to overcome the stator $I_{1}R_{1}$ drop at very low speeds and preserve the rated torque.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Closed-Loop V/f Control Drive

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph REF ["Reference Inputs"]
        SP["Speed Reference omega_r_star"]
        VR["V/f Profile Generator Block"]
    end
    subgraph CTRL ["Control & Modulation"]
        PWM["SPWM or SVPWM Modulator"]
        INV["3-Phase Voltage Source Inverter VSI"]
        GATE["IGBT Gate Driver Signals"]
    end
    subgraph MACH ["AC Induction Motor"]
        IM["3-Phase Squirrel Cage Induction Motor"]
        SENS["Current Sensors I_abc"]
        SPD["Speed Sensor Encoder Tachogenerator"]
    end
    subgraph FB ["Feedback Loops"]
        CMP["Comparator and PI Controller"]
    end
    SP --> CMP
    SPD --> CMP
    CMP --> VR
    VR -->|V_ref at f_ref| PWM
    SENS --> PWM
    PWM --> GATE
    GATE --> INV
    INV -->|Variable V and f| IM
    IM -->|Torque and Speed| SPD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The speed reference $\omega_{r}^{\star}$ is compared with the measured rotor speed. The error drives a **PI controller**, whose output commands the **V/f profile generator**. The generator outputs a voltage reference at the corresponding frequency. The **PWM modulator** translates this into switching signals for the **3-phase VSI**, which feeds the induction motor. **Current sensors** provide inner-loop current limiting (essential for short-circuit and overload protection).

### 4.2 V/f Profile and Torque-Speed Envelope

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REG1 ["Region 1: Constant Torque V/f Region f from 0 to 50 Hz"]
        R1["V rises linearly with f. Flux constant. T_max constant. Torque-speed curves form a family with same peak."]
    end
    subgraph REG2 ["Region 2: Constant Power Field Weakening f from 50 to 100 Hz"]
        R2["V clamped at rated. Flux falls as 1 slash f. T falls. P held constant. Drive derated in torque."]
    end
    subgraph REG3 ["Region 3: High-Speed Series Region f greater than 100 Hz"]
        R3["V constant. Flux very low. T and P both fall. Used only for brief accelerations."]
    end
    REG1 --> REG2 --> REG3
```

### 4.3 Torque-Speed Curve Family Under V/f Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CURVES ["Family of Torque-Speed Curves at Different Frequencies"]
        C1["Curve at 10 Hz: peak at low s, T_max = rated"]
        C2["Curve at 25 Hz: peak at slightly higher s"]
        C3["Curve at 50 Hz: rated frequency, peak at design s_m"]
        C4["Curve at 75 Hz: field-weakened, peak T reduced"]
        C5["Curve at 100 Hz: severe field weakening"]
    end
    C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C5
    ENVELOPE["Constant T_max envelope in V/f region, falling envelope in field-weakening region"]
    CURVES --> ENVELOPE
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Open-Loop V/f Drive (Most Common Industrial Form)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT ["Input Stage"]
        MAINS["3-Phase AC Mains 50 Hz 415 V"]
        RECT["Uncontrolled Diode Bridge Rectifier"]
        DCBUS["DC Link Capacitor Bank C_dc"]
    end
    subgraph INVERTER ["Inverter Stage"]
        IGBT["6 IGBT VSI with Anti-Parallel Diodes"]
        GATES["Isolated Gate Driver Board"]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL ["Control Stage Digital Signal Processor"]
        VFGEN["V/f Profile Generator LUT"]
        PWMGEN["SPWM Generator Carrier Comparison"]
        PROT["Overcurrent and Overvoltage Protection"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT ["Output Stage"]
        MOTOR["3-Phase Induction Motor"]
        LOAD["Mechanical Load"]
    end
    MAINS --> RECT --> DCBUS --> IGBT --> MOTOR --> LOAD
    GATES --> IGBT
    VFGEN --> PWMGEN --> GATES
    SENS2["DC Link Voltage Sensor and Motor Current Sensors"] --> PROT --> PWMGEN
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Note:** The architecture above corresponds to the classic **AC-DC-AC indirect frequency converter** (rectifier → DC link → inverter). It is the topology used in 90 % of low-voltage industrial VFDs. The V/f law is implemented in a **Look-Up Table (LUT)** inside the DSP/microcontroller, with a piecewise-linear curve (linear up to rated frequency, flat thereafter) plus a low-frequency boost segment.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
*What is meant by V/f control of an induction motor? Why is it necessary to vary voltage proportional to frequency?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

V/f control is a scalar speed-control technique for three-phase induction motors in which the **stator supply voltage $V_{1}$ and frequency $f$ are varied simultaneously in a fixed ratio** so that $V_{1}/f$ remains constant at its rated value. **(1 mark)**

It is necessary because the air-gap flux $\phi = V_{1}/(4.44 \, f \, N \, k_{w})$. If only frequency is reduced while voltage is held constant, the flux rises above rated, causing **magnetic saturation, excessive magnetising current, and overheating**. Conversely, if voltage alone is reduced, flux collapses and torque $T \propto \phi I_{2} \cos \psi_{2}$ falls drastically. **(2 marks)**

By keeping $V_{1}/f$ constant, the air-gap flux — and therefore the maximum torque capability — is preserved across the operating speed range. **(extra depth, awarded for completeness)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*State any three advantages and one limitation of V/f control of induction motors.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages:** **(any 3 × 1 mark each)**
- Simple control algorithm — no need for rotor-flux estimation or coordinate transformations.
- Low hardware cost — open-loop operation is possible with just a VSI and a DSP-based modulator.
- Good speed regulation in the constant-torque region, suitable for fans, pumps, conveyors.

**Limitation:** **(1 mark)**
- Slow dynamic response (no direct flux/torque control); not suitable for high-performance servo applications requiring sub-millisecond torque updates.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Sample Paper 1, Modified]**

**Q. (a)** Derive the relationship between voltage, frequency, and air-gap flux in an induction motor, and hence obtain the V/f control law. Explain with a neat sketch the linear V/f profile used in practice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An induction motor rated at **415 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole, 1440 RPM** is to be operated at **25 Hz** using V/f control. Compute (i) the required line-to-line voltage, (ii) the synchronous speed at 25 Hz, (iii) the rotor speed at 4 % slip, and (iv) the new maximum torque as a percentage of rated (assume $L_{2}$ is unchanged and $R_{2} \ll X_{2}$ at 50 Hz). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation — 5 marks, Sketch — 2 marks**

*Derivation:*

Starting from the per-phase equivalent circuit and neglecting stator impedance drop:

$$V_{1} \approx E_{1} = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi \, k_{w}$$

Solving for flux:

$$\phi = \dfrac{V_{1}}{4.44 \, f \, N \, k_{w}}$$

Since $N$ and $k_{w}$ are fixed by machine construction, $\phi$ depends only on the **ratio $V_{1}/f$**. To keep $\phi$ at its rated value $\phi_{rated}$ for all operating frequencies, we must have:

$$\dfrac{V_{1}}{f} = \dfrac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}} = \text{constant}$$

This is the **V/f control law**. In practice, the profile is piecewise:

$$V_{1}(f) = \begin{cases} V_{boost} + \left(\dfrac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}}\right) f & 0 \le f \le f_{rated} \\[1em] V_{rated} & f > f_{rated} \end{cases}$$

*Sketch:*

```
V (V)
  ^
  |              ___________________________
  |             /
  |            /  (constant V/f slope = V_rated/f_rated)
  |           /
  |          /
  |         /
  |        /
  |       /
  |      /  (low-frequency boost region)
  |     /
  |____/__________________________________> f (Hz)
       0    3    10   25   50   100
```

The V/f profile consists of three segments: a **low-frequency boost** segment (with offset $V_{boost}$), a **linear V/f region** (slope $= V_{rated}/f_{rated}$), and a **constant-voltage field-weakening region** beyond $f_{rated}$.

**(b) Numerical Problem — 7 marks**

**Given:** $V_{LL,rated} = 415$ V, $f_{rated} = 50$ Hz, $P = 4$ poles, $N_{r,rated} = 1440$ RPM, target $f = 25$ Hz, slip $s = 0.04$.

**(i) Required line-to-line voltage at 25 Hz — 1.5 marks:**

Per-phase rated voltage: $V_{ph,rated} = 415 / \sqrt{3} = 239.6$ V.

Per-phase V/f ratio at rated point: $239.6 / 50 = 4.792$ V/Hz.

At 25 Hz: $V_{ph} = 4.792 \times 25 = 119.8$ V (per phase).

Line-to-line: $V_{LL} = 119.8 \times \sqrt{3} = 207.5$ V ≈ **208 V**.

**(ii) Synchronous speed at 25 Hz — 1.5 marks:**

$$N_{s} = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P} = \dfrac{120 \times 25}{4} = 750 \text{ RPM}$$

**(iii) Rotor speed at 4 % slip — 1.5 marks:**

$$N_{r} = N_{s}(1 - s) = 750 \times (1 - 0.04) = 750 \times 0.96 = 720 \text{ RPM}$$

**(iv) Maximum torque as % of rated — 2.5 marks:**

Since $V_{1}/f$ is preserved and $T_{max} \propto E_{1}^{2}/X_{2}$ with $X_{2} \propto f$ and $E_{1} \propto f$, the analysis from Section 3.2 showed that $T_{max}$ is **constant in the V/f region**. Therefore:

$$\dfrac{T_{max}(25 \, Hz)}{T_{max}(50 \, Hz)} \times 100 = \mathbf{100 \, \%}$$

The drive can deliver the same peak torque at half speed — a key advantage of V/f control.

---

**Question B (Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Retest]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the constant-torque, constant-power, and field-weakening regions in a V/f-controlled induction-motor drive, with the help of torque-speed and V/f profiles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare V/f control with direct torque control (DTC) under the following heads: (i) control variable, (ii) dynamic response, (iii) complexity, (iv) parameter sensitivity, (v) typical applications, (vi) torque ripple, (vii) switching frequency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Operating Regions — 7 marks**

An induction-motor drive under V/f control exhibits three distinct operating zones:

**Zone 1 — Constant-Torque Region (0 ≤ f ≤ f_rated):**

- Voltage rises linearly with frequency: $V_{1}/f$ = constant.
- Air-gap flux $\phi$ = rated.
- $T_{max} \approx$ constant (proved in Section 3.2).
- The torque-speed curve family has the same peak torque, with the breakdown slip $s_{m} = R_{2}/X_{2} = R_{2}/(2\pi f L_{2})$ shifting rightward (toward lower slip) as $f$ rises.
- Used for conveyors, hoists, traction at low speeds.

**Zone 2 — Constant-Power Region (f_rated ≤ f ≤ 2 f_rated, typical):**

- Voltage clamped at $V_{rated}$ (limited by DC bus).
- Flux $\phi \propto 1/f$ — **field-weakening**.
- Torque falls approximately as $1/f$.
- Power $P = T \cdot \omega \approx$ constant.
- Used for spindle drives, electric vehicles at cruising speed.

**Zone 3 — High-Speed Series Region (f > 2 f_rated):**

- Voltage and flux both low; torque falls rapidly.
- Power falls; drive is severely derated.
- Used only for brief accelerations or constant-power fan loads.

**Zone 4 — Low-Frequency Boost (0 ≤ f ≤ 3 Hz):**

- A voltage boost $V_{boost}$ is added to overcome stator $I_{1}R_{1}$ drop and preserve rated flux.
- Without boost, the drive would lose torque at very low speeds.

**Sketch of the Complete Torque-Speed Envelope:**

```
T (Nm)
 ^
 |     * rated T_max envelope
 |    /|\
 |   / | \
 |  /  |  \____ constant-power envelope
 | /   |       \
 |/    |        \
 |_____|_________\________________> N (RPM)
      N_s,rated  2*N_s,rated
```

**(b) V/f vs DTC Comparison Table — 7 marks (1 mark per correct row)**

| Head | V/f Control | Direct Torque Control (DTC) |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Control variable | Stator voltage magnitude and frequency | Stator flux and torque (direct, decoupled) |
| (ii) Dynamic response | Slow (10–50 ms) | Very fast (1–5 ms) |
| (iii) Complexity | Simple; scalar algorithm | Complex; requires flux/torque estimators and hysteresis controllers |
| (iv) Parameter sensitivity | Low–moderate; mainly $R_{1}$ at low f | High; $R_{s}$ drift affects flux estimation |
| (v) Typical applications | Pumps, fans, conveyors, HVAC | Servos, robotics, high-performance machine tools |
| (vi) Torque ripple | Low (smooth PWM) | Higher (hysteresis-band switching) |
| (vii) Switching frequency | Fixed (carrier-based PWM) | Variable (hysteresis-driven) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the boost segment** in the V/f profile — this is a very common omission. Always draw the low-frequency region separately.
> 2. **Confusing $V_{rated}$ with line-to-line vs phase voltage** in numerical problems. Recall that the V/f ratio uses **per-phase RMS** values; convert $V_{LL}$ to $V_{ph}$ by dividing by $\sqrt{3}$.
> 3. **Forgetting units** — the V/f ratio's units are V/Hz, not V or Hz alone. State it explicitly.
> 4. **Mixing up synchronous and rotor speed** in numerical problems. Synchronous speed corresponds to the *supply* frequency, while rotor speed involves slip.
> 5. **Stating only the formula $V/f =$ constant** without the derivation or physical justification. KTU examiners want both the *why* and the *how*.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Law:** $V_{1}/f = \text{constant}$ in the sub-synchronous region to maintain rated air-gap flux.
- **Origin:** Derived from the transformer EMF equation $E_{1} = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi \, k_{w}$.
- **Boost Segment:** A small voltage offset is mandatory below ~3 Hz to compensate the stator $I_{1}R_{1}$ drop.
- **Field Weakening:** Above $f_{rated}$, voltage is clamped; flux falls as $1/f$, torque as $1/f$, power remains constant up to the mechanical limit.
- **Torque Capability:** $T_{max}$ is **constant** in the V/f region (proved by substituting $E_{1} \propto f$ and $X_{2} \propto f$ into the $T_{max}$ equation).
- **Slip at Breakdown:** $s_{m} = R_{2}/(2\pi f L_{2})$ — **decreases** with frequency, so the torque-speed knee migrates rightward in the curve family.
- **Hardware:** Realised via a **3-phase VSI with SPWM or SVPWM**; no rotor-position sensor required in open-loop V/f mode.
- **Stator impedance drop** is the principal reason V/f control is *open-loop approximate*; for high-performance drives, **field-oriented control (FOC)** is preferred.
- **Pole count matters** — synchronous speed $N_{s} = 120 f / P$ is the first check before any numerical problem.
- **Three regions to memorise:** Constant-torque (below rated f), constant-power (above rated f), low-speed boost (below 3 Hz).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slip Power Recovery Scheme - Using Inverters and AC Voltage Regulators - Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Slip Power Recovery Scheme - Using Inverters and AC Voltage Regulators - Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Slip Power Recovery Scheme (SPRS) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> The **Slip Power Recovery Scheme (SPRS)** is a variable-speed control strategy employed in **wound-rotor (slip-ring) induction motors** in which the **slip-frequency power** circulating in the rotor circuit is not dissipated as heat (as in conventional rotor-resistance control), but is instead **rectified, processed through a static power converter, and either fed back to the AC supply mains or usefully utilized in the drive system**.

The slip power is mathematically expressed as:

$$
P_{slip} = s \cdot P_g
$$

where:
- $s$ = per-unit slip of the induction motor
- $P_g$ = air-gap power transferred across the air gap from stator to rotor

By recovering this $s \cdot P_g$ instead of burning it in external resistors, the **overall drive efficiency is dramatically improved**, especially at low speeds where the slip (and therefore slip power) is high.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why wound-rotor (slip-ring) motor?**
> A squirrel-cage rotor has no external access to the rotor circuit, so slip power cannot be tapped. Hence SPRS is exclusively applicable to **slip-ring induction motors** — these have brushes and slip rings that allow direct electrical access to the rotor winding terminals.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hydro-Dam Analogy"

Imagine a water reservoir at the top of a hill and a turbine at the bottom:

| Scenario | Induction Motor Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Water flowing down the hill loses energy as heat through friction in pipes | Conventional rotor-resistance control — slip power is wasted as $I^2R$ heat |
| A small generator is placed mid-way in the pipeline to **recapture the kinetic energy** of descending water | **Slip Power Recovery Scheme** — the energy in the rotor circuit is converted back to supply-frequency AC and pumped into the mains |
| A valve at the top controls how much water descends → controls turbine speed | Inverter firing angle $\alpha$ controls DC link voltage → controls motor speed |

In simple words: **instead of wasting the slip energy in a resistor "dump", we recycle it back to the power supply like regenerative braking in an electric vehicle.**

## 1.3 Two Principal Sub-Topics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Implementation Routes covered in this KTU Module:**
>
> 1. **Using Power Electronic Inverters** — *Static Kramer Drive* and *Static Scherbius Drive*
> 2. **Using AC Voltage Regulators (AC-AC Controllers)** — primarily for **soft-starting and limited speed control** of cage/slip-ring motors

The combination of these two techniques under one chapter reflects the KTU 2024 emphasis on **comparative power-electronic drive topologies** for induction motors.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard quantities are used throughout the analysis:

- **Supply frequency:** $f_s = 50\,\text{Hz}$ (India, KTU-affiliated institutions)
- **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = 120f_s/P$ where $P$ is the number of poles
- **Slip:** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$
- **Standstill rotor line-to-line voltage:** $V_{2L}$ (also called $V_{ro}$) — typically **415 V to 1100 V** range for industrial slip-ring motors
- **Stator-to-rotor turns ratio:** $a = N_s/N_r$
- **Inverter firing-angle range:** $30° \le \alpha \le 90°$ (for full-speed control range)
- **Converter output voltage form factor:** $K = 3\sqrt{6}/\pi \approx 2.34$ (three-phase bridge)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Power-Flow Anatomy of an Induction Motor (Pre-requisite)

For any induction motor, the input electrical power splits as follows:

$$
P_{in} = P_{stator\,loss} + P_g
$$

where the **air-gap power** $P_g$ further divides into **rotor copper loss** and **mechanical power developed**:

$$
P_g = P_{rotor\,Cu} + P_{mech}
$$

Since rotor resistance referred to slip is $R_2/s$, the rotor copper loss is $s$ times the air-gap power:

$$
P_{rotor\,Cu} = s \cdot P_g
$$

$$
P_{mech} = (1-s) \cdot P_g
$$

The **slip power** (the power circulating at slip frequency in the rotor circuit) equals the rotor copper loss:

$$
P_{slip} = s \cdot P_g = T_e \cdot \omega_{slip} = T_e \cdot (s\omega_s)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Big Idea:** In a slip-ring motor running at, say, $s = 0.3$ (70% speed), **30% of the air-gap power** is potentially available in the rotor circuit for recovery. For a 500 kW motor, this is **150 kW** of energy that can be recycled — a major economic and thermal advantage.

## 2.2 Static Kramer Drive (Diode Rectifier + Line-Commutated Inverter)

This is the **most industrially important** SPRS topology in the KTU syllabus.

### Topology Description
1. **Rotor winding** (3-phase, slip frequency $sf$) feeds a **3-phase diode bridge rectifier**
2. The **DC link** connects the rectifier to a **3-phase line-commutated thyristor inverter**
3. The inverter is connected to the **AC supply mains** (50 Hz, fixed voltage/frequency)

### Operating Principle — Step-by-Step
- The diode rectifier converts the **slip-frequency AC** at the rotor terminals into a **smooth DC voltage** $V_d$ in the DC link
- The line-commutated inverter converts this DC back into **supply-frequency AC** and pumps it into the mains
- The **DC link current** $I_d$ is the same through both converters (they are in series, DC side)
- **Speed control** is achieved by varying the inverter firing angle $\alpha$, which changes the back-EMF $V_i$ presented to the DC link, thereby altering the rotor effective resistance

### Direction of Power Flow
- Power flows: **Rotor → Rectifier → DC Link → Inverter → Supply mains**
- The slip energy is **regenerated** to the supply

### Limitation
- **Sub-synchronous operation only** ($0 \le N_r < N_s$)
- Because the diode rectifier is unidirectional, power can only flow **out of the rotor**

## 2.3 Static Scherbius Drive (Force-Commutated Inverter or Cycloconverter)

To overcome the unidirectional limitation of the Kramer drive, the diodes are replaced with **thyristors (force-commutated)** in the rotor-side converter. This allows:

- **Sub-synchronous operation** with slip power returned to supply (same as Kramer)
- **Super-synchronous operation** ($N_r > N_s$, $s < 0$) with power fed **into the rotor** from the supply — the motor now runs as a **frequency-changer / power flow controller**

### Two Sub-Topologies
| Sub-type | Rotor-side Converter | Application Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Scherbius with Forced-Commutated Inverter** | Thyristor inverter with commutation capacitors | Sub- and super-synchronous, requires complex commutation |
| **Scherbius with Cycloconverter** | AC-AC cycloconverter directly at rotor | Wide range, large power (>1 MW) drives |

## 2.4 AC Voltage Regulator (AC-AC Phase Controller)

When the topic mentions **AC Voltage Regulators**, the KTU syllabus refers to a **stator-side AC voltage controller** using **back-to-back connected thyristor pairs** (or triacs). This is a **non-recovery** speed control technique used for:

1. **Soft-starting** of squirrel-cage and slip-ring induction motors — limits starting inrush current to **2–3× full-load current** instead of the direct-on-line **6–8×**
2. **Limited-range speed control** below base speed (square-law torque loads only — fans, pumps)

> [!NOTE]
> **AC Voltage Regulator vs SPRS:**
> - SPRS recovers slip energy → high efficiency
> - AC Voltage Regulator **dissipates the controlled energy** (or just limits voltage) → simple and cheap, but **less efficient**. It is the *soft-starter* and *economy-grade speed controller* of the drive world.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Air-gap power | $P_g = T_e \cdot \omega_s$ | Function of torque and synchronous speed |
| 2 | Slip power | $P_{slip} = s \cdot P_g$ | Power available in rotor circuit |
| 3 | Mechanical power | $P_{mech} = (1-s) \cdot P_g$ | Useful output developed |
| 4 | Rectifier DC output voltage | $V_d = (3\sqrt{6}/\pi) \cdot s \cdot V_{ro}$ | $V_{ro}$ = standstill rotor phase voltage (RMS) |
| 5 | Inverter DC back-voltage | $V_i = (3\sqrt{6}/\pi) \cdot V_{s,ph} \cdot \cos\alpha$ | $V_{s,ph}$ = supply phase voltage (RMS) |
| 6 | **Kramer speed equation** | $s = \dfrac{V_{s,ph}}{V_{ro}} \cdot \cos\alpha$ | Core result — KTU frequently asked |
| 7 | Maximum achievable slip (theoretical) | $s_{max} = V_{s,ph} / V_{ro}$ | When $\alpha = 0°$ (inverter fully on) |
| 8 | DC link current | $I_d = (T_e \cdot s) / V_d$ | Slip-power-current relation |
| 9 | Power recovered to supply | $P_{rec} = V_i \cdot I_d$ | Approximate DC-side power balance |
| 10 | AC voltage regulator RMS output | $V_{o,rms} = V_s \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{\alpha}{\pi} + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$ | Resistive load, $\alpha$ in radians |
| 11 | Stator voltage-controller speed ratio | $N_r \propto V_s$ (constant-frequency) | Below base speed, square-law loads |
| 12 | Drive efficiency (Kramer) | $\eta \approx \dfrac{(1-s) P_g}{P_{in}}$ | Neglecting stator + converter losses |
| 13 | Power factor at supply | $pf_{supply} \approx \cos\alpha$ | Lagging, line-commutated inverter |
| 14 | Range of firing angle | $30° \le \alpha \le 90°$ | Standard Kramer drive operating range |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Energy savings**: In a 1000 kW fan running at 60% speed for 5000 hours/year, SPRS saves lakhs of rupees annually versus damper/vane control
- **Soft starting**: AC voltage regulators protect motor windings, belts, and gears from mechanical shock during direct-on-line starting
- **Industry adoption**: SPRS is the **workhorse of large industrial drives** in cement, steel, mining, paper, water-pumping, and HVAC industries

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Speed Equation of Static Kramer Drive (Core KTU Derivation)

### Given
A 3-phase, $P$-pole, slip-ring induction motor with:
- Stator supply: line-to-line RMS voltage $V_{1L}$, frequency $f_s = 50\,\text{Hz}$
- Standstill rotor line-to-line RMS voltage: $V_{2L}$
- Turns ratio: $a = N_s/N_r$, so $V_{ro,ph} = V_{1L,ph}/a$ per rotor phase at standstill
- Slip-ring motor running at slip $s$ with rotor frequency $sf_s$

### To Derive
The slip $s$ as a function of the inverter firing angle $\alpha$.

### Step 1: Rotor voltage at running slip
The rotor phase voltage at standstill is $V_{ro,ph}$. At slip $s$, the rotor induced EMF (line-to-line) becomes:

$$
V_{2L,ph}(s) = s \cdot V_{ro,ph}
$$

**Logic:** The rotor frequency is $sf_s$ and rotor EMF is proportional to frequency, hence EMF scales linearly with slip.

### Step 2: DC output voltage of the 3-phase diode bridge rectifier
For a 3-phase full-bridge diode rectifier, the average DC output voltage is:

$$
V_d = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot V_{2L}(s) = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot (s \cdot V_{ro,LL})
$$

**Logic:** $V_{2L}(s) = s \cdot V_{ro,LL}$ where $V_{ro,LL}$ is the line-to-line rotor voltage at standstill. The factor $3\sqrt{2}/\pi$ is the **standard 3-phase bridge rectification constant** (≈ 1.35).

### Step 3: DC back-EMF of the line-commutated thyristor inverter
For a 3-phase thyristor inverter operating at firing angle $\alpha$, the average DC voltage (acting as a back-EMF against the rectifier) is:

$$
V_i = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot V_{1L} \cdot \cos\alpha
$$

**Logic:** When $\alpha < 90°$, the inverter delivers power from DC to AC side; the polarity of $V_i$ opposes $V_d$ in the DC link. The factor $3\sqrt{2}/\pi$ is again the bridge conversion constant, and $\cos\alpha$ is the standard inverter control characteristic.

### Step 4: DC link steady-state condition
In steady state, ignoring the small DC link resistance, the rectifier and inverter voltages balance:

$$
V_d = V_i
$$

Substituting Step 2 and Step 3:

$$
\frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot s \cdot V_{ro,LL} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot V_{1L} \cdot \cos\alpha
$$

### Step 5: Cancel the common factor $3\sqrt{2}/\pi$

$$
s \cdot V_{ro,LL} = V_{1L} \cdot \cos\alpha
$$

### Step 6: Solve for slip $s$ (the celebrated KTU equation)

$$
\boxed{\; s \;=\; \frac{V_{1L}}{V_{ro,LL}} \cdot \cos\alpha \;}
$$

**Logic Check:** When $\alpha = 0°$, $s$ is maximum, motor runs at **lowest speed**. When $\alpha = 90°$, $\cos\alpha = 0$, $s = 0$, motor runs at **synchronous speed**. This matches physical intuition — increasing $\alpha$ reduces the effective rotor resistance seen by the rotating field, raising the speed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power-Recovery Relation in Kramer Drive

### Given
Same Kramer drive with developed torque $T_e$, slip $s$, and DC link current $I_d$.

### Step 1: Air-gap power in terms of torque
$$
P_g = T_e \cdot \omega_s
$$

**Logic:** Torque × synchronous angular speed gives the total power crossing the air gap from stator to rotor.

### Step 2: Slip power extracted
$$
P_{slip} = s \cdot P_g = s \cdot T_e \cdot \omega_s
$$

**Logic:** Fraction $s$ of the air-gap power is at slip frequency and is therefore tapped at the rotor.

### Step 3: DC link current from slip power and DC voltage
The DC link carries the slip power at DC voltage $V_d$:

$$
I_d = \frac{P_{slip}}{V_d} = \frac{s \cdot T_e \cdot \omega_s}{V_d}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute the expression for $V_d$ from earlier
$$
V_d = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot s \cdot V_{ro,LL}
$$

Therefore:

$$
I_d = \frac{s \cdot T_e \cdot \omega_s}{\frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot s \cdot V_{ro,LL}} = \frac{T_e \cdot \omega_s \cdot \pi}{3\sqrt{2} \cdot V_{ro,LL}}
$$

### Step 5: Final simplified expression
$$
\boxed{\; I_d \;=\; \frac{\pi \, T_e \, \omega_s}{3\sqrt{2} \, V_{ro,LL}} \;}
$$

**Crucial Insight:** The DC link current is **independent of slip** — it depends only on torque. This is a very elegant result of the Kramer drive and is often asked in KTU examinations.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Efficiency Comparison — With vs Without SPRS

### Scenario A: Conventional Rotor-Resistance Control (no recovery)
Rotor copper loss = $s \cdot P_g$ is dissipated in external resistor.

$$
\eta_A = \frac{P_{mech} - P_{mech\,loss}}{P_{in}} = \frac{(1-s) P_g - P_{rot,mech}}{P_{in}}
$$

At low speed ($s$ large), the rotor copper loss becomes huge → poor efficiency.

### Scenario B: Static Kramer Drive (slip power recovered)
Rotor copper loss effectively zero (slip power returned to mains).

$$
P_{in} = P_{g} - s \cdot P_g = (1-s) P_g \quad \text{(net input from mains after recovery)}
$$

$$
\eta_B = \frac{(1-s) P_g - P_{mech\,loss}}{(1-s) P_g + P_{stator\,loss}}
$$

### Numerical Example (KTU-style)
A 500 kW induction motor runs at $s = 0.25$ ($N_r = 0.75 N_s$). Assume $P_{stator} = 15\,\text{kW}$, $P_{mech} = 8\,\text{kW}$.

- **Scenario A:** $P_{slip} = 0.25 \times P_g \approx 0.25 \times 500 = 125\,\text{kW}$ dissipated as heat
  - $\eta_A = (1 - 0.25)(500) - 8 \;\big/\; 500 + 15 + 125 = 367/640 \approx 57.3\%$

- **Scenario B:** Slip power recovered
  - $\eta_B = 367 \;\big/\; 500 - 125 + 15 = 367/390 \approx 94.1\%$

**Improvement: ~37 percentage points** — a massive practical advantage of SPRS for high-power drives.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Summary of Drive Topologies

| Parameter | Conventional (Rotor Resistance) | Static Kramer | Static Scherbius | AC Voltage Regulator |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Speed range | Below $N_s$ | Below $N_s$ | **Below and Above $N_s$** | Below $N_s$ |
| Power recovery | No (waste heat) | Yes → supply | Yes → supply / from supply | No (stator-side) |
| Converter count | None | 2 (rectifier + inverter) | 2 (controlled) | 1 (AC-AC pair) |
| Efficiency at low speed | Poor (large $sP_g$ loss) | **High** | **High** | Moderate |
| Harmonics on supply | None | Yes (6-pulse + 5/7th) | Yes | Yes (sub-harmonics) |
| Supply power factor | Motor PF | $0.7\text{–}0.85$ lagging | Variable | $0.5\text{–}0.9$ lagging |
| Starting current | $6\text{–}8 \times I_{fl}$ | Limited | Limited | **Limited to $2\text{–}3 \times I_{fl}$** |
| Typical power rating | Any | 100 kW – 20 MW | 500 kW – 50 MW | 1 kW – 1 MW |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | High | Low |
| Motor type | Slip-ring | Slip-ring | Slip-ring | Cage **or** slip-ring |
| KTU exam weightage | Low | **Very High** | **High** | **High** |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Static Kramer Drive (Sub-Synchronous SPRS)

The following Mermaid block-diagram represents the **complete power and control path** of a Static Kramer Drive. It maps every functional unit and its interaction, which is the recommended KTU-2024 way of presenting the topology in answer scripts.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Three Phase AC Supply Mains 50 Hz V1L] --> B[Slip Ring Induction Motor Stator]
    B --> C[Rotor winding at slip frequency sfs]
    C --> D[Three Phase Diode Bridge Rectifier]
    D --> E[DC Link Inductor Ld Smoothing]
    E --> F[DC Link Capacitor Cd Filter]
    F --> G[Three Phase Line Commutated Thyristor Inverter Firing Angle alpha]
    G --> H[Step up Transformer Optional]
    H --> A
    I[Speed Reference Nref] --> J[Controller Firing Circuit]
    J --> G
    K[Tachogenerator on Motor Shaft] --> J
```

### Functional Reading of the Block Diagram

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| A | Three-phase AC mains (50 Hz) feeds the stator and receives recovered energy |
| B | Stator of slip-ring induction motor; sets up rotating magnetic field at $\omega_s$ |
| C | Rotor winding at slip frequency $sf_s$; slip rings tap this energy |
| D | Diode bridge converts slip-frequency AC to DC (unidirectional) |
| E, F | LC filter smooths DC link current $I_d$ |
| G | Line-commutated inverter: DC → 50 Hz AC; $\alpha$ controls speed |
| H | Optional transformer to match inverter voltage to mains |
| I → J → G | Closed-loop speed control: reference vs tachogenerator feedback |
| K → J | Actual speed feedback for closed-loop regulation |

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Static Scherbius Drive (Sub + Super-Synchronous)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Supply Mains 50 Hz] --> B[Stator of Slip Ring Motor]
    B --> C[Rotor Winding Slip Frequency sfs]
    C --> D[Three Phase Forced Commutated Thyristor Converter Rotor Side]
    D --> E[DC Link Inductor and Filter]
    E --> F[Three Phase Line Commutated Inverter Supply Side]
    F --> A
    G[Speed Reference and Direction Logic] --> H[Controller]
    H --> D
    H --> F
    I[Tachogenerator] --> H
    J[Mode Selector Subsync or Supersync] --> H
```

### Functional Reading

- **Block D** is now a **bidirectional** force-commutated converter (replaces the diode bridge of Kramer)
- **Block J** selects the operating mode — if motor must run above $N_s$, the rotor-side converter acts as an inverter and pumps power from mains → rotor
- The **DC link current direction** flips when crossing $N_s$, a key KTU exam observation

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: AC Voltage Regulator (Soft-Starter / Limited Speed Controller)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Three Phase AC Supply] --> B[Back to Back Thyristor Pair per phase T1 T2 T3 T4 T5 T6]
    B --> C[Stator of Induction Motor]
    C --> D[Load Fan Pump Compressor]
    E[Ramp Generator Soft Start Reference] --> F[Firing Angle Controller alpha t]
    F --> B
    G[Current Sensor Stator] --> F
    H[Voltage Sensor] --> F
```

### Functional Reading

- Each phase has **two anti-parallel thyristors** (or one triac) — these are the **AC voltage regulators**
- The **firing-angle controller** ramps $\alpha$ from $180°$ (zero voltage) to a small value (full voltage) over a preset time — this is the **soft-start** function
- For limited-range speed control, $\alpha$ is held at a value that gives the desired reduced stator voltage

---

## 4.4 Power-Flow Sankey-Style Diagram: With and Without SPRS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electrical Input Pin] -->|Pg| B[Air Gap]
    B -->|sPg| C1[Rotor Cu Loss Conventional]
    B -->|1-s Pg| D1[Mechanical Power]
    C1 --> E1[Dissipated as Heat]

    A2[Electrical Input Mains] -->|Pg| B2[Air Gap Kramer]
    B2 -->|sPg Recovered| F[Inverter and Transformer]
    F -->|Back to Mains| A2
    B2 -->|1-s Pg| D2[Mechanical Power]
    D2 --> G[Load]
```

### Interpretation
- **Top branch (Conventional):** All $sP_g$ is lost as heat
- **Bottom branch (Kramer SPRS):** The same $sP_g$ is **redirected back to the supply** — the mains input is reduced by exactly this amount, and the heat dissipation in the rotor circuit is eliminated
