# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"What is meant by slip power recovery scheme? Why is it preferred over conventional rotor-resistance control for high-power induction motor drives?"**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
A slip power recovery scheme (SPRS) is a method of speed control used with **wound-rotor (slip-ring) induction motors** in which the **slip-frequency power** present in the rotor circuit ($P_{slip} = sP_g$) is **rectified, processed by a power-electronic converter, and fed back to the AC supply mains** instead of being dissipated as heat in external resistors.

**Advantages over rotor-resistance control (1.5 marks):**
1. The slip power is **recovered and reused** rather than wasted, giving **much higher efficiency** especially at low speeds
2. Continuous and **smooth speed control** is possible from near-zero to synchronous speed by varying the inverter firing angle $\alpha$
3. **No external resistor bank** → no bulky heat-dissipating rheostats, lower cooling requirement
4. For large drives (hundreds of kW to MW range), the energy savings translate to significant economic benefit

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Why cannot a slip power recovery scheme be implemented on a squirrel-cage induction motor?"**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

A squirrel-cage induction motor has its rotor winding **permanently short-circuited inside the rotor body** and the conductor bars are embedded in the laminated iron slots with no external electrical access. There are **no slip rings or brushes** to tap the rotor circuit externally.

Since the SPRS requires **direct electrical access to the rotor circuit** so that the slip-frequency voltage can be rectified and processed, only a **wound-rotor (slip-ring) induction motor** with externally accessible rotor terminals can be used in such a scheme.

Hence, **SPRS is impossible with a squirrel-cage motor** and is exclusively used with **slip-ring induction motors**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a **Static Kramer Drive** and explain its working principle with a suitable power flow diagram. Derive the expression for the **slip of the motor in terms of the inverter firing angle**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, slip-ring induction motor has a **standstill rotor line-to-line voltage of 400 V** and is connected to a **stator supply of 415 V, 50 Hz**. If the motor runs at **1425 rpm** with a Static Kramer drive, calculate the **inverter firing angle $\alpha$** required. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Block Diagram (2 marks):**
Refer to the Static Kramer block diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes. The diagram should clearly show:
- Stator fed from 3-phase mains
- Rotor terminals connected to a 3-phase diode bridge rectifier
- DC link (with inductor/capacitor) connecting rectifier to a line-commutated thyristor inverter
- Inverter output connected back to the AC mains
- Speed feedback loop

**2. Working Principle (3 marks):**
- The 3-phase rotor winding at slip frequency $sf_s$ delivers a slip-frequency AC voltage whose magnitude is proportional to slip: $V_2(s) = s \cdot V_{ro}$
- The **diode bridge** rectifies this to a DC voltage $V_d$ across the DC link
- The **line-commutated thyristor inverter** (firing angle $\alpha$) inverts this DC back to 50 Hz AC and feeds it to the supply
- The DC link current $I_d$ is common to both converters, and the back-EMF $V_i$ of the inverter opposes $V_d$
- In steady state, the speed is governed by $\alpha$ — increasing $\alpha$ reduces the effective rotor resistance, increasing speed
- The slip power $sP_g$ is thus recovered to the mains; the motor input is reduced by this amount

**3. Derivation of Speed Equation (2 marks):**
Refer to the derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes.

$$
\boxed{\; s = \frac{V_{1L}}{V_{ro,LL}} \cdot \cos\alpha \;}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Frequency $f_s = 50\,\text{Hz}$, $P = 4$ poles
- Stator line voltage $V_{1L} = 415\,\text{V}$
- Rotor standstill line-to-line voltage $V_{ro,LL} = 400\,\text{V}$
- Actual speed $N_r = 1425\,\text{rpm}$

**Step 1:** Synchronous speed

$$
N_s = \frac{120 \, f_s}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500\,\text{rpm}
$$

**Step 2:** Slip

$$
s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1425}{1500} = \frac{75}{1500} = 0.05
$$

**Step 3:** From the Kramer speed equation

$$
s = \frac{V_{1L}}{V_{ro,LL}} \cdot \cos\alpha
$$

$$
0.05 = \frac{415}{400} \cdot \cos\alpha
$$

$$
\cos\alpha = \frac{0.05 \times 400}{415} = \frac{20}{415} = 0.04819
$$

**Step 4:** Firing angle

$$
\alpha = \cos^{-1}(0.04819) \approx 87.24°
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Common mistake #1:** Confusing line voltage with phase voltage. The Kramer equation uses **line-to-line** values consistently. If you mix V_ph and V_LL you will lose 2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Common mistake #2:** Computing $N_s$ with the wrong pole formula. Always verify: $N_s = 120f/P$, not $60f/P$ (which is for Hz↔rad/s conversion, NOT for rpm).
> 3. **Common mistake #3:** Not specifying whether $V_{ro}$ is phase or line value. State it explicitly: "rotor standstill **line-to-line** voltage = 400 V".

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Static Scherbius Drive** and explain how it differs from the Static Kramer drive. Why can the Scherbius drive operate in the **super-synchronous region**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of a **3-phase AC Voltage Regulator** used as a **soft-starter** for an induction motor. Why is the starting current limited to 2–3 times full-load current? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Block Diagram (1.5 marks):**
Refer to **Section 4.2**. The Scherbius block diagram has a **bidirectional force-commutated thyristor converter** on the rotor side, in series with a line-commutated inverter on the supply side through a DC link.

**2. Working Principle (2.5 marks):**
- In the **sub-synchronous mode** ($0 < N_r < N_s$, $s > 0$), the rotor-side converter acts as a **rectifier** and the line-side converter as an **inverter** — slip power flows rotor → supply (same as Kramer)
- In the **super-synchronous mode** ($N_r > N_s$, $s < 0$), the **roles reverse** — the rotor-side converter now acts as an **inverter** and pumps power from the mains into the rotor, while the line-side converter acts as a **rectifier**
- The control of firing angles of both converters determines the operating speed and power flow direction

**3. Difference from Kramer Drive (1.5 marks):**

| Feature | Kramer | Scherbius |
|---|---|---|
| Rotor-side converter | Diode bridge (uncontrolled) | Force-commutated thyristor (controlled) |
| Speed range | Sub-sync only | Sub-sync **and** super-sync |
| Power flow direction | One-way (rotor→supply) | Bi-directional |
| Complexity | Simpler | More complex (commutation circuits) |

**4. Super-synchronous operation (1.5 marks):**
Because the rotor-side converter is now **bidirectional**, when the motor speed exceeds synchronous, the rotor induced voltage reverses polarity (slip becomes negative). The controlled converter can handle this polarity reversal, allowing power to be **fed into the rotor** from the supply. The motor thus operates as a **frequency changer** and can run above synchronous speed.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**1. AC Voltage Regulator Topology (2 marks):**
A 3-phase AC voltage regulator uses **two anti-parallel thyristors per phase** (or one triac per phase), connected in series between the AC supply and the stator terminals of the induction motor. The firing angle $\alpha$ of the thyristor pair is varied to control the RMS value of the voltage applied to the stator.

**2. Soft-Starting Action (2.5 marks):**
- At start, $\alpha$ is set close to **180°** → output voltage is nearly **zero** → motor receives negligible voltage → starting current is very small
- A **ramp generator** slowly decreases $\alpha$ from 180° towards a small value (say 30°) over a preset time interval (typically 5–30 seconds)
- The stator voltage gradually increases from zero to full-rated voltage
- The motor accelerates smoothly without the **6–8× inrush** of direct-on-line starting

**3. Starting Current Limited to 2–3× FLC (2.5 marks):**
- At any instant during soft-start, the stator voltage is well below rated value
- Since motor impedance is roughly constant, the current is proportional to applied voltage
- At 30% of rated voltage, the inrush current is limited to about 30% of the DOL inrush, i.e., about **2–3× FLC** instead of 6–8× FLC
- This protects the motor windings, supply transformer, and the driven mechanical system (belts, gears) from thermal and mechanical stress

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Common mistake #1:** Confusing "AC voltage regulator" with "DC chopper". The KTU module clearly uses **AC-AC controllers**, NOT DC-DC choppers. Drawing a chopper circuit will fetch zero marks.
> 2. **Common mistake #2:** Not specifying **anti-parallel** connection. Two SCRs in *series* (not anti-parallel) cannot conduct on the alternate half-cycle.
> 3. **Common mistake #3:** Stating that the AC voltage regulator is used for **full-range speed control**. It is primarily a **soft-starter** and provides only limited-range speed control for **square-law loads** (fans, pumps).

---

## 5.3 Common Applications of Slip Power Recovery and AC Voltage Regulators

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-recognized application domains (frequently asked in 3-mark questions):**

| Application Domain | Drive Topology Used | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Centrifugal pumps (water, oil, chemical)** | SPRS (Kramer) or AC Voltage Regulator | Large power, long running hours, square-law torque → massive energy savings |
| **Forced-draft / induced-draft fans (boilers, power plants)** | SPRS (Kramer) | 100s of kW; need variable speed for combustion control |
| **Compressors (air, refrigeration, gas)** | SPRS or AC Voltage Regulator | Process optimization through speed control |
| **Conveyor belts (mining, cement)** | SPRS | Multiple motor coordination, soft start |
| **Cement mills and crushers** | SPRS (Scherbius for very large) | Very high power, smooth speed control |
| **Wind-energy generation systems** | Static Scherbius | Variable-speed generator, slip power recovered |
| **Pump-storage hydro stations** | Static Scherbius (cycloconverter) | Motoring + generating modes |
| **Large HVAC systems** | AC Voltage Regulator | Soft-start of big motors, energy savings |
| **Rolling mills (steel plants)** | SPRS (Scherbius) | Wide speed range, regenerative braking |
| **Ship propulsion (electric)** | SPRS / Cycloconverter | Very high power (multi-MW) |

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SPRS** is **exclusively for slip-ring (wound-rotor) induction motors** — squirrel-cage cannot be used.
- **Slip power** = $s \cdot P_g$ — this is the power that can be recovered in the rotor circuit.
- **Static Kramer Drive** uses a **diode rectifier + line-commutated inverter** → **sub-synchronous operation only**.
- **Static Scherbius Drive** uses a **bidirectional controlled converter** → can operate **above and below synchronous speed**.
- The **core speed equation** for Kramer drive is $\; s = (V_{1L}/V_{ro,LL}) \cdot \cos\alpha \;$ — memorize and be able to derive.
- **Firing-angle range**: $30° \le \alpha \le 90°$ for normal Kramer operation; $90°$ corresponds to synchronous speed.
- **DC link current** in a Kramer drive is **independent of slip** — it depends only on torque: $I_d = \pi T_e \omega_s / (3\sqrt{2} V_{ro,LL})$.
- **AC Voltage Regulator** uses **back-to-back thyristor pairs** per phase → primarily a **soft-starter**, secondary speed controller for **square-law loads only**.
- **Soft-start** limits starting current to **2–3× FLC** (vs 6–8× for DOL).
- **Supply power factor** of Kramer drive ≈ $\cos\alpha$ — lagging, degrades as $\alpha$ increases.
- **Efficiency improvement** with SPRS can be **20–40 percentage points** higher than rotor-resistance control at low speeds.
- **Applications** to remember: fans, pumps, compressors, cement mills, wind generators, pump-storage hydro.
- **Scherbius can regenerate above synchronous speed** — Kramer cannot.
- **Diode bridge unidirectionality** is the fundamental reason Kramer is sub-sync only.
- **Disadvantage of SPRS**: poor supply power factor, harmonics, and need for slip-ring motor.
- **KTU marks-trap**: always specify whether voltages are **line-to-line (LL) or phase (ph)** — equation changes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 30-Second Memory Trigger:**
> *"Kramer = diode + thyristor inverter → SUB-synchronous, recycle $sP_g$ to mains. Scherbius = thyristor + thyristor → SUB + SUPER-synchronous, power flow can reverse. AC voltage regulator = anti-parallel thyristors per phase → SOFT-START + limited speed control of fans/pumps."*
